proposals.title: Partial Identification of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis with Proxy Variables and Imperfect Simulators
proposals.motivation: The critical 'no unmeasured confounders' assumption is often violated in observational clinical studies, leading to biased causal estimates. Pre-2026 methods for sensitivity analysis or using proxies are often limited to point estimation or simple settings, failing to provide robust, informative bounds for complex time-to-event outcomes with heterogeneous effects, which are central to personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating proxy variables of unobserved confounders with flexible machine learning models and leveraging biased offline data within a partial identification framework, we can derive tighter, clinically meaningful bounds on heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival outcomes, even when the no unmeasured confounders assumption is relaxed.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to incorporate proxy variables, treating the unobserved confounder as a latent variable. Using a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture, we will learn a distribution over the latent confounder from its measured proxies. This distribution will be integrated into a survival model to simulate potential outcomes under different treatment assignments. We will then apply partial identification techniques, inspired by work on biased offline RL benchmarks, to derive bounds on the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) function by systematically varying the assumed strength of residual confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical method for quantifying uncertainty in HTE estimation from observational survival data with unmeasured confounding. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating causal inference methods under realistic, assumption-violating conditions, directly impacting clinical decision-making where absolute certainty is unattainable.
proposals.novelty: The integration of deep latent variable models for proxy confounding with non-parametric survival HTE estimation and a formal partial identification strategy to produce bounded, heterogeneous effects is a novel synthesis beyond existing sensitivity analyses or point-estimate proxy methods.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects with Latent Confounder-Treatment Interactions via Variational Causal Survival Networks
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine but is confounded by unmeasured variables. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's work on survival outcomes with hidden covariates, often assume additive or simple interactions between treatments and latent confounders. This fails to capture complex, non-linear effect modifications, leading to biased HTE estimates in scenarios like critical care or oncology where treatment efficacy may depend intricately on a patient's latent physiological state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variational inference framework, which explicitly models non-linear and non-additive interactions between a treatment variable and the latent representation of confounders, will yield more accurate and robust estimates of individualized treatment effects in time-to-event outcomes compared to methods assuming additive separability.
proposals.method: We will extend the Variational Autoencoder for Causal Inference (VAE-CI) and survival architectures by integrating a structured treatment-latent space interaction network. The model will use a deep kernel or a structured multi-head attention mechanism within the decoder to allow the treatment variable to modulate the generative process of the outcome non-linearly. For survival outcomes, we will adapt the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees framework to handle censored data, using the flexible representations from the interaction-augmented VAE as input to non-parametric effect estimators, enabling the discovery of complex heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for estimating HTEs that is robust to complex, non-additive confounding structures. It will contribute a benchmark for non-linear effect modification in causal survival analysis and deliver open-source software integrating variational inference with modern survival and causal learning toolkits, directly applicable to clinical datasets like electronic health records.
proposals.novelty: While latent variable models for causal inference exist, explicitly parameterizing and learning flexible, non-additive interaction functions between treatment and the latent confounder space is novel. This moves beyond the standard additive assumption in most pre-2026 doubly robust or representation-based methods for HTE estimation.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests with Misclassified Treatment: A Measurement Error Model for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In observational clinical studies, treatment assignment is often recorded with error, such as misclassification in electronic health records or imperfect adherence proxies. Standard causal inference methods, including those for survival outcomes, assume treatment is perfectly observed, leading to biased estimates of Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE). This problem is acute in time-to-event settings where censoring compounds the difficulty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling treatment misclassification within a non-parametric causal forest framework can significantly reduce bias in estimated heterogeneous treatment effects for survival outcomes, compared to methods that ignore measurement error.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to incorporate a measurement error model for the treatment variable. The approach will treat the true treatment as a latent variable, with the observed treatment linked via a known or estimated misclassification matrix (e.g., sensitivity/specificity). We will develop an EM-algorithm variant of causal survival forests, where the E-step imputes probabilistic true treatment assignments, and the M-step grows trees to maximize a survival-splitting criterion (like log-rank) on the imputed data. Inference will use bootstrap aggregation of such trees to estimate the CATE function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first non-parametric, machine learning method for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects under treatment misclassification for censored time-to-event data. It will deliver a practical software tool for clinical researchers handling noisy treatment data and establish a benchmark for this important, understudied problem.
proposals.novelty: While measurement error in covariates is studied, its integration into *treatment* assignment for modern CATE estimation in survival analysis is novel. The fusion of latent variable models with causal forest architectures for robust HTE estimation represents a significant methodological advance beyond pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Hyperparameter Selection for Causal Survival Forests via Cross-Validated Covariate Balance and Model Stability
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine, but methods like causal survival forests require tuning hyperparameters (e.g., tree depth, honesty parameters). Without access to ground truth effects, practitioners lack principled criteria for model selection, often resorting to ad-hoc choices that can bias estimates and reduce reliability in clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a selection criterion combining (a) post-estimation covariate balance across treatment groups within leaf nodes and (b) the stability of estimated effect rankings across cross-validation folds will identify hyperparameters that maximize the generalization performance of HTE estimates, even when true effects are unobserved.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of causal survival forests and recursively imputed trees. For a given hyperparameter set, we will compute a joint score: (1) the standardized mean difference of prognostically important covariates within estimated treatment/control leaves, aggregated across the forest, and (2) the Kendall's W concordance of patient-level effect rankings across k-fold cross-validation. We will implement a search over the hyperparameter space (e.g., using Bayesian optimization) to minimize this joint score, benchmarking against simulated data with known effects and semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts with hidden or partially known treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, validation-based tool for tuning causal machine learning models in survival settings, moving beyond reliance on simulated ground truth. It will directly enhance the reliability of clinical HTE estimation for tasks like treatment effect prediction. The proposed criteria will be integrated into an open-source software package for broader use.
proposals.novelty: While covariate balance and model stability are known concepts, their formal combination into a single, optimized objective for hyperparameter selection in causal survival forests—without any outcome data in the tuning step—is novel. This approach bypasses the need for ground truth effects, which existing model selection methods for causal forests often implicitly require.

proposals.title: Causal EffectVAE: A Variational Framework for Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects with Multiple Treatments and Continuous Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 causal inference for personalized medicine is dominated by methods for binary treatments and binary or survival outcomes. Many real-world clinical decisions involve choosing among multiple treatment options (e.g., different drug types or dosages) and evaluating their effect on continuous measures (e.g., blood pressure, biomarker levels). Existing VAE-based models for causal effect estimation, such as CEVAE, are fundamentally limited to the binary setting, creating a significant methodological gap for these common scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized VAE architecture, which explicitly models the structured latent space for multiple, potentially ordered treatments and continuous potential outcomes, can provide unbiased and accurate estimates of individual-level causal effects in these broader, clinically relevant settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the CEVAE framework by replacing the treatment model with a multinomial or ordinal logistic likelihood for multiple treatments and the outcome model with a Gaussian or flexible density network for continuous outcomes. The core innovation is a structured latent variable model that disentangles confounding factors from the outcome-predictive information, using techniques like the Information Bottleneck, to ensure the latent representation is suitable for counterfactual prediction. Estimation will be performed via amortized variational inference, and we will incorporate targeted regularization to enforce the balancing of latent confounders across treatment groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, deep learning-based tool for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) from observational data across a wider range of clinical decision contexts. It will enable the application of modern causal representation learning to problems like optimal dosage selection and comparative effectiveness research with continuous endpoints, directly supporting precision medicine initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs have been used for binary causal inference, their adaptation to the multiple-treatment, continuous-outcome case is non-trivial and requires novel architectural choices and regularization strategies to maintain identifiability and performance, moving beyond simple multi-task extensions of existing models.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects from High-Dimensional Proxies: A Causal Survival Framework for Medical Images and Clinical Text
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, a major challenge in personalized medicine is leveraging the rich, unstructured data in electronic health records (EHRs), like medical images and clinical notes, to predict how treatments affect time-to-event outcomes (e.g., disease progression, mortality). Existing methods for heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation in survival analysis often rely on structured covariates, discarding valuable signal in high-dimensional proxies that may encode critical confounders or effect modifiers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending Shalit's framework for HTE estimation in time-to-event outcomes to incorporate deep learning representations of images and text will yield more accurate and clinically informative estimates of individualized treatment effects from observational data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework or a similar doubly-robust causal survival estimator. A multi-modal neural network (e.g., a CNN for images and a transformer/BiLSTM for clinical notes) will be trained to learn a shared, low-dimensional representation of the high-dimensional proxy data. This representation will serve as the covariate input for the causal survival model, with careful regularization (e.g., propensity score balancing, representation adversarial learning) to control for confounding. We will benchmark against methods that use only structured data or naive concatenation of deep features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological pipeline for integrating complex, unstructured data into causal survival analysis, directly addressing a key data bottleneck in observational clinical research. It will deliver new software tools and benchmarks for the community, enabling more precise estimation of treatment effect heterogeneity from real-world data sources like EHRs and medical archives.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning for survival prediction exists, its integration into *causal* survival frameworks for HTE estimation using multi-modal proxies is novel. This moves beyond using deep features for prediction alone, explicitly tackling the confounding challenge central to causal inference with unstructured data.

proposals.title: Identifiability of Causal Effects in Latent Confounder Models with Proxies: A Theoretical Analysis for Variational Autoencoder Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Estimating causal effects from observational data is a core challenge in clinical prediction and treatment effect heterogeneity. A prominent pre-2026 approach uses variational autoencoders (VAEs) to infer latent confounders from high-dimensional proxy variables, but the theoretical conditions under which the causal effect is uniquely identifiable (non-parametrically) remain under-specified. Without formal guarantees, these powerful methods risk producing biased estimates, limiting their reliability for clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, under a set of precise structural assumptions linking the treatment, outcome, latent confounder, and observed proxies, the average causal effect is non-parametrically identifiable within a VAE-based model class, even when the latent variable model is only weakly supervised.
proposals.method: We will build on the recent 'causal effect variational autoencoder' (CEVAE) framework and related proxy-based identification theory. The approach will involve: (1) formally specifying the graphical model and the VAE's generative and inference networks; (2) applying do-calculus to derive the target functional; and (3) proving identifiability by establishing that the observed data distribution under the model constraints uniquely pins down this functional, leveraging results on completeness of certain conditional moment restrictions. We will extend the analysis to settings with time-to-event (survival) outcomes subject to censoring, linking to Shalit's prior work on heterogeneous treatment effects with censored data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous identifiability proofs for VAE-based causal effect estimation with proxies, bridging a gap between applied machine learning and causal theory. It will yield practical, testable conditions for researchers to validate their model assumptions, strengthening the foundation for using deep latent variable models in high-stakes clinical and policy settings.
proposals.novelty: While identifiability for linear latent variable models is well-studied, formal proofs for non-parametric, deep generative models like VAEs are scarce. This analysis uniquely combines modern variational inference architectures with classic causal identification theory, specifically addressing the proxy-confounding scenario common in medical records research.

proposals.title: Adversarially Debiased Survival Representations for Causal Effect Estimation from Observational Time-to-Event Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational survival data is critical for personalized medicine but is confounded by selection bias. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees for survival outcomes, often rely on strong modeling assumptions or propensity score models that can be misspecified. A representation learning approach that directly enforces balance could offer more robust estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: Learning patient representations that are predictive of the time-to-event outcome while being invariant to the treatment assignment via adversarial training will yield less biased and more generalizable estimates of conditional average treatment effects (CATE) in survival settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the adversarial domain adaptation framework for counterfactual prediction (e.g., based on the work of Johansson et al. and Shalit et al. pre-2026) to censored survival outcomes. A neural network will learn a representation Φ(X). A treatment predictor (adversary) will try to predict treatment T from Φ(X), while a survival prediction head (e.g., a Cox partial likelihood or deep survival model) will predict the event time from Φ(X) and T. The model will be trained with a gradient reversal layer to maximize the adversary's loss, encouraging Φ(X) to be invariant to T. We will benchmark against established methods like Bayesian Causal Forests and recursively imputed survival trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible deep learning method for estimating survival HTEs that directly mitigates confounding via representation balancing. It will contribute a benchmark for adversarial debiasing in survival analysis and offer a practical tool for clinical researchers analyzing electronic health records or registry data where time-to-event outcomes and confounding are paramount.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing has been explored for binary/continuous outcomes, its tailored application to censored survival data—integrating with modern deep survival models—is novel. This directly combines Shalit's themes of survival analysis, hidden confounders, and causal inference with an adversarial learning framework not yet fully explored for this specific task pre-2026.

proposals.title: Modeling Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis with Flow-Based Latent Variable Models
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is critical for personalized medicine but is challenged by complex, high-dimensional confounders and censoring. Variational autoencoders (VAEs) with Gaussian latent priors are increasingly used to learn representations for causal estimation, but the restrictive Gaussian assumption may fail to capture the true, potentially multi-modal, distribution of latent confounders, leading to biased effect estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the Gaussian latent prior in a VAE-based causal survival model with a flexible normalizing flow will yield a more accurate representation of the latent confounder distribution, leading to improved estimation of individualized treatment effects and survival curves.
proposals.method: We will build upon the 'Recursively Imputed Trees' framework for survival HTE, replacing its representation learning module. The core is a conditional VAE where the encoder outputs parameters for a base Gaussian, which is then transformed by a stack of invertible, neural network-based flow layers (e.g., RealNVP or MAF) to produce a complex latent prior. The decoder will model the time-to-event outcome using a flexible hazard model (e.g., Cox or DeepHit). We will derive a modified evidence lower bound (ELBO) for training and integrate targeted regularization for causal identification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more expressive tool for learning from censored observational data, advancing the methodology for personalized treatment effect estimation in survival analysis. It will offer a principled way to relax a key modeling assumption in causal VAEs, potentially improving the robustness of clinical decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: While normalizing flows are established for density estimation, their integration into the latent space of a VAE specifically for the task of causal survival analysis with heterogeneous effects is novel. This directly addresses a limitation in current VAE-based causal methods used in clinical prediction.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Outcomes with Relational Confounding via Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: In observational clinical and epidemiological studies, units (e.g., patients, clinics) are often interconnected, leading to latent confounding via network or relational structures. Pre-2026 causal inference methods for survival analysis, like Shalit's Recursively Imputed Trees, typically assume independent units, potentially biasing treatment effect estimates when this assumption is violated. Modeling these relational dependencies is crucial for valid inference in settings like healthcare delivery networks or studies of infectious diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the relational structure among units using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) will yield less biased and more accurate estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in time-to-event data compared to methods that ignore such structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel survival GNN architecture that integrates a graph convolutional network (GCN) or Graph Attention Network (GAT) layer to process relational proxy data (e.g., patient referral networks, geographic proximity). This layer will generate unit-specific embeddings that capture latent relational confounding. These embeddings will be used as additional inputs to a survival HTE estimator, such as a modified Recursively Imputed Survival Forest or a Deep Cox Mixture model. We will train the model using a causal objective that balances representation learning for treatment assignment and outcome prediction, adapting pre-2026 frameworks like Dragonnet or CFRNet for censored outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for causal survival analysis with networked data, expanding the toolkit for reliable observational studies in medicine and public health. It will deliver a benchmarked, open-source software implementation and demonstrate its utility on real-world clinical datasets, such as those involving hospital networks or multi-center trials.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for prediction, their integration into *causal* estimation of heterogeneous survival effects, specifically to handle relational confounding, is a novel synthesis of Shalit's previous work on HTE, survival, and causal inference with emerging pre-2026 geometric deep learning techniques.

proposals.title: Contrastive Learning of Latent Proxy Representations for Improved Causal Effect Estimation in Survival Outcomes
proposals.motivation: In observational studies, key confounders are often unmeasured, requiring the use of noisy proxy variables. Pre-2026 methods for handling proxies, like regularization or two-stage regression, can yield biased effect estimates if the proxy-to-latent mapping is poorly specified. Learning a robust, low-dimensional representation from high-dimensional proxies is crucial for accurate estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects, especially in time-to-event settings where censoring adds complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contrastive learning objective, which enforces similarity between proxies from individuals with similar latent states and dissimilarity otherwise, can learn a more informative and disentangled representation of the hidden confounder than standard autoencoders or factor models, leading to less biased estimates of conditional average treatment effects on survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep learning framework that integrates a contrastive representation learning module with a survival outcome model. The encoder will be trained using a contrastive loss (e.g., SimCLR or NT-Xent) on augmented proxy data, aiming to pull together representations from the same latent subgroup while pushing apart others. This representation will then be used as an adjusted covariate in a flexible survival model, such as a DeepSurv or a recursively imputed survival tree, to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects. We will benchmark against existing methods for hidden confounding on semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for causal inference with hidden confounding that is more robust to proxy noise and misspecification. It will extend the toolkit for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in survival analysis, a critical area in clinical research. The resulting software and benchmarks will aid researchers in leveraging high-dimensional proxy data more effectively.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has excelled in computer vision and NLP, its application to learn representations specifically for latent confounding in causal survival analysis is novel. The integration of a contrastive objective with established causal survival estimators (e.g., recursively imputed trees) to handle proxy variables is a non-obvious and promising synthesis of pre-2026 techniques.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Causal Survival Forests: Stochastic Variational Inference for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Massive Observational Datasets
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data in large-scale electronic health records is crucial for personalized medicine but computationally prohibitive with existing Bayesian nonparametric methods. Pre-2026, methods like Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) for survival outcomes or recursively imputed trees for HTEs do not scale to millions of patients due to their reliance on Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a survival forest model with a structured variational approximation, trained with mini-batches via stochastic gradient descent, can accurately estimate HTEs from censored data at scale, matching the performance of full MCMC inference while being orders of magnitude faster.
proposals.method: We will extend the Bayesian Causal Forest framework to survival outcomes using a proportional hazards or accelerated failure time model with a tree ensemble. The key innovation is replacing MCMC with stochastic variational inference (SVI). We will derive a mean-field variational distribution over tree structures and leaf parameters, using a reparameterization trick for continuous relaxations of the discrete tree topology. The evidence lower bound (ELBO) will be optimized using adaptive mini-batch stochastic gradient descent (e.g., Adam), enabling single-pass training on massive datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable, fully Bayesian nonparametric method for HTE estimation in survival analysis, enabling application to nationwide registries and large biobanks. It will deliver open-source software, bridging the gap between flexible causal inference models and modern large-scale machine learning practice.
proposals.novelty: While SVI for Bayesian trees exists, its application to causal survival forests with censored data and treatment effect estimation is novel. The integration of SVI with the specific data augmentation schemes needed for censored survival outcomes within a causal framework represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Lightweight Causal Survival Forests for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation with Few Proxy Variables
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational survival data is crucial for personalized medicine but often requires high-dimensional covariate data to control confounding. In many real-world clinical settings, only one or two key proxy variables (e.g., a single biomarker or a composite score) are reliably measured, rendering complex, high-dimensional models prone to overfitting and instability. A methodologically rigorous yet simple approach for this low-information setting is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a survival forest architecture, explicitly regularized for low-dimensional proxy input and paired with a targeted learning step for censored outcomes, can provide robust and interpretable estimates of HTEs when only one or two confounding proxies are available.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to a 'Few-Proxy' setting (FP-RIST). The method will employ shallow, highly regularized causal survival trees grown on the limited proxy variables, using a novel splitting criterion that prioritizes treatment effect heterogeneity over prognostic effects. To handle censoring, we will integrate a targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE) step, using the forest output as an initial estimator for the conditional hazard, followed by a fluctuation step optimized for the low-dimensional covariate space to achieve double robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, computationally efficient tool for HTE estimation in data-scarce clinical scenarios. It will extend the causal survival forest literature to a pragmatically important but understudied setting and offer clear guidance on the feasibility of personalization when rich covariate data is unavailable.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods like RIST and causal forests are designed for high-dimensional settings, this work explicitly designs and optimizes a survival forest for the opposite regime—extreme covariate sparsity—which requires fundamentally different regularization and estimation strategies to avoid misleading conclusions.

proposals.title: Learning Latent Confounder Models from Proxy Variables: An Inverse Problem Approach for Causal Inference with Imperfect Measurements
proposals.motivation: In observational studies, true confounders are often unobserved, and researchers must rely on noisy proxy variables (e.g., diagnostic codes, lab tests). Pre-2026 methods like deconfounding or instrumental variable networks assume a known or simple proxy model, which is often misspecified. Learning the data-generating process for proxies from estimated confounders can improve the robustness of causal effect estimation, especially for time-to-event outcomes where censoring complicates the problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a set of estimated latent confounders (e.g., from a variational autoencoder or factor model), it is possible to accurately learn the conditional distribution of the observed proxy variables, and that incorporating this learned measurement model will reduce bias in downstream causal estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage variational framework. First, we adapt a structured VAE (e.g., a Causal Effect Variational Autoencoder) to infer a latent confounder distribution from proxies, treatments, and outcomes. Second, we fix the inferred confounder posteriors and train a separate, invertible neural network (e.g., a Normalizing Flow) to model the complex likelihood P(proxies | confounders). This model will be evaluated on semi-synthetic benchmarks derived from real-world clinical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV) and applied to a survival analysis task using the recursively imputed trees method to handle censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method to model the proxy measurement process, moving beyond the common assumption of simple additive noise. It will yield a more complete causal graph specification for use with g-methods and doubly robust estimators, directly enhancing the reliability of treatment effect estimates in clinical prediction tasks where confounders are proxied.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on inferring confounders from proxies, inverting the problem to learn the measurement model from estimated confounders is largely unexplored. The integration of flexible, invertible generative models for the proxy process within a causal inference pipeline for survival data represents a novel synthesis of deep learning and causal methodology.

proposals.title: Active Proxy Selection for Causal Effect Estimation: An Information-Theoretic Framework with Survival Outcomes
proposals.motivation: In observational studies, especially in clinical settings, key confounders are often expensive or invasive to measure, leading researchers to rely on noisy proxy variables. Current methods assume a fixed set of proxies, but there is no principled framework for deciding *which* proxies to measure from a larger candidate set to maximize the precision of causal effect estimates under a measurement budget. This is critical for efficient study design in personalized medicine and survival analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active selection strategy, which sequentially chooses proxy measurements to maximize the expected reduction in uncertainty about the target causal effect, will yield more precise and data-efficient estimates than random or heuristic selection, particularly when dealing with time-to-event outcomes subject to censoring.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian active learning framework grounded in information theory. The method will treat the unmeasured confounder as a latent variable in a structural causal model. Using pre-2026 techniques like variational inference for hidden variable models, we will maintain a posterior distribution over the confounder and the causal effect (e.g., a survival hazard ratio). At each step, we will compute the expected information gain (EIG) for each candidate proxy—formalized as the reduction in entropy of the causal effect posterior—and select the proxy maximizing EIG. The approach will integrate with Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees for survival outcomes and doubly robust estimation to handle censored data and ensure robustness to model misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable algorithm for optimal proxy variable selection in causal inference, directly applicable to clinical study design. It will extend the causal inference toolbox for survival analysis by integrating active measurement selection, offering a principled way to allocate measurement resources. We will release benchmark datasets and software to evaluate the method against baselines.
proposals.novelty: While active learning for prediction is well-studied, its application to *causal effect estimation* via proxy selection is novel. The integration of this selection strategy with models for censored time-to-event outcomes and hidden confounding addresses a clear gap in pre-2026 methodology, moving beyond passive use of proxies.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Prediction Under Compositional Shift: Robust Estimation with Unseen Latent Components
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, models are often trained under the assumption that all latent patient subtypes or disease mechanisms are present in the training data. This 'known component' assumption fails in practice, as novel patient subgroups, disease variants, or environmental factors can emerge. This compositional shift threatens the validity of personalized treatment recommendations derived from historical observational data, such as electronic health records.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the data-generating process as a mixture of latent components and employing robust estimation techniques, we can develop models for time-to-event outcomes that maintain calibrated performance and valid heterogeneous treatment effect estimates even when novel, unseen components appear at test time.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of hidden variable models and survival causal forests. The approach involves: 1) Learning a latent component model (e.g., a mixture model or variational autoencoder) on the training covariates and (censored) outcomes. 2) Integrating a robust loss function, such as a Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) objective over latent groups, to penalize worst-case performance across inferred components. 3) Adapting the Recursively Imputed Trees algorithm to condition on the inferred latent structure and the DRO-regularized predictions, ensuring that treatment effect estimates are stable under shifts in component prevalence or the introduction of new components.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for robust causal survival analysis in the presence of latent compositional shift. It will yield practical algorithms for safer deployment of predictive and prescriptive models in clinical settings where patient populations evolve. We will also release a benchmark suite simulating such shifts in semi-synthetic data derived from real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: While existing work on domain adaptation and robust ML often addresses covariate shift, and causal inference methods handle observed confounding, our focus on robustness to *unseen latent components* in a survival and treatment effect context is novel. This bridges causal inference, survival analysis, and robust representation learning in a new way.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Under Non-Additive and Non-Independent Causal Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in many causal inference methods, including those for heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation, is the additive or independent contribution of treatment and covariates to the outcome. In complex clinical settings, such as time-to-event outcomes in fertility treatments or oncology, treatment effects likely interact with patient covariates in non-additive, synergistic, or inhibitory ways. Pre-2026 HTE methods often fail to capture these complex compositional mechanisms, risking biased effect estimates and suboptimal personalized treatment recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling non-additive, non-independent compositional mechanisms between treatment and covariates will yield more accurate and robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects, particularly in survival and other clinical prediction tasks where standard additive models are misspecified.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel neural network architecture that integrates principles from causal representation learning and structured additive models. The model will parameterize the outcome mechanism using a tensor decomposition or a structured interaction network to capture higher-order, non-additive dependencies between the treatment assignment and hidden representations of confounders. We will ground our approach in the potential outcomes framework, extending survival-specific methods like recursively imputed trees to handle these complex interactions, and employ targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) for robust inference under model misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for HTE estimation when causal mechanisms are compositional and non-independent. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating HTE estimators under model misspecification and offer new insights into clinical case studies, such as the effect of embryo culture duration, where biological interactions are inherently non-additive.
proposals.novelty: While existing work on HTE estimation handles non-linearity, it largely assumes additive separability of treatment and covariate effects. Our proposal directly challenges this independence assumption, formally modeling the compositional structure of the causal mechanism, which is a significant departure from standard practice in causal machine learning pre-2026.

proposals.title: Causal Representation Learning with Latent Outcome-Directed Confounding: A Framework for Bidirectional and Inverted Causal Structures in Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Standard causal inference for treatment effect and survival prediction assumes a unidirectional causal graph where covariates temporally precede and cause outcomes. However, in many clinical settings, this assumption is violated. For instance, a latent disease progression state (a future outcome) can influence the measurement of biomarkers (observed covariates), creating a form of outcome-directed confounding. Existing methods, including those for hidden confounders, fail to model this inverted or bidirectional causality, leading to biased estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) and survival functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling scenarios where latent outcomes causally influence observed covariates—or where causality is bidirectional—through a causal representation learning framework will yield more accurate and interpretable estimates of individualized treatment effects and survival distributions in the presence of such complex confounding structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture where the latent space is explicitly partitioned into (a) a treatment-invariant prognostic factor, (b) a latent 'future outcome' variable that can influence the observed covariates, and (c) a standard confounding factor. The model will be trained using a combination of evidence lower bound (ELBO) reconstruction loss and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust loss for the observed time-to-event or binary outcome, enforcing the desired causal structure. We will extend the framework to handle censored survival data, building on concepts from recursively imputed survival trees and latent survival analysis. The approach will be benchmarked against standard causal forests and latent confounder models on semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts (e.g., MIMIC-IV).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for causal inference under non-standard causal direction assumptions, expanding the toolkit for HTE estimation in complex biomedical data. It will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating causal methods when latent outcomes confound covariates, and offer new insights into clinical problems where disease progression retrospectively affects measured patient states.
proposals.novelty: While latent confounder models exist, the explicit inversion of the causal direction between outcomes and covariates within a representation learning setup is novel. This moves beyond merely correcting for hidden confounders to fundamentally rethinking the assumed causal graph structure in predictive clinical modeling.

proposals.title: Causal Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Complex, Compositional Data: A Graph-Structured Survival Model
proposals.motivation: Causal inference for treatment effect heterogeneity has advanced for tabular and simple attribute-object data, but many real-world domains, such as medical imaging (scenes with multiple anatomical structures) and multi-component clinical interventions, involve complex compositional structures with relational dependencies. Pre-2026 methods often ignore these relational structures or collapse them into flat feature vectors, potentially biasing effect estimates and obscuring meaningful subgroup discoveries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the compositional and relational structure of complex data within a causal survival framework will yield more accurate and interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) compared to methods that treat features as independent.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of recursively imputed trees for survival outcomes to handle graph-structured data. The core method will involve: 1) Representing compositional data (e.g., scenes) as attributed graphs using pre-2026 graph neural network (GNN) architectures (e.g., Graph Attention Networks). 2) Learning a shared representation that separates prognostic and treatment-specific components, building on ideas from TARNet and Dragonnet. 3) Integrating this representation into a survival-specific recursive imputation algorithm to handle censored time-to-event outcomes, enabling HTE estimation conditional on the graph structure and its node/edge attributes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled method for causal inference in relational and compositional data with survival outcomes. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating HTEs in complex structured domains and deliver a software tool that bridges causal inference, survival analysis, and geometric deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of graph-structured representation learning with recursive imputation for censored survival data within a causal framework, moving beyond simple attribute-based effect estimation to model effects in relationally structured subgroups.

proposals.title: Robust Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation for Survival Outcomes: A Benchmark Study on Real-World Data with Complex Confounding and Long-Tail Distributions
proposals.motivation: Current methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival analysis are primarily developed and validated on curated, often synthetic, datasets. This creates a significant gap between methodological research and clinical application, where data is characterized by complex, unmeasured confounding, high censoring rates, and long-tail distributions of patient characteristics. Establishing the robustness of HTE methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) on such realistic data is crucial for trustworthy deployment in personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art causal survival methods, including RIST and extensions incorporating hidden confounder models, will exhibit significantly degraded and variable performance on real-world datasets with natural confounding compared to their performance on standard semi-synthetic benchmarks, and that their relative ranking will change based on dataset-specific characteristics.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark suite of 5-7 real-world observational healthcare datasets (e.g., from MIMIC-IV, SEER, and clinical registries) with time-to-event outcomes and documented confounding challenges. We will systematically evaluate the RIST framework, Bayesian causal forests for survival, and survival counterfactual networks. Performance will be assessed using calibrated simulation-based evaluation strategies (e.g., using propensity score-based data generation) to approximate ground truth HTEs, measuring error in estimated Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE) across the distribution, especially in long-tail subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first focused benchmark of causal survival HTE methods on diverse, real-world clinical data, establishing practical performance baselines and failure modes. It will yield concrete guidance for practitioners on method selection based on dataset properties and will highlight specific limitations—such as sensitivity to unmeasured confounding—that must be addressed in future methodological development.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for causal estimation on cross-sectional outcomes, a systematic evaluation focused on the unique challenges of survival data (censoring, time-varying effects) in realistic, heterogeneous clinical settings is novel. The explicit focus on performance degradation due to natural, rather than artificially injected, confounding and long-tail distributions moves beyond standard methodological validation.

proposals.title: Causal Sufficiency Scores: Benchmarking Disentangled Representations for Off-Policy Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Disentangled representation learning promises to identify latent causal factors, but current metrics (e.g., Beta-VAE score, DCI) assess statistical independence, not causal utility. For clinical decision-making, a representation must be causally sufficient—containing all confounders—to enable valid off-policy estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational survival data. Without metrics that directly evaluate this sufficiency, practitioners cannot reliably select representations for downstream causal tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a representation's performance on a suite of simulated, intervention-based tasks—where ground-truth causal factors are known—provides a direct, quantifiable measure of its causal sufficiency, which will strongly correlate with its accuracy in estimating real-world HTEs from biased observational data.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark of synthetic datasets with known causal graphs and hidden confounders, building on frameworks like CausalWorld. We will train state-of-the-art disentanglement methods (e.g., β-TCVAE, FactorVAE) and evaluate them with both existing metrics and our proposed Causal Sufficiency Score (CSS). The CSS will measure the representation's performance in accurately predicting the effects of targeted interventions in a held-out environment, using established causal estimators (e.g., Doubly Robust Learning, R-learner) adapted for survival outcomes with censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first benchmark and metric suite specifically designed to evaluate the causal adequacy of learned representations. It will yield practical guidance for selecting representations in clinical prediction tasks, such as estimating the effect of extended embryo culture on implantation, and advance methodology for leveraging biased offline data in reinforcement learning for healthcare.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond correlational disentanglement metrics to intervention-based evaluation is a fundamental shift. The integration of this evaluation with established causal inference estimators for time-to-event data, a core challenge in Shalit's prior work, creates a novel, task-driven validation paradigm.

proposals.title: Causal-Language Survival Models: Integrating Structured Knowledge for Robust Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Time-to-Event Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but is challenged by high-dimensional, unstructured clinical covariates and complex censoring. Pre-2026, causal survival forests and neural network-based models have advanced HTE estimation but often lack robustness and fail to leverage the rich, structured medical knowledge embedded in clinical ontologies and literature, limiting their generalization to rare patient subgroups or novel treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating structured medical knowledge bases (e.g., SNOMED-CT, UMLS) and pre-trained biomedical language models (e.g., BioBERT, PubMedBERT) into a causal survival estimation framework will yield more robust and generalizable estimates of individualized treatment effects by constraining and informing the model with domain-relevant priors and relationships.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework. The model will encode high-dimensional patient data (e.g., clinical notes, lab histories) using a pre-trained biomedical language model to create dense representations. These will be fused with structured embeddings of diagnostic codes and drug identifiers from a medical knowledge graph via a cross-attention mechanism. The fused representation will serve as input to a survival-supervised neural network with a treatment-conditional head, trained with a doubly-robust causal loss for time-to-event outcomes to estimate conditional average treatment effects (CATEs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available method for knowledge-augmented causal survival analysis and a benchmark demonstrating its performance on real-world clinical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV, SEER). It will advance the field by showing how symbolic and sub-symbolic knowledge integration can improve the stability and transportability of personalized treatment effect estimates in the presence of censoring.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge graphs and language models have been used for prediction, their integration into a formal causal inference framework for survival analysis, specifically for HTE estimation under censoring, is a novel synthesis of Shalit's prior work on causal survival trees with emerging pre-2026 representation learning techniques.

proposals.title: Neuro-Symbolic Program Synthesis for Interpretable Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but remains challenging. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees, often struggle to discover and represent complex, compositional effect modifiers—combinations of latent or observed covariates—in a human-interpretable form. This limits clinical trust and actionable insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neuro-symbolic program synthesis framework, which searches a space of interpretable programs over patient features, can more accurately and robustly identify compositional subgroups with distinct survival responses to treatment than contemporary black-box or tree-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a model combining a neural network encoder for high-dimensional, possibly censored, covariates with a symbolic program induction module. The encoder will learn representations, while the induction module, using techniques like DreamCoder or library learning, will synthesize compact programs that define subgroup membership and predict individual survival curves under treatment and control. We will integrate this with a causal regularization objective, such as a representation balancing term, to control for confounding, and evaluate on benchmarks from Shalit's prior work on IVF and medical survival data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable framework for HTE discovery in survival settings, advancing beyond black-box predictions. It will provide a benchmark for neuro-symbolic causal inference and deliver a tool that outputs human-understandable rules for treatment effect heterogeneity, directly informing clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of program induction—a powerful symbolic AI technique—with the causal estimation of survival effects, moving from associative subgroups to causally-defined, interpretable patient strata. This addresses a key gap between the flexibility of neural methods and the need for interpretability in clinical applications.

proposals.title: Causal Video Understanding: Estimating Treatment Effects of Spatio-Temporal Action-Object Compositions
proposals.motivation: In clinical and behavioral sciences, interventions are often complex, time-extended sequences of actions applied to specific objects (e.g., a surgical procedure). Pre-2026 causal inference from observational data focuses on static or single-point treatments, failing to model these structured, temporal compositions. Extending causal models to video sequences would enable the estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for real-world, procedural interventions captured in visual data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal model integrating temporal attention mechanisms with hidden confounder balancing can reliably estimate the effect of a compositional action-object treatment, defined over video segments, on a time-to-event or scalar clinical outcome.
proposals.method: We will extend the RIST (Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) framework to video. First, a spatio-temporal video backbone (e.g., a 3D CNN or Video Transformer) will segment the video and extract latent representations of action-object compositions as the time-varying treatment. We will adapt the RIST algorithm to handle this sequential, high-dimensional treatment, using recurrent or attention-based networks to model hidden time-varying confounding and perform targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) for effect estimation. The model will be validated on benchmark video datasets with simulated outcomes and real-world clinical video data of physiotherapy sessions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for estimating causal effects from observational video data, moving beyond static features to dynamic treatments. It will contribute a new benchmark for causal video understanding and a practical method for clinicians to evaluate the efficacy of complex behavioral or procedural interventions from real-world recordings.
proposals.novelty: While video recognition and causal inference are mature fields pre-2026, their integration for estimating effects of compositional treatments is novel. The key innovation is the formalization of video segments as structured, time-varying treatments within a robust survival/causal estimation framework like RIST, addressing hidden confounding in temporal visual data.

proposals.title: Causal Compositional Generation: Synthesizing Novel Image Concepts via Identified Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: Current generative models (e.g., GANs, diffusion models) can produce high-fidelity images but lack a principled, causal understanding of compositional concepts (e.g., 'object A on top of B'). This leads to unreliable control and poor generalization when generating novel, valid combinations. Grounding generation in a learned causal model, as developed in Uri Shalit's prior work on heterogeneous treatment effects, would enable robust, interpretable synthesis of specified compositions, which is critical for applications in data augmentation for vision tasks and scientific image simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: A causal model of compositional concepts, identified from observational image data, can be inverted to serve as a structured prior for a generative process, enabling the reliable synthesis of novel, valid image compositions that adhere to the underlying causal relationships.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Heterogeneous Treatment Effect estimation for image covariates using recent advances in causal representation learning (e.g., CausalVAE). First, we will learn a structural causal model (SCM) where interventions on latent variables correspond to compositional concepts (treatments), and outcomes are image features. We will then invert this SCM: given a desired compositional target (e.g., a specific treatment effect), we will use the identified causal mechanisms to constrain a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) or a diffusion model. The generation process will be guided by the SCM to ensure the output satisfies the causal dependencies, using techniques from causal transport for distribution matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for causally-constrained image generation, providing stronger guarantees on the validity of synthesized compositions. It will also demonstrate a new application for identified treatment effect models, moving beyond prediction to controlled synthesis. The resulting benchmark and method will bridge causal inference and generative AI.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference is typically used for estimation and prediction, its direct application to *invert* a model for controlled generation of complex, high-dimensional outputs is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from standard conditional generation by enforcing identifiability and validity through a pre-learned causal graph.

proposals.title: Leveraging Invalid Counterfactuals for Causal Structure Discovery in Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In causal inference for clinical prediction and treatment effect heterogeneity, identifying the true causal structure from observational data is a fundamental challenge. Pre-2026 methods often rely solely on observed positive examples (valid patient trajectories), ignoring the rich information contained in known invalid or impossible compositions (e.g., biologically implausible treatment-outcome sequences). Systematically leveraging these 'negative examples' could provide crucial constraints to disentangle causal relationships from spurious associations.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling known invalid data patterns as constraints during causal discovery will yield more accurate and interpretable causal graphs, which in turn will improve the estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects, particularly in survival settings with censored outcomes.
proposals.method: We propose a novel framework integrating constraint-based causal discovery with machine learning for survival analysis. First, we will formalize domain knowledge of 'impossible compositions' as hard logical constraints or soft penalties within a causal structure learning algorithm, such as an adapted PC or GES algorithm. This refined causal graph will then inform the specification of a survival model, building on Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees for time-to-event outcomes, to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects while ensuring the model respects the causal constraints derived from both positive and negative examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodology for incorporating domain knowledge of impossibilities into causal discovery, moving beyond correlation-based learning. It will deliver a software tool and theoretical results demonstrating how such constraints reduce model variance and identifiability issues in estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for survival outcomes from biased observational data.
proposals.novelty: While constraint-based causal discovery exists, the explicit use of *invalid* clinical or compositional examples as a primary source of constraints is novel. This approach inverts the typical learning paradigm, using what cannot happen to better understand what can, specifically within the context of clinical treatment effect estimation.

proposals.title: Identifiable Latent Dynamics for Causal Survival Prediction via Regularized Flexible Priors
proposals.motivation: Sequential models like state-space models and recurrent neural networks are crucial for predicting time-to-event outcomes and heterogeneous treatment effects from longitudinal clinical data. However, these models often suffer from non-identifiability, where a flexible prior on the initial latent state and complex transition dynamics are interchangeable, leading to unstable and uninterpretable estimates of patient trajectories and treatment responses. This instability undermines their reliability for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a flexible, learnable prior (e.g., a normalizing flow) within a constrained optimization framework that penalizes compensation between the prior and transition dynamics will yield identifiable latent representations, leading to more robust and interpretable estimates of individualized survival functions and dynamic treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational framework for sequential data with time-to-event outcomes. The initial state distribution will be parameterized by a normalizing flow. The key innovation is a novel regularizer applied to the learned transition function, such as a spectral norm penalty or a Jacobian-based constraint, that discourages it from absorbing variability that should be attributed to the prior. This forces the transition dynamics to be the primary explainer of temporal evolution. We will integrate this into a causal survival model to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects, leveraging techniques from Shalit's prior work on handling censored data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for learning identifiable latent dynamics in sequential models, improving the stability and interpretability of predictions. It will directly contribute to the field of causal inference for survival analysis by enabling more reliable estimation of dynamic treatment regimes from biased, observational longitudinal data.
proposals.novelty: While flexible priors and complex transitions are common, the explicit, regularized joint optimization to ensure identifiability by disentangling their roles is novel. This moves beyond standard variational inference, which does not address this specific identifiability trade-off.

proposals.title: Modeling Heterogeneous Initial Conditions: A Mixture Prior Approach for Sequential Latent Variable Models in Clinical Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks using sequential latent variable models (e.g., for survival analysis or treatment effect estimation), the standard Gaussian prior for the initial latent state is often an unrealistic oversimplification. Patient populations are heterogeneous, with distinct subgroups (e.g., different disease severities or comorbidities) that should correspond to different initial latent regimes. A misspecified prior can bias downstream inference on hidden covariates and treatment effects, limiting model personalization and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: Employing a flexible, non-Gaussian mixture prior (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Model or spike-and-slab) for latent state initialization will better capture patient heterogeneity at time zero, leading to more accurate and interpretable estimates of hidden clinical states and their subsequent impact on time-to-event outcomes and heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will extend sequential models like latent-variable recurrent neural networks or hidden Markov models for survival analysis. The initial state z0 will be drawn from a prior p(z0) parameterized as a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with learnable weights, means, and covariances, or a spike-and-slab distribution. Inference will be performed via variational methods, deriving an evidence lower bound (ELBO) that includes the mixture prior. We will benchmark against standard Gaussian priors on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., from ICU time-series or fertility treatment cycles), evaluating performance on tasks like dynamic prediction and conditional average treatment effect (CATE) estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for incorporating population heterogeneity into the initialization of dynamic clinical models. It will yield improved methods for personalizing predictions from longitudinal data and more accurate estimation of how hidden patient states moderate treatment effects over time. The approach directly addresses a key modeling limitation in causal inference with sequential confounding.
proposals.novelty: While mixture models are known in clustering, their application as an informative prior for the initial state in sequential latent variable models for clinical prediction is novel. This bridges ideas from geophysical inverse problems to machine learning for healthcare, moving beyond the ubiquitous and often inappropriate Gaussian initial prior.

proposals.title: Causal Inverse Planning with Non-Gaussian Latent Priors for Robust Off-Policy Treatment Strategies
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, planning optimal treatment sequences from observational data is an inverse problem complicated by partial observability of patient state and multi-modal initial conditions (e.g., ambiguous diagnoses). Standard latent variable models for planning, like deep variational approaches, often assume Gaussian priors, which fail to capture complex, skewed, or multi-modal belief states, leading to non-robust or overly simplistic policies. This limits their utility in safety-critical domains where accounting for full uncertainty is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating treatment planning as inference in a latent space with a flexible, non-Gaussian prior—learned from offline data—will yield more robust and diverse policy strategies that better reflect clinical uncertainty and lead to improved off-policy performance in sequential decision tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of control-as-inference and offline model-based RL. First, we will learn a deep generative model of state-action trajectories using variational inference with a flexible prior, such as a normalizing flow or a mixture model, to capture multi-modal latent patient states from biased observational data. For inverse planning, we will treat the desired outcome (e.g., survival past a time horizon) as observed evidence and use approximate inference (e.g., amortized SVGD or sequential Monte Carlo) to infer the sequence of actions that maximizes the posterior probability of success under the complex prior. This integrates causal adjustment for time-varying confounding via the learned latent dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for robust off-policy planning under partial observability, directly applicable to dynamic treatment regimes. It will provide new benchmarks for evaluating heterogeneous treatment effects in sequential settings, moving beyond point estimates to distributions of plausible strategies. The methods will enhance the safety and interpretability of AI-assisted clinical planning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit integration of flexible, non-Gaussian latent state priors—informed by causal structure from offline data—into the control-as-inference paradigm for treatment planning, moving beyond the ubiquitous Gaussian assumption to directly model clinical ambiguity and multi-modality.

proposals.title: Flexible Priors for Latent State Initialization in Hidden State Models for Causal Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient populations are often heterogeneous, with subgroups exhibiting distinct baseline risks that may not be well-captured by a simple Gaussian prior. Pre-2026 methods for survival analysis and treatment effect estimation using hidden state models (e.g., for time-varying confounding) typically assume a Gaussian prior on the initial latent state for tractability. This restrictive assumption can bias the learned representations and downstream estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects, especially when the true latent risk distribution is multi-modal or heavy-tailed.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the standard Gaussian prior on the initial latent state in hidden state models with a flexible, learnable prior (e.g., a mixture model or normalizing flow) will yield more accurate representations of patient heterogeneity, leading to improved estimation of individualized survival curves and conditional average treatment effects (CATE) in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend a state-of-the-art framework, such as the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) model from Shalit's prior work, by modifying its latent variable initialization. Specifically, we will model the prior distribution p(z_0) using a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) or a RealNVP normalizing flow, whose parameters are learned jointly with the encoder/decoder networks. The model will be trained on right-censored observational data using a variational lower bound adapted for survival outcomes. We will benchmark against the Gaussian-prior baseline on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., from IVF studies) to evaluate performance in CATE estimation and survival prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for incorporating complex patient heterogeneity into causal survival models, improving the personalization of treatment effect estimates. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating latent state models in healthcare and offer a practical tool for clinical researchers aiming to identify subgroups with differential treatment responses from observational data.
proposals.novelty: While flexible priors are known in deep generative models, their integration into causal hidden state models for censored survival data is novel. This approach directly addresses a key modeling limitation in pre-2026 clinical prediction methods that blend hidden states and causal inference.

proposals.title: Learning Context-Compressed History Representations for Causal Survival Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient trajectories are inherently non-Markovian, where future health states depend on a long, complex history of treatments, biomarkers, and covariates. Standard Markov models are computationally tractable but often miss critical long-range dependencies, while modeling full history is infeasible. This limits the accuracy of personalized survival predictions and the estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects over time, which are central to Uri Shalit's research on causal inference for time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model jointly learning a low-dimensional, dynamic compression of patient history and context-dependent transition dynamics will more accurately predict survival outcomes and estimate time-varying treatment effects than models assuming Markovian structure or using fixed, hand-engineered history summaries.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent state-space model where a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer encoder compresses the observed patient trajectory into a latent context vector. This context conditions the parameters of a survival model (e.g., a Cox proportional hazards model or a discrete-time hazard network) for the next time step. The system will be trained end-to-end using a variational inference framework to learn the compression and dynamics, with a tailored objective combining reconstruction loss, prediction accuracy for time-to-event data, and, for causal queries, a regularization term inspired by targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) to control for time-dependent confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new modeling framework for handling long-range dependencies in clinical time-series data within a tractable probabilistic model. It will yield a method for more accurate and interpretable estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival analysis, directly contributing to the field of causal inference with longitudinal data. The proposed benchmarks will advance methodology for evaluating off-policy reinforcement learning in healthcare.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully joint, end-to-end learning of a *dynamic* history compression specifically optimized for survival prediction and causal estimation, moving beyond fixed summary statistics or Markov assumptions, which is non-trivial given the challenges of censoring and time-dependent confounding in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Modeling Non-Markovian State Transitions in Clinical Trajectories via Attention-Based Historical Context
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like survival analysis or treatment effect estimation, patient state transitions (e.g., disease progression) are often modeled as Markovian for tractability. This assumption is frequently violated, as future health states can depend on complex, long-range histories of past conditions and treatments. Existing methods for handling non-Markovian dynamics, such as higher-order Markov models, are rigid and suffer from data sparsity, limiting their utility in personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling long-range dependencies in patient state sequences using context-aware models adapted from computational linguistics will yield more accurate predictions of future states and individualized treatment effects than standard Markovian models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the n-gram and neural language model paradigm to state transition data. First, we will formalize k-th order state transition models as 'state-grams' and estimate them using regularization to handle sparsity. Second, we will develop a neural network architecture that processes sequences of past states (and treatments) using an attention mechanism over a sliding history buffer, akin to a transformer decoder with causal masking. This model will learn to weight relevant past states dynamically. We will benchmark these methods against standard Markov models on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV) for tasks like next-state prediction and counterfactual estimation of time-varying treatments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, flexible framework for non-Markovian state transition modeling in clinical settings. It will contribute new methodological tools for causal inference and reinforcement learning with offline data where history matters, directly improving the estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects over time. The resulting models will offer better interpretability through attention weights, highlighting which past events drive future predictions.
proposals.novelty: While n-grams and attention are standard in NLP, their systematic adaptation to formally replace the Markov assumption in general state transition models, particularly for clinical causal and survival tasks, is novel. This approach moves beyond simple higher-order Markov chains by enabling data-driven, variable-length historical dependence.

proposals.title: Modeling Long-Range Dependencies in Patient Trajectories: A Non-Markovian Framework for Dynamic Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation often rely on Markovian state-transition models, which assume the future depends only on the immediate past.
