This is a severe limitation in chronic diseases like cancer or heart failure, where patient progression and treatment response are shaped by long-term history, including past treatments, cumulative side effects, and disease evolution. Existing methods for survival analysis and dynamic treatment regimes largely ignore these higher-order dependencies, risking biased effect estimates and suboptimal recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling long-range dependencies in patient trajectories using flexible, non-Markovian transition models will significantly improve the accuracy of dynamic treatment effect estimation and long-term outcome prediction compared to standard Markovian approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel neural architecture combining attention mechanisms (e.g., Transformers) with hidden semi-Markov models to capture variable-length historical dependencies in sequences of clinical covariates and treatments. The model will be trained on large-scale electronic health record data using a counterfactual objective, building on Shalit's prior work on survival trees and causal inference. We will derive a consistent estimator for heterogeneous treatment effects over time by adapting the framework of G-estimation for structural nested models to this non-Markovian setting, explicitly accounting for informative censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for learning from longitudinal observational data where history matters, directly applicable to personalized medicine. It will yield a benchmarked, open-source tool for non-Markovian dynamic treatment effect estimation, advancing the fields of causal inference and clinical machine learning beyond the restrictive Markov assumption.
proposals.novelty: While attention models are known in sequence modeling, their principled integration with causal survival estimation to handle long-term dependencies, informative censoring, and dynamic treatment regimes in clinical data is a novel and non-trivial synthesis. This moves beyond simple Markovian extensions or fixed-length history windows common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Adaptive Low-Rank Variational Inference for Uncertainty-Aware Causal Survival Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) on time-to-event outcomes, capturing parameter uncertainty is crucial for reliable decision-making. Standard mean-field variational inference (MFVI) fails to model posterior correlations, leading to underestimated uncertainty, while full-covariance approximations are computationally prohibitive for high-dimensional models. A scalable, structured posterior approximation is needed to make principled uncertainty quantification viable in modern causal and survival analysis with deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive low-rank plus diagonal variational posterior can efficiently capture the dominant dependencies in the parameter space of deep survival and treatment effect models, leading to more accurate uncertainty estimates and improved calibration of causal effect predictions without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational inference framework where the posterior covariance is Σ = VVᵀ + D, with V a low-rank matrix and D diagonal. The optimal rank k will be determined adaptively during training by monitoring the eigenspectrum of the generalized Gauss-Newton matrix, a curvature approximation of the ELBO, to retain only the most influential correlation directions. We will integrate this posterior into deep survival models (e.g., DeepSurv, Cox-Time) and treatment effect estimators (e.g., Dragonnet, CFRNet), using stochastic gradient descent with reparameterization gradients for optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for scalable, full-covariance Bayesian deep learning in clinical prediction, directly applicable to HTE estimation from censored data. It will yield more trustworthy uncertainty intervals for individual treatment effect predictions, enhancing the reliability of data-driven clinical decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: The method's novelty lies in the adaptive, data-driven selection of the low-rank component based on the curvature of the objective, moving beyond fixed-rank heuristics. This bridges scalable variational inference with the specific needs of causal survival analysis, a combination not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Structured Variational Inference for Causal Survival Analysis: A Network-Constrained Covariance Posterior
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, accurate uncertainty quantification is critical for decision-making. Standard variational inference methods use a mean-field assumption, ignoring dependencies between latent variables, which can lead to underestimated uncertainty and poor model calibration, especially in high-dimensional settings with hidden confounding. This limits the reliability of learned models for personalized treatment effect estimation in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parameterizing the variational posterior's covariance with a structured, sparse network—inspired by ecological interaction matrices—will capture essential dependencies between latent clinical covariates, leading to more accurate uncertainty estimates and improved performance in causal survival tasks compared to mean-field or dense, unconstrained approximations.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational inference framework where the posterior covariance matrix is constrained to be the inverse of a sparse precision matrix, regularized with a graph Laplacian prior to promote smoothness and community structure akin to ecological networks. This structure will be learned jointly with the model parameters via stochastic gradient descent, using the reparameterization trick. We will integrate this structured posterior into a survival model with latent variables for hidden confounding, building on recent work like recursively imputed trees, and evaluate it on benchmarks for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation with time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computationally tractable method for full-covariance variational inference in high dimensions, specifically tailored for clinical prediction models. It will deliver improved uncertainty quantification for estimated treatment effects in survival analysis, enhancing the reliability of data-driven clinical decision support. The ecological analogy offers a principled way to introduce domain-informed structure into probabilistic deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the structural principles of ecological interaction networks (e.g., sparsity, community structure) to constrain the variational posterior's covariance, moving beyond generic low-rank approximations. This structured, interpretable approach to capturing dependencies is largely unexplored in causal inference and survival analysis with latent variable models.

proposals.title: Full-Covariance Variational Inference for Causal Survival Analysis: Improving Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and causal inference for survival outcomes, variational inference (VI) is used to approximate complex posterior distributions, such as in models with hidden confounders or latent variables. The standard mean-field approximation, which assumes a factorized posterior, is computationally convenient but can severely underestimate posterior uncertainty and distort dependency structures. This is particularly problematic for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from censored data, where accurate uncertainty quantification is crucial for reliable clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing a full-covariance variational posterior, which models parameter dependencies, will yield more accurate and less overconfident estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival models compared to standard mean-field VI, especially in the presence of latent confounding.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational inference framework for a causal survival model, such as a latent variable model for time-to-event outcomes with hidden covariates. The core technical innovation is replacing the mean-field posterior with a multivariate Gaussian parameterized by a full Cholesky factor, using the reparameterization trick for low-variance gradient estimation. We will integrate this into a tree-based or neural network architecture for flexible HTE estimation, similar to the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework but with a probabilistic, full-covariance backbone. Evaluation will use established benchmarks for offline reinforcement learning and causal inference with biased data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable method for uncertainty-aware estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects from censored observational data. It will advance the methodological toolkit for causal survival analysis by demonstrating how moving beyond mean-field assumptions improves the reliability of clinical predictions. The resulting software and benchmarks will directly benefit researchers studying treatment effect heterogeneity in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While full-covariance VI is known in machine learning, its application to causal survival analysis and HTE estimation from censored data is novel. This approach challenges the entrenched use of factorized approximations in this specific domain, aiming to capture crucial dependencies between latent treatment and outcome models that mean-field methods ignore.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Hierarchical Priors for Robust Estimation of Time-Varying Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational longitudinal data is crucial for personalized medicine, but standard methods assume treatment effects are stationary. In clinical reality, patient physiology and treatment responses evolve due to disease progression, adaptation, or external factors, creating non-stationary dynamics. Existing survival and causal methods struggle to robustly track these time-varying effects while avoiding overfitting to noise and maintaining statistical validity under censoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the non-stationary evolution of treatment effect parameters via a meta-learned hierarchical prior over a latent process will enable more robust and interpretable estimation of time-varying HTEs from censored time-to-event data, outperforming methods that treat non-stationarity as noise or abrupt breaks.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured probabilistic meta-learning model where a primary network (e.g., a recurrent or structured deep survival model) outputs time-to-event distributions, but its emission parameters are governed by a latent stochastic process with its own dynamics. This latent process will be regularized by a meta-learned hierarchical prior, encouraging smooth, temporally consistent evolution. We will adapt and extend concepts from neural processes and Bayesian meta-learning (e.g., MAML) to learn this prior from multiple patient trajectories, enabling the model to differentiate true regime shifts from noise. Inference will handle right-censoring via recursive imputation strategies aligned with Shalit's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for estimating time-varying causal effects in survival settings, explicitly addressing non-stationarity. It will yield a method that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in sequential learning and offers more stable, interpretable long-term predictions of treatment benefit. We will release benchmarks for evaluating non-stationary HTE estimation in offline clinical data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of structured hierarchical meta-learning priors into the causal survival analysis framework to explicitly model and regularize the *process* of parameter non-stationarity, rather than treating it as a nuisance or detecting abrupt changepoints.

proposals.title: Adaptive Causal Survival Networks: A Complementary Learning System for Non-Stationary Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from longitudinal observational data, such as electronic health records, is challenging due to non-stationarity: treatment protocols, patient populations, and disease dynamics evolve over time. Standard models with fixed parameters fail to adapt, while naively retraining models risks catastrophic forgetting of past, still-relevant knowledge. This stability-plasticity dilemma limits the long-term utility of predictive and causal models in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network architecture with dual, interacting parameter systems—a fast-learning plastic component and a slow-changing consolidated component—governed by a meta-learning rule based on prediction error and temporal context, will enable accurate, adaptive estimation of time-varying treatment effects while preserving robust long-term causal knowledge.
proposals.method: We will extend a recurrent or transformer-based survival model (e.g., Dynamic-DeepHit) by factorizing its parameters for transition and emission functions. Each parameter vector will be split into plastic (θ_p) and consolidated (θ_c) parts, with the effective parameter computed as a context-dependent gated combination. A meta-learner, inspired by optimization-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML), will use recent temporal batches to compute a fast gradient update to θ_p. A consolidation mechanism, analogous to synaptic tagging and capture, will selectively transfer information from θ_p to θ_c based on the importance and stability of the update, evaluated via an online Fisher Information approximation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled method for continual learning in causal inference and survival analysis, directly addressing temporal shift. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating HTE estimators in non-stationary environments and offer a practical tool for adaptive clinical decision support that evolves with medical practice without forgetting validated causal relationships.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and continual learning are active areas, their integration into causal survival models via a biologically-inspired dual-parameter system, explicitly designed to manage the trade-off between adapting to new data and preserving causal knowledge, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond simple fine-tuning or ensemble methods prevalent in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Adaptive Meta-Learning for Non-Stationary Survival Trajectories with Latent Parameter Drift
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient risk trajectories and treatment effect dynamics are inherently non-stationary, influenced by evolving protocols, environmental factors, and pathogen evolution. Existing online learning and survival analysis methods, including hidden Markov models and time-varying coefficient models, struggle to disentangle short-term observational noise from genuine long-term parameter drift, leading to poor forecasting and representational collapse in continuous monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the latent dynamics of a primary survival or treatment effect model as being governed by a separate, slower-time-scale meta-process will enable more robust online tracking of non-stationary outcomes and provide predictive forecasts of future model parameter states.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian online learning framework. The primary layer is a latent variable survival model (e.g., a recurrent neural network or a Cox model with time-varying coefficients) for time-to-event prediction. Its parameters are not static but are governed by a secondary meta-model, implemented as a Gaussian Process or a low-rank linear dynamical system, which learns the drift. We will employ variational inference with adaptive, dual-time-scale updates: a fast update for the primary model given new data, and a slower, regularized update for the meta-model, using techniques from continual learning to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for continuous causal estimation in evolving clinical cohorts, distinguishing signal drift from noise. It will yield a concrete algorithm for online, non-stationary survival analysis that can forecast its own future calibration needs, directly applicable to dynamic treatment regime learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit, learnable separation of time scales for parameter evolution within a unified online inference scheme, moving beyond simple windowing or forgetting factors to actively model and predict the meta-dynamics of the core statistical model.

proposals.title: Time-Varying Hidden Markov Models for Non-Stationary Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient physiology and treatment responses evolve due to disease progression, adaptation, or external factors, violating the stationarity assumptions of standard latent variable models. Pre-2026 methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) and survival functions from observational data often rely on static latent representations, which can yield biased estimates when underlying dynamics are non-stationary. This is critical for personalizing long-term treatment strategies in chronic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the non-stationarity of emission and transition processes in a latent variable framework will significantly improve the accuracy of dynamic treatment effect estimation and time-to-event prediction from longitudinal, censored observational data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees framework by embedding it within a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) where the neural network parameters governing state transitions and the mapping from latent states to observed outcomes (emissions) are modeled as functions of time or of learned temporal contexts. Specifically, we will parameterize these functions using meta-learning networks (e.g., hypernetworks) that condition on a temporal embedding, allowing the model's dynamics to adapt smoothly over the observation period. Estimation will leverage variational inference and the Gumbel-Softmax trick to handle the discrete latent states, integrated with off-policy policy evaluation techniques to address confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible model class for estimating time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects and survival functions under non-stationary dynamics. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating causal inference methods on non-stationary data and offer clinical interpretability through the evolution of the learned latent states over time.
proposals.novelty: While time-varying coefficients exist in statistics, integrating non-stationary, neural parameterized dynamics into a latent variable model for causal survival analysis is novel. This moves beyond static hidden confounder models or simple time-indexed coefficients to a framework where the very structure of the latent dynamics evolves.

proposals.title: Amortized Variance Reduction for Straight-Through Estimators in Causal Generative Models of Survival
proposals.motivation: Estimating individualized treatment effects from observational time-to-event data requires models that can capture complex, structured data-generating processes, including discrete clinical decisions and censoring mechanisms. Current deep generative approaches for counterfactual estimation struggle to incorporate these non-differentiable components efficiently, leading to biased or high-variance gradient estimates that hinder learning. This limits the practical application of flexible models in clinical settings where interpretable, discrete logic is often present.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an amortized neural network to propose low-variance gradient directions for non-differentiable operations within a causal generative model will significantly improve the stability and sample efficiency of learning, leading to more accurate estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative adversarial network (GAN) or variational autoencoder (VAE) framework for time-to-event outcomes, where the generator includes discrete sampling steps (e.g., for treatment assignment or censoring indicators). For these steps, we employ a straight-through estimator (STE) but augment it with a small, amortized proposal network that takes the generator's latent state as input and outputs a refined gradient for the non-differentiable node. This proposal network will be trained concurrently with the main model using a variance-aware objective, drawing on techniques from REINFORCE and control variates. We will benchmark the method on semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts (e.g., MIMIC-IV) and evaluate its performance against standard STE and policy gradient baselines in estimating survival curves under different treatment rules.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical method for integrating domain knowledge and discrete structures into deep generative models for clinical prediction and causal inference. It will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating gradient estimation in structured generative models and offer open-source code tailored for survival analysis with heterogeneous effects. The resulting models will enable more faithful simulation of clinical pathways for offline policy evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While STEs are known, coupling them with a learned, amortized network specifically for variance reduction in the context of causal survival models is novel. This moves beyond simple relaxation or REINFORCE, directly addressing the high-variance bottleneck in a scalable, model-agnostic way for clinical applications.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Strategies for Causal Generative Models with Non-Differentiable Components in Clinical Time-to-Event Prediction
proposals.motivation: Modern generative models for clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, such as those for survival analysis, often rely on fully differentiable architectures to enable gradient-based learning. This requirement restricts model design, excluding clinically meaningful but non-differentiable components like discrete latent variables for patient subtypes or thresholding functions for treatment decisions. Consequently, we cannot fully leverage hybrid models that combine continuous representations with structured, discrete logic, limiting their expressiveness and interpretability in heterogeneous treatment effect estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gradient-free outer-loop optimizer, specifically a modern Evolution Strategy (ES), can effectively train complex generative models containing non-differentiable operations by treating the model as a black box, optimizing continuous parameters to maximize data likelihood without requiring end-to-end differentiability.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative model for time-to-event data with a non-differentiable discrete latent layer representing potential patient subpopulations. The model's continuous parameters (e.g., weights of neural network encoders/decoders) will be optimized using a state-of-the-art ES algorithm (e.g., CMA-ES or a Natural Evolution Strategy variant). The ES will propose populations of parameter vectors, evaluate each by running the full generative model (including its discrete sampling steps) to compute a scalar objective (e.g., marginal log-likelihood via a particle filter), and update the parameter distribution based on performance. This will be benchmarked against gradient-based baselines using differentiable relaxations (e.g., Gumbel-Softmax) on synthetic and real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for building and training a new class of hybrid generative models that incorporate discrete, structured reasoning for clinical prediction tasks. It will expand the methodological toolkit for causal inference and survival analysis by enabling models that are both more interpretable and more expressive. We will release open-source code and establish benchmarks for ES-based training of non-differentiable generative models.
proposals.novelty: While ES is known in reinforcement learning and robotics, its application as a surrogate trainer for non-differentiable causal generative models in clinical biostatistics is novel. This approach fundamentally decouples model design from gradient constraints, a significant shift from prevailing paradigms that rely on differentiable relaxations or REINFORCE-style estimators.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis via Non-Differentiable Hidden Markov Models
proposals.motivation: Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) and their extensions are powerful for modeling time-to-event outcomes with latent patient states, crucial for personalized treatment effect estimation. However, existing methods for learning such models from observational data, especially within a causal framework, often rely on restrictive assumptions of differentiability for transitions and emissions. This precludes the use of clinically meaningful, discrete decision rules or non-differentiable simulators, limiting model realism and applicability to complex clinical settings like IVF cycles or oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating modern gradient estimation techniques (e.g., REINFORCE, Gumbel-Softmax) with causal survival forests, we can learn HMMs with structured, non-differentiable components while still obtaining consistent estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects from censored observational data.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel algorithm that combines a structured HMM with a treatment-effect estimator. The HMM's discrete state transitions or emission distributions will be parameterized via discrete latent variables. We will employ the Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization trick for continuous relaxation during training where possible, and REINFORCE-style score function estimators for purely discrete operations. This model will be integrated into a meta-learner framework, building on Shalit's prior work with recursively imputed survival trees, to estimate Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE) for survival outcomes, ensuring robustness to censoring and hidden confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological bridge between flexible, interpretable generative models with discrete structure and rigorous causal estimation for survival outcomes. It will enable clinical researchers to incorporate domain knowledge via discrete rules into predictive models without sacrificing the ability to learn from data. We will release benchmark datasets and an open-source implementation to facilitate adoption.
proposals.novelty: While gradient estimation tricks exist, their integration into a causal survival framework for learning HMMs with non-differentiable components is novel. This directly relaxes a key technical barrier in applying complex latent-state models to treatment effect estimation from biased offline data, a core challenge in Shalit's research theme.

proposals.title: Structured Normalizing Flows for Scalable and Stable Variational Inference in Causal Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, complex posterior dependencies (e.g., between baseline hazards, treatment effects, and censoring mechanisms) are common, but standard mean-field variational approximations fail to capture them. While expressive flows offer flexibility, their training is unstable and their black-box nature lacks the interpretable structure needed for reliable causal and survival analysis, limiting their use in high-stakes medical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing normalizing flows with enforced, domain-informed dependency structures (e.g., block-sparse or low-triangular Jacobians), we can achieve expressive yet tractable posterior approximations that guarantee stable training and exact density evaluation, leading to more accurate uncertainty quantification in heterogeneous treatment effect models.
proposals.method: We will design structured invertible transformations where the Jacobian has a prescribed sparse pattern (e.g., block-diagonal per latent subgroup, or causal ordering constraints), ensuring analytical KL computation with a Gaussian base distribution. These flows will be integrated into a variational inference framework for survival models with hidden covariates. Training will use a natural-gradient or quasi-Newton optimizer (like L-BFGS) adapted to the flow's parameterization to overcome curvature issues common in flow-based VI.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a robust variational inference tool that captures structured dependencies essential for causal survival analysis, directly improving the estimation of time-to-event heterogeneous treatment effects. The method will yield a practical, scalable algorithm with convergence guarantees, bridging the gap between flexible deep learning and interpretable, stable probabilistic modeling for clinical data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic normalizing flows, our approach hardcodes application-relevant structural constraints into the flow architecture to ensure tractability, combining the expressivity of flows with the stability and interpretability of structured approximations—a targeted solution for causal inference problems not addressed by existing flow-based VI.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Variational Inference with Adaptive Patches for Modeling Complex Dependencies in Survival and Causal Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, latent variable models must capture complex, non-Markovian dependencies among patient covariates and latent states. Standard variational inference (VI) families, such as mean-field or sequential models, often fail to represent these intricate posterior structures, leading to biased uncertainty estimates and suboptimal predictions. This limits the reliability of personalized clinical decision support derived from such models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel variational family, inspired by hierarchical multi-scale material modeling, can more accurately approximate complex posteriors by adaptively blending a fast, factorized global approximation with localized, high-fidelity 'patch' distributions on subsets of highly dependent latent variables.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured variational family with a hierarchical architecture. A base mean-field distribution will provide global, efficient inference. An adaptive gating network, trained alongside, will identify residual dependency structures among latent variables (e.g., in hidden states or confounders). For identified clusters, we will deploy expressive 'patch' distributions parameterized by small transformer or graph neural networks to model their joint posterior accurately. The method will be integrated into a variational objective and evaluated on benchmarks for survival analysis with time-varying covariates and causal inference with hidden confounding, using biased offline clinical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, more expressive variational inference framework specifically designed for complex dependency patterns in clinical data. It will provide improved posterior approximations for models used in survival and causal prediction, leading to more accurate uncertainty quantification and treatment effect estimates. The adaptive, hierarchical approach offers a principled trade-off between computational efficiency and model fidelity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual transfer of the Representative Volume Element (RVE) paradigm from materials science to variational inference, creating an adaptive, patch-based variational family. This moves beyond sequential or fixed-structure approximations to dynamically capture non-Markovian, cluster-like dependencies not addressed by existing VI methods.

proposals.title: Causal Stress-Testing of Sequential Models: A Framework for Robust Generalization in Clinical Prediction
proposals.motivation: Sequential models for prediction and treatment effect estimation are increasingly applied to diverse clinical datasets, but claims of generalizability are often based on average performance on limited benchmarks. Real-world data presents heterogeneous challenges like irregular sampling, informative censoring, and time-varying confounding, which can lead to brittle performance and uninterpretable failures. A systematic framework to diagnose which algorithmic components succeed under specific data pathologies is critically needed for reliable deployment in healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential model's performance can be causally attributed to its specific architectural components (e.g., attention mechanisms, hidden state models) when confronted with isolated, synthetic data challenges, and that this attribution framework will accurately predict and explain its performance on complex real-world clinical sequences.
proposals.method: We will first develop a taxonomy of sequential data challenges (e.g., temporal sparsity, concept drift, hidden confounding) and generate a suite of synthetic benchmarks that isolate each. We will then stress-test a unified algorithm, building on Shalit's prior work on survival trees and hidden covariate models, incorporating components like recurrent neural networks, transformers, and causal forests. Finally, we will deploy the model on real-world clinical datasets (e.g., electronic health records for sepsis or cardiovascular outcomes) and use targeted causal mediation analysis to attribute performance variations back to the algorithmic components challenged by the synthetic benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open-source benchmark suite for stress-testing sequential models, 2) a causal attribution method for model diagnostics that moves beyond correlation, and 3) concrete design principles for building robust sequential models for clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation. This will advance the field from benchmarking to explainable engineering of reliable methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the causal, rather than correlational, link between synthetic stress-tests and real-world performance, and the formal mediation framework for attributing model behavior to specific components, which is absent from standard ablation studies or benchmark collections.

proposals.title: SDPC: A CASP-Inspired Framework for Benchmarking Causal and Predictive Models on Heterogeneous Sequential Data
proposals.motivation: Evaluating methods for sequential prediction and treatment effect estimation across domains like healthcare and economics is fragmented, with no standardized, blind benchmarks. This hinders objective comparison of generalizability, a core challenge for methods like those for survival analysis with hidden confounders. A community-wide challenge framework, proven transformative in computational biology via CASP, is urgently needed to drive rigorous progress in sequential data science.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a blind, periodic benchmarking challenge with curated sequential datasets will provide definitive, objective evidence on the relative performance and robustness of unified algorithms for prediction and causal inference, revealing their true domain generalizability beyond initial demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will design and launch the Sequential Data Prediction Challenges (SDPC). This involves: 1) Curating diverse, large-scale sequential datasets (e.g., longitudinal electronic health records, economic time-series, IoT sensor streams) with held-out future steps or outcomes. 2) Defining transparent evaluation suites combining domain-specific predictive accuracy (e.g., time-dependent Brier score) and causal estimand metrics (e.g., precision in estimating heterogeneous effects). 3) Running blind competitions where participants, including our team applying recursively imputed survival trees and offline RL methods, submit predictions for independent assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first community-standard, blind benchmark for sequential prediction and causal inference, enabling direct comparison of diverse methodologies. It will produce a public leaderboard and detailed analyses of algorithm strengths/weaknesses across domains, guiding future methodological development. The released datasets and framework will become a foundational tool for the field.
proposals.novelty: While benchmark datasets exist, the adoption of a blind, CASP-style challenge model with a focus on heterogeneous sequential data integrating both prediction and causal inference tasks is novel. This moves beyond static comparisons to a dynamic, ongoing test of real-world generalizability.

proposals.title: Learning Discrete-Continuous Latent Dynamics for Causal Heterogeneity in Time-to-Event and Treatment Sequences
proposals.motivation: Real-world sequential data, such as clinical ICU records or industrial sensor logs, are inherently hybrid, mixing discrete events (e.g., treatments, alarms) with continuous dynamics (e.g., vitals, degradation). Pre-2026 methods often model these aspects separately, limiting the ability to infer how discrete interventions causally affect continuous latent states over time, which is crucial for personalized prediction and understanding heterogeneous treatment effects in survival and other time-to-event settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified sequential model that jointly infers discrete latent regimes and continuous latent states from messy observational data will provide a more accurate and interpretable framework for estimating heterogeneous causal effects in time-to-event outcomes, outperforming methods that treat discrete and continuous dynamics independently.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational inference framework for a Switching State-Space Model (SSSM), where a discrete latent variable (modeled by a recurrent network or transformer) governs switches between dynamical regimes, and continuous latent variables evolve within each regime via neural ODEs or linear Gaussian dynamics. The model will be trained on sequences with right-censored outcomes, extending concepts from the researcher's work on recursively imputed trees to handle missingness. We will incorporate a targeted regularization loss derived from causal inference theory to de-bias estimates of the effect of discrete interventions (e.g., drug administration) on the continuous latent trajectory and the subsequent time-to-event outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable, general-purpose tool for decomposing complex temporal data into interpretable discrete-event timelines and associated continuous dynamics. It will advance the field of causal inference for time-to-event data by providing a new method to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects that accounts for the interplay between discrete interventions and latent continuous patient states, directly applicable to clinical and industrial prognostics.
proposals.novelty: While SSSMs and latent variable models exist, their integration with modern causal inference objectives for censored survival data, and their application as a unified engine for automatic decomposition of heterogeneous real-world sequences, represents a novel synthesis of the researcher's expertise in survival analysis, hidden covariates, and causal learning.

proposals.title: Generalizing a Unified Offline Causal Survival Algorithm to Large-Scale, High-Dimensional Sequential Domains
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in time-to-event data from observational sequences are fragmented, often handling censoring, hidden confounding, and sequential decisions separately. This limits robust application to complex, real-world datasets like electronic health records (EHR) or customer journeys, where all these challenges co-occur. A unified algorithm that addresses these jointly has been proposed but requires rigorous validation on diverse, large-scale sequences to prove its practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: The unified algorithm, which integrates survival analysis, causal inference for hidden covariates, and offline policy learning, will maintain calibration and outperform specialized baselines when applied to high-dimensional sequential datasets from non-medical domains, such as user engagement or industrial maintenance logs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core architecture from 'Heterogeneous Treatment Effect in Time-to-Event Outcomes,' which uses recursively imputed survival trees within a multi-task framework for censored outcomes and hidden confounding. This will be extended with a transformer-based encoder to handle long, high-dimensional sequential covariates. We will benchmark against state-of-the-art separate methods for causal survival (e.g., dynamic survival forests) and offline reinforcement learning (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning) on curated datasets from tech platforms (user churn) and industry (machine failure).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical evidence for a unified causal survival algorithm's generalizability, establishing a new benchmark for sequential decision evaluation under censoring. It will deliver open-source, pre-processed sequential datasets and a software package to bridge methodological gaps between biostatistics, causal inference, and machine learning communities.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious aspect is testing whether a method developed for clinical IVF data, with its specific censoring and confounding structures, can effectively capture complex, non-medical temporal dependencies and hidden states, a translation not guaranteed by existing theory.

proposals.title: Stable Causal Survival Modeling via Dynamically Coordinated Hybrid Networks
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event data, such as patient survival, is crucial for personalized medicine. Modern methods often use complex neural architectures for separate emission (outcome) and transition (time dynamics) functions, but their joint training is notoriously unstable, leading to poor convergence and unreliable effect estimates. This instability limits the practical deployment of powerful hybrid models in clinical settings where robustness is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dedicated, lightweight coordination module that actively monitors and modulates the gradient flow and feature interactions between the emission and transition networks will enable stable, robust training of hybrid sequential models, leading to superior estimation of individualized treatment effects in survival analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a Dynamically Coordinated Hybrid Network (DCHN) for survival analysis. The model uses a recurrent network for the transition function and a feed-forward network for the emission function. A novel coordination module, implemented as a small meta-network, will continuously monitor the hidden states and gradients of both sub-networks. It will apply adaptive gradient scaling (inspired by gradient surgery) and soft attention-based feature gating to align their learning dynamics, preventing one network from dominating or diverging. We will benchmark against established methods like Deep Survival Machines and CTM, using semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., SEER, MIMIC-IV) to evaluate stability and performance in estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for survival outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, stable framework for training hybrid neural models for causal survival analysis. It will yield a publicly available benchmark demonstrating the training stability and performance gains of coordinated architectures. The resulting models will offer clinicians more reliable tools for personalizing treatment timing and selection based on time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid networks and gradient manipulation techniques exist separately, the novel integration of a dedicated, active coordination module for real-time alignment of sub-network dynamics in a causal survival context is unexplored. This moves beyond simple loss weighting or adversarial training to a more holistic, model-aware stabilization strategy.

proposals.title: Dynamic Interface Networks for Heterogeneous Sequential Models: A Composite Materials Analogy for Survival and Treatment Effect Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation, sequential models must capture complex, multi-scale patterns: static covariates, dynamic treatment assignments, and time-to-event outcomes. Existing monolithic architectures (e.g., standard RNNs) often struggle to model the distinct emission (observation) and transition (state evolution) dynamics optimally, leading to suboptimal representation learning for causal and survival tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid neural architecture, with specialized modules for emission and transition functions connected by a learned, dynamic interface network—inspired by the functionally graded interfaces in composite materials—will yield more accurate and robust predictions for time-to-event outcomes and heterogeneous treatment effects than homogeneous or loosely coupled models.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a Transformer or CNN processes static and time-varying covariates for the emission module, and a recurrent or graph network handles the hidden state transitions. The core innovation is a trainable interface network, implemented as a small, attention-based meta-learner or a gated mixture-of-experts layer. This interface will dynamically combine gradients and representations from both modules during training, optimizing information flow akin to a material composite's interfacial region. We will evaluate on benchmark survival datasets (e.g., SUPPORT, METABRIC) and HTE estimation tasks from clinical trials, using established metrics like time-dependent C-index and policy risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, bio-inspired neural architecture design principle for sequential prediction, 2) a practical method for improving accuracy in clinical prediction tasks where emission and transition dynamics are inherently different, and 3) new benchmarks for hybrid models in causal survival analysis, extending the methodological toolkit for personalized medicine.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid models exist, the explicit design and learning of a dynamic, gradient-blending interface network, drawing a direct analogy to material science composites, is a novel architectural strategy not explored in pre-2026 machine learning for healthcare. It moves beyond simple concatenation or residual connections.

proposals.title: Architectural Inductive Biases for Robust Causal Dynamic Modeling with Noisy, Censored, and Small-Sample Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical and experimental sciences, estimating treatment effects from longitudinal data is crucial, but datasets are often small, noisy, and subject to censoring. While methods like survival forests and hidden Markov models address these issues individually, the interaction between model architecture and data degradation is poorly understood. Designing models that are inherently robust to these common data constraints would significantly improve the reliability of causal inference in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Hybrid neural architectures that explicitly encode structural priors (e.g., temporal locality, sparsity, or graph dependencies) into the emission and transition functions of a dynamic model will exhibit superior robustness and data efficiency compared to generic architectures when trained on degraded data typical of clinical studies.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as learning a hidden Markov model or a state-space model where emission and transition functions are parameterized by hybrid neural networks. We will systematically test combinations: CNNs for spatial/temporal locality in emissions, Graph Neural Networks for structured covariates in transitions, and attention mechanisms for long-range dependencies. Using benchmarks from survival analysis and offline reinforcement learning with biased data, we will train these models on datasets degraded by added noise, simulated missingness, and reduced sample size, evaluating their performance in estimating heterogeneous treatment effects or predicting time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a principled framework for selecting model architectures based on anticipated data quality, moving beyond ad-hoc design. It will yield concrete guidelines for building data-efficient dynamic models for causal inference, directly applicable to clinical prediction problems with limited, imperfect data. The benchmarks and analysis will clarify which inductive biases mitigate specific data deficiencies.
proposals.novelty: Instead of proposing a new monolithic model, we systematically dissect how architectural components confer robustness, bridging causal inference methodology with deep learning architecture design. This focus on architectural inductive biases for data-efficient learning in dynamic settings is underexplored in pre-2026 causal machine learning literature.

proposals.title: Hybrid Neural Networks for Dynamic Modeling of Treatment Effects in Time-to-Event Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) in survival analysis is crucial for personalized medicine, but existing methods often rely on restrictive parametric assumptions for the underlying stochastic process. Modern neural approaches for state-space models, such as deep Markov models, typically use homogeneous architectures (e.g., RNNs or transformers) for both emission and transition functions, which may not capture the distinct complexities of observational data generation and latent disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid neural architecture, explicitly decoupling and specializing the emission and transition networks, will yield more accurate and interpretable estimates of dynamic treatment effects from censored time-to-event data compared to homogeneous architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal deep Markov model where the transition function, modeling latent health state evolution, is implemented via a structured recurrent network (e.g., GRU-D) to handle irregular sampling and model disease dynamics. The emission function, linking latent states to high-dimensional, time-varying covariates (e.g., lab values), will use a flexible feed-forward or transformer network. For HTE estimation, we will integrate this hybrid model with a targeted learning framework or a modified R-learner adapted for survival outcomes, building on Shalit's prior work with recursively imputed survival trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, flexible framework for dynamic treatment effect estimation in survival contexts, explicitly addressing model misspecification in the latent process. It will contribute a new benchmark method for offline evaluation of treatment policies using biased observational data with time-dependent confounding.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid models exist in other domains, their application for causal survival analysis is unexplored. The key novelty is the principled architectural separation tailored to the distinct generative mechanisms of state transitions and noisy observations, moving beyond the homogeneous deep sequential models currently used in the field.

proposals.title: HAIS-VI: Scalable Hybrid Variational Inference for Mixed Latent Variable Models via Hamiltonian Annealed Importance Sampling
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, models often require latent variables that are both discrete (e.g., patient subgroups, treatment assignments) and continuous (e.g., physiological trajectories). Pre-2026 variational inference (VI) methods struggle with such mixed spaces, as discrete variables break pathwise gradients, and continuous relaxations like Gumbel-Softmax can introduce bias. This limits the development of expressive, interpretable models for survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation from complex, censored data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid inference algorithm, combining Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for continuous latents with a temperature-annealed Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for discrete latents within a unified variational framework, will provide a more scalable and lower-bias solution for mixed latent variable models compared to existing VI or MCMC-only approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a single inference network that outputs parameters for both the continuous latent distribution and the relaxed discrete distribution. For the continuous part, we will use Hamiltonian flows to enable efficient sampling from complex posteriors. For the discrete part, we will use a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation whose temperature is annealed according to a schedule during training. The training objective will be an annealed evidence lower bound (ELBO), derived from an importance-weighted bound, that progressively sharpens the discrete approximations while jointly optimizing the continuous flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general-purpose, scalable inference tool for models with mixed discrete-continuous latents, directly applicable to causal survival analysis and treatment heterogeneity estimation. It will advance methodological research by bridging ideas from Hamiltonian MCMC and variational inference with continuous relaxations, offering a practical alternative to complex, model-specific inference schemes.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the principled integration of Hamiltonian dynamics for continuous variables and annealed discrete relaxations within a single, end-to-end trainable variational framework, an approach inspired by but distinct from hybrid methods in computational physics. This unified treatment of mixed variables within VI is non-trivial and underexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Modeling with Mixed Latent State Spaces for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient heterogeneity often arises from a mix of discrete (e.g., disease subtypes, adherence patterns) and continuous (e.g., physiological severity) latent factors. Pre-2026 methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival analysis, such as recursively imputed survival trees, primarily handle continuous confounders. A framework that explicitly models mixed discrete-continuous latent states is needed to better capture this complexity and improve the personalization of treatment timing and selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling a mixed discrete-continuous latent state space within a causal survival framework will yield more accurate and interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data with censoring.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to incorporate a structured latent variable model. The approach will combine a variational autoencoder (VAE) with a discrete latent component (using a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for gradient-based learning) to infer the mixed state. This latent representation will be integrated into a survival model, such as a Cox-based or deep parametric survival network, with treatment assignment modeled via a propensity network. Estimation will leverage pre-2026 advances in variational inference for missing data and counterfactual reasoning to handle censoring and ensure identifiability under causal assumptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible method for HTE estimation in survival outcomes that can discover clinically meaningful patient subgroups (discrete states) while adjusting for continuous severity. It will contribute a publicly available benchmark for evaluating causal survival models with mixed latent structures and offer more nuanced guidance for personalized treatment strategies in oncology and chronic disease management.
proposals.novelty: While latent variable models exist, their integration into a *causal* survival framework for HTE estimation with *mixed* discrete-continuous states is novel. This moves beyond standard latent confounder models, which are typically continuous, to enable the discovery of interpretable latent phenotypes that modify treatment effect.

proposals.title: Causal Multi-Scale Temporal Abstraction: Efficient and Robust Representations for Long-Horizon Clinical Time Series
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal clinical data, such as electronic health records, present long sequences with multi-scale temporal dependencies. Current deep learning methods for long sequences, like Transformers, are computationally prohibitive and often learn spurious correlations, leading to poor generalization under distribution shifts and unreliable treatment effect estimates in downstream causal tasks. A scalable method that learns robust, transferable temporal concepts is urgently needed for reliable clinical prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical temporal model trained with a causal invariance objective can learn computationally efficient, multi-scale representations of long sequences that are robust to distribution shifts and improve performance on downstream tasks like heterogeneous treatment effect estimation and survival analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical encoder using memory-efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., Performer or Linformer) to process long sequences. The model will be trained with a multi-task objective combining sequence reconstruction and an adversarial or contrastive loss to enforce invariance to identified nuisance factors (e.g., hospital-specific coding practices). The resulting representations will be evaluated on downstream tasks using established causal inference frameworks for time-to-event outcomes, specifically adapting methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees to leverage the abstracted features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable framework for learning from long-horizon patient data that prioritizes out-of-distribution robustness. It will contribute new benchmarking protocols for evaluating temporal representations under distribution shift and demonstrate improved estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival settings, directly advancing reliable clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: The integration of causal invariance principles into the architecture and training of efficient long-sequence models is novel, moving beyond mere compression to learn representations explicitly designed for reliability in causal downstream tasks prevalent in clinical research.

proposals.title: Sparse Global Routing for Efficient Causal Survival Analysis from Long-Term Patient Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal patient data, such as high-frequency physiological monitoring or dense electronic health records, forms extremely long time series critical for predicting time-to-event outcomes like disease progression or treatment failure. Standard Transformer-based models for such tasks suffer from quadratic complexity in sequence length, making them computationally infeasible and obscuring interpretable long-range dependencies. Efficiently modeling these dependencies is essential for accurate, individualized survival prediction and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that processes long sequences via local chunked attention, guided by a sparse global router learned from a downsampled view, will achieve computational efficiency comparable to linear attention methods while preserving the modeling accuracy of full self-attention for long-range, clinically-relevant dependencies in survival analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a Hierarchical Chunked Attention with Global Router (HCAGR) model. The input sequence is divided into overlapping local chunks processed with standard self-attention. In parallel, a lightweight router network (e.g., a small MLP or convolutional network) operates on a drastically downsampled global representation of the sequence. This router learns to predict a sparse adjacency matrix, establishing direct attention links only between the most semantically related distant chunks (e.g., linking a treatment initiation chunk to a later adverse reaction chunk). The final architecture integrates local and sparse global attention. We will benchmark HCAGR against full Transformers and efficient variants (e.g., Linformer, Performer) on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets for censored survival prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable architecture for survival analysis from long sequences, directly addressing a bottleneck in clinical ML. It will contribute a new benchmark for efficient sequence modeling in medical contexts and offer improved interpretability through the learned sparse global connections, which may reveal long-range clinical patterns. The method will be integrated into the causal survival analysis toolkit, enhancing the estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects over time.
proposals.novelty: While chunking and hierarchical methods exist, the key novelty is the adaptive, learned sparse global router inspired by genomic indexing, which is distinct from fixed-pattern or low-rank projection approaches. This specifically tailors long-range connectivity to the semantic needs of the clinical prediction task, a concept not yet explored in survival analysis.

proposals.title: Dynamic Resource Allocation for Causal Survival Analysis in Long, Imperfect Clinical Time Series
proposals.motivation: Modern clinical datasets, such as longitudinal EHRs or wearables data, present a dual challenge: they are extremely long and plagued by noise, irregular sampling, and informative censoring. Existing methods for survival or treatment effect estimation often fail to scale computationally or are brittle to these data imperfections. A unified framework is needed to make reliable, individualized predictions from such data under realistic computational and data-quality constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A resource-aware inference framework that dynamically allocates computational budget between sequence-length processing and sophisticated noise/imputation handling, guided by online data-quality diagnostics, will yield more accurate and robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes than static, monolithic algorithms.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-algorithm that integrates Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees for survival and hidden covariate models. The core is a controller that, for each patient's time series, runs online diagnostics (e.g., noise level, missingness pattern, censoring intensity) to decide between: 1) investing budget in adaptive subsampling or hierarchical compression of the long sequence, or 2) investing in more sophisticated multiple imputation or de-noising steps. This decision will be optimized via a reinforcement learning policy, trained on biased offline benchmarks, to minimize a downstream causal estimation error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, adaptive algorithm for scalable and robust inference on messy longitudinal clinical data. It will yield a theoretical and empirical guide for trading off sequence modeling complexity against data-cleaning effort. The resulting software will enable more reliable personalized survival predictions from real-world data sources.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-optimization and dynamic trade-off between two typically separate algorithmic concerns—scalability to long sequences and robustness to noise—through a principled, diagnostic-driven resource allocation model, rather than treating them as independent problems.

proposals.title: Efficient Causal Survival Modeling for Long-Horizon Clinical Time Series via Structured State Space and Selective Attention
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from long, irregularly sampled clinical time series (e.g., longitudinal EHR data) is computationally prohibitive with existing RNN- or Transformer-based models. The quadratic complexity of full self-attention and the vanishing gradients in long RNNs limit scalability, hindering the application of causal survival methods to cohorts with rich, long-term patient histories. This bottleneck prevents the discovery of nuanced, time-varying treatment effect dynamics critical for personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating structured state space sequence models (S4) with a causal, selective attention mechanism for key time points (e.g., treatment administration, observed events), we can achieve near-linear scaling in sequence length while maintaining or improving the accuracy of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel deep survival architecture. The core sequence encoder will be an S4 layer, known for its efficient long-range dependency modeling, to process the long input covariate histories. For causal inference, we will augment this with a gated attention module that selectively attends to time steps proximal to treatment assignments and observed outcomes, informed by the potential outcomes framework. This hybrid model will be trained using a loss function combining the time-to-event likelihood (handling censoring) with a targeted regularization term for robust heterogeneous effect estimation, building on Shalit's previous work with recursively imputed trees and doubly robust estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, open-source software tool for causal survival analysis on long sequences, directly enabling research on long-term treatment effect heterogeneity. It will establish a new benchmark for computational efficiency in this domain, validated on real-world IVF (as in prior work) and publicly available long-term EHR datasets like MIMIC-IV.
proposals.novelty: While S4 models have shown promise in long sequence modeling, their application to causal inference for survival outcomes, combined with a principled, selective attention mechanism for identifying key causal time windows, is novel. This moves beyond simply applying off-the-shelf sequence models to a tailored architecture for causal identification.

proposals.title: Body-Fitted Variational Inference: A Coordinate Transform Framework for Flexible Posterior Approximation in Causal Survival Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, Bayesian inference with complex, high-dimensional posteriors is crucial for uncertainty quantification. Standard variational inference (VI) often fails with simple mean-field approximations, which cannot capture posterior dependencies or multi-modality, leading to biased uncertainty estimates. This is particularly acute in survival analysis with time-to-event outcomes and hidden confounders, where model misspecification can invalidate causal conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing normalizing flows as learned, invertible coordinate transforms—analogous to body-fitted grids in CFD—will enable the construction of variational families rich enough to accurately approximate the complex posteriors arising in causal survival models, thereby improving the fidelity of treatment effect estimation and uncertainty quantification.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a simple base distribution (e.g., isotropic Gaussian) is transformed via a conditional normalizing flow (e.g., a RealNVP or autoregressive flow architecture) parameterized by the observed data. This flow acts as the 'grid transformation,' mapping the simple domain to the complex 'physical' posterior. We will integrate this into a variational inference scheme for models involving survival outcomes, latent variables (e.g., hidden covariates), and treatment assignment, deriving the corresponding evidence lower bound (ELBO) and optimization procedure. The method will be benchmarked on synthetic and real-world clinical datasets against standard VI and MCMC baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, geometrically-inspired framework for building expressive variational approximations, directly applicable to causal survival analysis. It will yield a practical tool for researchers to obtain more accurate posterior distributions for treatment effects and latent states in settings with censoring and unmeasured confounding, enhancing the reliability of clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While normalizing flows are known in deep learning, the novel conceptual analogy to CFD grid generation provides a fresh, structured motivation for their application in clinical causal inference, moving beyond their typical use as generative models. This bridges disparate fields to address a core limitation in Bayesian methods for healthcare.

proposals.title: Flexible Variational Inference for Causal Survival Analysis with Normalizing Flows
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but is complicated by censoring and complex confounding. Pre-2026 variational inference (VI) methods for such models often use overly simplistic variational posteriors (e.g., mean-field Gaussians), which can bias estimates of uncertainty and treatment effect heterogeneity. More expressive posteriors are needed to capture complex dependencies between latent variables, such as hidden confounders and individual-level frailties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using normalizing flows to parameterize the variational posterior in causal survival models will yield a more accurate approximation of the true posterior, leading to improved estimation of treatment effect heterogeneity and better uncertainty quantification compared to standard VI baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured variational autoencoder framework for time-to-event outcomes with treatment assignment. The variational posterior over latent confounders and individual effects will be modeled using a conditional normalizing flow (e.g., a RealNVP or MAF architecture), where the flow's base distribution and transformation parameters are outputs of an encoder network. This flexible posterior will be integrated into a survival likelihood that handles censoring. We will adapt the causal inference objectives from Shalit's prior work (e.g., 'Recursively Imputed Trees') to this probabilistic framework, enabling principled estimation of conditional average treatment effects (CATE) in survival settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, more expressive class of variational models for causal survival analysis, improving the fidelity of posterior approximations in the presence of complex latent structure. It will provide a methodological advance in combining normalizing flows with causal and survival objectives, and deliver a practical software tool for more reliable HTE estimation from observational clinical data.
proposals.novelty: While normalizing flows are established for density estimation, their application to structure the variational posterior in *causal survival models* is novel. This moves beyond using flows for general-purpose VI to address a specific, high-stakes inference problem where posterior accuracy directly impacts clinical conclusions.

proposals.title: Resource-Aware, Adaptive Inference for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data with censoring is a core challenge in clinical prediction. Existing methods, like Bayesian causal forests or deep survival models, often rely on a single inference paradigm (e.g., variational inference (VI) or particle-based methods), which can be inefficient. VI may underfit complex posteriors, while particle methods are computationally prohibitive for large-scale clinical datasets, limiting their practical utility in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned, adaptive gating mechanism that dynamically routes inference subtasks between computationally cheap VI and high-fidelity particle methods, based on local posterior complexity and a computational budget, will yield more sample-efficient and robust estimates of HTEs in survival models than static inference strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework with a neural scheduler that takes as input local gradient statistics and latent representations from a treatment-effect model (e.g., a deep Cox model or a survival forest). This scheduler, trained via reinforcement learning with a reward balancing approximation error and computational cost, will output a gating decision. For 'simple' regions, it will use fast VI; for 'complex' regions (e.g., near decision boundaries or with high uncertainty), it will allocate particles from a sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) sampler. The entire system—scheduler, treatment effect model, and inference networks—will be co-trained end-to-end on biased offline clinical data, using pre-2026 doubly robust or IPTW-based objectives to handle confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, resource-aware inference algorithm that offers robust performance guarantees for HTE estimation in survival analysis. It will directly enable more accurate and computationally feasible personalization of treatment timing and selection from observational clinical data. We will release benchmarks comparing our method to state-of-the-art causal survival forests and deep latent variable models.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the *learned, adaptive routing* between fundamentally different inference paradigms (VI and particle methods) based on a dynamic assessment of local posterior geometry and explicit resource constraints, which is absent from prior hybrid inference schemes. This moves beyond simple ensemble or static switching strategies.

proposals.title: Sequential Importance Resampling for Variational Particle Refinement: A Hybrid Method for Robust Bayesian Inference in Clinical Prediction Models
proposals.motivation: Variational inference (VI) scales Bayesian inference but can underestimate posterior variance and miss modes, which is critical for reliable uncertainty quantification in clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation. Particle-based methods offer better approximations but are computationally expensive. A hybrid approach that efficiently combines their strengths is needed for robust, high-dimensional inference in healthcare settings with censored or time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: By dynamically weighting and resampling an ensemble of variational distributions during optimization—inspired by ensemble data assimilation—the proposed method will produce more accurate and diverse posterior approximations than standard VI, particularly for multi-modal or skewed posteriors common in heterogeneous treatment effect models.
proposals.method: We will maintain an ensemble of Gaussian variational particles, each parameterized by a mean and covariance. During stochastic gradient descent, we compute importance weights for each particle using the exact likelihood of a mini-batch relative to its variational approximation. A systematic resampling step, akin to Sequential Monte Carlo, will then prune low-weight particles and duplicate high-weight ones, redistributing optimization effort. This core algorithm will be integrated into a survival analysis framework to handle censored time-to-event data, evaluating performance on benchmarks for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, computationally tractable algorithm that mitigates the mode-seeking failure of VI while retaining its scalability. It will deliver a practical tool for more reliable uncertainty estimation in causal inference and survival prediction tasks, directly applicable to clinical decision support. The method will be benchmarked against existing VI and particle-based approaches.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the adaptation of ensemble forecasting resampling techniques to guide variational optimization, creating a novel feedback loop between particle diversification and gradient-based parameter updates. This specific synthesis of sequential Monte Carlo principles with stochastic variational inference is unexplored in pre-2026 machine learning.

proposals.title: Efficient Hybrid Attention-State Inference for Scalable Causal Estimation with Time-to-Event Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data, such as in clinical trials or electronic health records, requires scalable latent variable models to handle complex, high-dimensional patient histories. Standard attention-based inference networks suffer from a quadratic computational bottleneck, making them infeasible for long patient sequences or large-scale datasets. This limits the practical application of flexible causal models in clinical settings where both long-range dependencies and computational efficiency are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid inference architecture, combining a linear-complexity efficient attention mechanism for global context with a lightweight parallel iterative refinement module (e.g., a structured state-space model), will enable scalable and accurate inference for latent variable models on long-sequence time-to-event data without sacrificing the ability to model complex dependencies.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel inference network where a Performer or Linformer block provides linear-time global context from the full input sequence (patient covariates and treatment history). This context vector will condition a parallel, iterative module—specifically a linear state-space model (SSM) like S4 or a small MLP—that refines the latent variables (e.g., latent patient frailty or treatment effect modifiers).
