The model will be trained via variational inference, with the objective tailored for survival analysis, integrating the scalable inference network into a causal survival framework like the one in Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable inference method for latent variable models applied to large-scale, long-sequence observational data, directly enabling more practical causal survival analysis. It will contribute a new benchmark for efficient latent variable inference in clinical prediction tasks. The resulting software will allow researchers to fit more expressive models to real-world healthcare datasets where sequence length is a constraint.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific hybrid design for *inference networks* in latent variable models, marrying efficient transformers with lightweight iterative refiners, which is distinct from using these architectures solely for primary sequence modeling. This approach is non-obvious for causal survival settings, where inference efficiency is paramount for repeated estimation calls during validation and sensitivity analysis.

proposals.title: A Global Workspace Inference Network for Causal Survival Analysis with Complex, Time-Varying Confounding
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data with complex, long-range dependencies in time-varying confounders remains a major challenge in clinical prediction. Standard RNN-based inference networks in variational models struggle to integrate information across distant time steps, potentially leading to biased posterior estimates of latent patient states and treatment effects. This limits the reliability of personalized survival predictions and causal effect estimation from observational clinical data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an inference network architecture based on Global Workspace Theory, using multi-head attention to integrate information from specialized modules, will more accurately model long-range dependencies in patient trajectories, leading to improved estimation of latent covariates and, consequently, more precise and robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a Global Workspace Inference Network (GWIN) for a causal survival model. Specialized neural modules will encode different aspects of observed patient history (e.g., treatments, vitals, lab results) into queries, keys, and values. These will be projected into a shared global workspace where a multi-head attention mechanism performs dynamic, context-aware integration across all time steps and data modalities. This integrated representation will parameterize the variational posterior over a latent disease progression state, which is then used in a structured causal survival model (e.g., a treatment-specific Cox proportional hazards model) for estimating individualized survival curves and conditional average treatment effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired architecture for variational inference in time-series models, specifically tailored for clinical applications. It will provide a new methodological tool for handling long-range dependencies in offline, biased observational data, advancing the state-of-the-art in causal survival analysis. We will demonstrate its utility on benchmarks and a real-world clinical dataset involving time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While attention mechanisms are established, their structured use as a global workspace for integrating modular, specialized encoders within a causal inference network for survival analysis is novel. This directly translates a cognitive neuroscience theory of consciousness into a practical engineering solution for a core problem in machine learning for healthcare.

proposals.title: ATTENTA: Attention-Guided Symbolic Inference for Interpretable Latent Dynamics in Clinical Time-to-Event Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, predicting time-to-event outcomes like disease progression or treatment failure requires models that capture complex, long-range dependencies in patient trajectories. While deep learning excels at prediction, its black-box nature limits clinical trust and actionable insight. Conversely, interpretable models like symbolic regression often fail to scale to the high-dimensional, irregularly sampled data common in electronic health records.
proposals.hypothesis: By using an attention mechanism to explicitly identify the relevant historical observations and their temporal lags, we can constrain a symbolic regression search to produce compact, interpretable dynamical equations for latent patient states, thereby achieving both high predictive accuracy and causal, clinically meaningful interpretability.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured variational inference network. A transformer-based encoder will process an input sequence of clinical observations and treatments, outputting attention weights over past time steps. These weights will gate the inputs to a latent variable, creating a sparse, symbolic-ready representation. A symbolic regression module (e.g., based on PySR or a genetic algorithm) will then search for mathematical expressions (e.g., ODEs or difference equations) governing the latent dynamics, using the attention weights as a prior to penalize terms from non-attended inputs. The model will be trained end-to-end on censored survival data using a variational lower bound combined with a survival likelihood.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for interpretable dynamic modeling of clinical time-series, directly linking attention-based relevance to symbolic equations. It will advance the field of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in survival analysis by producing interpretable dynamical models of patient trajectories under intervention. The resulting software will bridge deep sequential learning and symbolic AI for clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the tight, trainable coupling between the attention mechanism and the symbolic regression search space, forcing interpretability to be grounded in the model's actual temporal reasoning. This moves beyond post-hoc attribution on black-box models or unguided symbolic search.

proposals.title: Attention-based Inference Networks for Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation with Long-range Clinical Covariates
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation, patient histories often contain long sequences of irregularly sampled measurements. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), commonly used in inference networks for models like Deep Survival Machines or CEVAEs, struggle with long-range dependencies and can be computationally inefficient. This limits the ability to model complex temporal interactions in electronic health records (EHRs) that are crucial for accurate personalized predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing RNN-based encoders in survival and causal inference architectures with self-attention mechanisms will improve the modeling of long-range dependencies in clinical time series, leading to more accurate and robust estimates of survival functions and individualized treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel inference network architecture where the sequential encoder is based on a Transformer or a temporal self-attention module, potentially using pre-2026 adaptations like the Temporal Fusion Transformer or time-aware positional encodings. This will be integrated into established frameworks relevant to Shalit's work, such as Deep Survival Machines for time-to-event outcomes and Causal Effect Variational Autoencoders (CEVAE) for HTE estimation. We will benchmark against RNN-based baselines (e.g., LSTMs, GRUs) on real-world clinical datasets with long patient trajectories, evaluating performance via time-dependent C-index for survival and Precision in Estimation of Heterogeneous Effects (PEHE) for HTE.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, scalable method for building inference networks over long clinical histories, directly applicable to survival and causal prediction tasks. It will yield a publicly available benchmark comparing attention-based and RNN-based encoders in the clinical ML domain. The results will offer practical guidance on architecture choice for handling long-range dependencies in observational healthcare data.
proposals.novelty: While attention is widely used in prediction models, its systematic integration and evaluation specifically within the inference networks of deep latent variable models for survival and causal inference is not well explored. This proposal tailors the attention mechanism to the specific challenges of clinical time series (irregular sampling, censoring) within Shalit's established methodological frameworks.

proposals.title: Diagnostic-Driven Adaptive Interleaving of Variational Inference and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Causal Survival Models with Hidden Confounding
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data with censoring and potential hidden confounding is a core challenge in clinical prediction. Pre-2026, methods like recursively imputed trees and targeted maximum likelihood estimation offer solutions, but Bayesian approaches with flexible, multi-modal priors for hidden covariates remain computationally intractable. Existing variational inference (VI) methods are fast but may yield biased posteriors, while exact samplers like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) are too slow for routine clinical use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive, diagnostic-driven system that interleaves VI and HMC can guarantee exact posterior sampling for complex models (e.g., survival models with latent variables) with computational efficiency rivaling standalone VI, by using short HMC runs to automatically detect and correct variational approximation failures.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that iterates between a VI phase and a diagnostic HMC phase. The VI phase uses a flexible variational family (e.g., normalizing flows or mixture distributions). The diagnostic phase runs multiple short, non-convergent HMC chains initialized from the VI distribution to compute discrepancy metrics (e.g., effective sample size per gradient evaluation, or a kernelized Stein discrepancy). These metrics trigger an expansion of the variational family (adding mixture components) or adjustment of its parameters. The refined VI distribution then provides tailored mass matrices and initial points for a final, efficient production HMC run, ensuring convergence to the true posterior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, automated inference engine for complex Bayesian causal survival models, directly applicable to problems like evaluating heterogeneous effects of clinical interventions. It contributes a principled framework for coupling approximate and exact inference, ensuring statistical reliability without sacrificing computational practicality for clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing HMC not as a primary sampler, but as a diagnostic tool to guide the adaptive complexity of the variational approximation, creating a closed-loop system that guarantees exactness—a non-obvious synergy beyond existing static VI-HMC hybrids.

proposals.title: Annealed Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Refining Variational Approximations in Causal Survival Models
proposals.motivation: Variational Inference (VI) is crucial for scaling Bayesian inference in high-dimensional clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, but its approximations can be biased, especially for complex posteriors like those in survival analysis with hidden confounders. This bias can lead to incorrect uncertainty quantification, undermining reliable causal conclusions from observational data. A robust, automated method to refine a VI approximation into a faithful posterior is needed for trustworthy clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampler, initialized with a high-uncertainty (high-'temperature') state informed by a VI solution and then annealed, will more efficiently and accurately sample the true posterior than standard VI or a naively initialized MCMC, particularly for models with heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm where a fast mean-field VI fit provides initial parameters and a covariance estimate. This guides the initialization of an HMC sampler with a momentum distribution variance scaled by a temperature parameter T >> 1. Sampling begins at high T, encouraging exploration beyond the VI basin. Using a geometric cooling schedule, T is gradually reduced to 1, focusing the chain on the true posterior. We will implement this within a Pyro/NumPyro framework and test it on benchmark survival models with censored data and hidden covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated pipeline for posterior refinement, increasing the reliability of Bayesian causal and survival models. It will yield a practical algorithm that combines VI's speed with HMC's asymptotic exactness, directly improving uncertainty estimates for heterogeneous treatment effects. This enhances methodological rigor for pre-2026 clinical prediction research.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the structured, annealing-based *coupling* of VI and HMC, where VI's output actively configures the HMC's exploration phase, rather than merely providing a starting point. This adapts a molecular dynamics paradigm for stochastic optimization into a Bayesian refinement protocol.

proposals.title: Assumption-Aware Causal Inference via Validated Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Survival and Treatment Effect Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and causal inference for treatment effects, models often rely on strong assumptions (e.g., normality of random effects, conditional independence, correct censoring mechanisms) to remain tractable. Pre-2026, methods like variational inference (VI) offer speed but yield approximate posteriors, while exact samplers like HMC are computationally prohibitive for full models. There is a critical need for a principled, efficient method that diagnoses specific assumption violations from available data and applies exact corrections only where necessary, ensuring reliable inference for clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: That a formal diagnostic layer, using hypothesis tests on combined observational and experimental data, can reliably identify subspaces of a model's parameter space where core assumptions are violated, and that a subsequent, targeted HMC step can efficiently correct the posterior only in those subspaces, yielding a validated, assumption-aware posterior.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified pipeline. First, for a given model (e.g., a survival model with hidden covariates or a heterogeneous treatment effect model), we implement a battery of targeted statistical tests (e.g., tests for residual normality, conditional independence tests using knockoffs) on the observed data. The test outcomes parameterize a corrective HMC kernel, which is initialized from the VI solution but constrained to sample only the affected parameters or latent variables. This selective sampling uses a modified Hamiltonian where the potential energy is adjusted based on the diagnostic weights, focusing computational effort on necessary corrections while leaving well-specified subspaces unchanged.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodology for robust, computationally efficient Bayesian inference in clinical settings, directly linking model validation to posterior refinement. It will yield a software tool that outputs not just a posterior, but a quantitative validation report, enhancing trust in causal estimates of treatment effects and survival predictions. This bridges the gap between fast approximation and exact but costly inference in a principled way.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration lies in using formal diagnostic tests not just for model criticism, but to actively parameterize and constrain a subsequent exact sampling step. This moves beyond using HMC as a blanket post-VI refinement or running tests in isolation, creating a closed-loop, assumption-aware inference engine.

proposals.title: Exact Posterior Sampling for Causal Survival Analysis via Variational Inference Refined with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, variational inference (VI) enables scalable Bayesian inference for complex models with high-dimensional covariates. However, the approximate posteriors from VI can be biased, especially for skewed or multi-modal distributions, leading to unreliable uncertainty quantification for heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event data. Accurate posterior uncertainty is critical for clinical decision-making, where overconfident or biased intervals could misguide treatment policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a small number of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) steps, initialized from a VI-approximated posterior, will efficiently produce samples from the exact posterior, correcting for VI's approximation errors and yielding more reliable credible intervals for causal survival parameters without the computational cost of full MCMC from scratch.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm for models like Bayesian Cox proportional hazards or accelerated failure time models with heterogeneous treatment effects. First, a mean-field or structured variational distribution will be fit using stochastic gradient descent. Second, we will use this variational distribution to initialize multiple HMC chains, employing the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) for a limited number of steps to sample from the exact posterior. The method will be evaluated on benchmarks for causal survival analysis, including semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical covariates, measuring the fidelity of posterior credible intervals and treatment effect estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, hybrid inference tool that marries the scalability of VI with the accuracy of MCMC, specifically tailored for causal survival models. It will deliver a methodological advance for obtaining trustworthy uncertainty estimates in treatment effect prediction, directly impacting clinical research where reliable confidence intervals are paramount. We will release open-source software integrating with existing Pyro or Stan ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While HMC and VI are established, their sequential combination as a corrective refinement step is not standard in causal inference for survival outcomes. The novelty lies in tailoring this hybrid scheme to the specific challenges of biased, censored time-to-event data with hidden confounding, leveraging Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees and biased offline data.

proposals.title: Causal Discovery of Interpretable Disease Progression Models from High-Dimensional Clinical Time Series
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction, learning interpretable dynamical models from high-dimensional, censored patient data (e.g., EHR time series) is crucial for understanding heterogeneous treatment effects and disease trajectories. Current methods, like survival forests or black-box neural ODEs, often lack the dimensional consistency and physical plausibility needed for robust, generalizable, and clinically trustworthy models.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding physics-informed constraints (dimensional homogeneity, conservation laws) directly into the symbolic discovery pipeline, we can learn interpretable dynamical equations that are more robust to distribution shift and more causally meaningful than unconstrained models, especially when estimating individualized treatment effects on time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend a neural encoder to learn a low-dimensional state representation from irregular clinical observations. A physics-constraint module, implemented as a differentiable layer, will enforce dimensional homogeneity and optional conservation laws (e.g., a patient's 'health energy') on this latent space. We will then perform symbolic regression via Pareto-optimized genetic programming (e.g., using PySR) strictly within this constrained hypothesis space, balancing fit to time-to-event data (handling censoring via a survival loss), complexity, and a physics-compliance score. The final model will be a symbolic ODE system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for discovering robust, interpretable dynamical systems from biased, high-dimensional observational data. It will advance the field of clinical causal inference by yielding models whose structure is physically plausible, enhancing out-of-distribution generalization and enabling clearer mechanistic interpretation of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival analysis.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the hard integration of physics-based constraints (dimensional analysis, conservation) into the symbolic search loop itself, rather than as a post-hoc regularizer, ensuring the discovered equations are inherently physically consistent. This bridges causal inference, symbolic AI, and physics-informed machine learning in a novel way for clinical applications.

proposals.title: Grammar-Guided Neural-Symbolic Models for Interpretable Causal Dynamics in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, models must be both accurate and interpretable to inform decisions. Pre-2026, methods like neural networks excel at prediction but are black boxes, while symbolic regression offers interpretable dynamics but struggles with high-dimensional, censored data. Bridging this gap is crucial for discovering actionable, mechanistic insights from complex patient trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid model, where a neural network learns to bias a probabilistic context-free grammar for symbolic regression, will discover more accurate and interpretable representations of underlying survival dynamics and heterogeneous treatment effects than purely neural or symbolic methods alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework integrating a neural network with Grammatical Evolution (GE). The neural network, processing time-to-event data with potential hidden confounders, will output parameters for a probabilistic grammar over mathematical expressions (e.g., ODEs for hazard functions). This grammar will then constrain a GE-based symbolic regression search, using techniques like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees to handle censoring. The discovered symbolic expressions will provide interpretable transition functions, and their fitness will provide a learning signal to refine the neural network's grammar proposals.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel method for interpretable dynamic modeling in survival and causal inference, merging the flexibility of deep learning with the parsimony of symbolic models. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating interpretability in treatment effect estimation and provide a tool for generating clinically plausible hypotheses about disease progression mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior neural-symbolic works that use networks to evaluate symbols, our method uses the network to dynamically structure the symbolic search space via a learnable grammar. This integrates data-driven bias directly into the evolutionary algorithm's construction rules, a novel synergy for clinical time-to-event analysis.

proposals.title: Interpretable Causal Dynamics for Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation via Neural-Symbolic Regression
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects and survival dynamics from observational clinical data is crucial for personalized medicine, but state-of-the-art models like neural networks are often black boxes. This lack of interpretability hinders clinical trust and scientific discovery, especially when trying to understand the underlying transition dynamics of a patient's state over time. Pre-2026, symbolic regression offers interpretability but struggles with the high-dimensional, censored, and biased data common in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating symbolic regression with neural network feature learning will yield interpretable, parsimonious transition functions for dynamic systems without sacrificing the predictive accuracy needed for robust causal effect estimation in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture where a neural network encoder learns a low-dimensional representation from high-dimensional, time-varying covariates. This representation will serve as input to a symbolic regression module (e.g., based on genetic programming or AI Feynman) tasked with discovering analytic expressions for state transitions and hazard functions. The model will be trained end-to-end on biased offline data, leveraging Shalit's prior work on survival trees and causal inference to handle censoring and confounding, ensuring the discovered dynamics are causally regularized.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new method for interpretable dynamic modeling that bridges the performance of deep learning with the transparency of symbolic models. It will provide clinicians with understandable equations describing disease or treatment response trajectories, directly contributing to the fields of interpretable machine learning and causal inference for healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While neural networks and symbolic regression have been explored separately, their tight integration for discovering interpretable *transition functions* in the context of causal survival analysis with time-varying confounding is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc explanation of black-box predictions to the direct discovery of mechanistic-like equations from complex clinical data.

proposals.title: Meta-Adaptive Feature Reconfiguration for Few-Shot Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) and survival functions from limited clinical data is a core challenge in personalized medicine. Standard methods require large datasets, while few-shot learning approaches often underfit due to insufficient model capacity. This gap limits the application of precision medicine to rare conditions or novel treatments where only a handful of relevant patient sequences are available.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned adapter network, trained on a curriculum of diverse sequential prediction tasks, can rapidly reconfigure features from a pre-trained, high-capacity backbone model to enable accurate few-shot estimation of time-to-event outcomes and conditional average treatment effects (CATE).
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system: 1) a pre-trained transformer or deep survival model (e.g., DeepHit or a recurrent network) as a fixed feature extractor, trained on large-scale electronic health records for general sequence representation. 2) A small, meta-learned neural adapter (e.g., a hypernetwork or a set of modulatory layers) will be optimized using Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) on a synthetic and real-world curriculum of few-shot tasks. These tasks will involve predicting survival curves and HTEs from sequences with censored data, forcing the adapter to learn general reconfiguration strategies for novel treatments or patient subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for sample-efficient causal and survival analysis from sequential data, directly applicable to clinical studies with small cohorts. It will extend the meta-learning and adapter paradigm into the domain of censored outcome prediction and heterogeneous effect estimation, creating a new benchmark for few-shot sequential decision support.
proposals.novelty: While adapters and meta-learning exist in vision/NLP, their application to reconfigure features for few-shot causal survival analysis is novel. The integration of a robust, pre-trained clinical backbone with a task-curriculum for learning adaptation rules specifically for treatment effect estimation moves beyond standard transfer learning.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Prediction from Sparse, Irregularly Sampled Clinical Trajectories via Compressed Sensing Priors
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient monitoring often yields sparse, irregularly sampled time-series data (e.g., lab values, vital signs), especially in outpatient or resource-limited contexts. This sparsity severely limits the application of standard sequential models for predicting time-to-event outcomes like disease progression or treatment failure, which require rich temporal context. Pre-2026 methods for handling such data often rely on strong assumptions about missingness or require extensive imputation, which can bias causal effect estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a patient's underlying clinical trajectory is compressible in a learned latent space, and that imposing a compressed sensing-inspired sparsity prior on this representation will enable accurate reconstruction of the full trajectory and robust estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects from only a few, irregularly sampled observations per patient.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep learning architecture combining a set encoder for irregular observations with a recurrent or transformer-based decoder. The model will be trained with a novel objective that enforces a low-rank or sparse code in a bottleneck layer, analogous to the sensing matrix in compressed sensing. We will adapt the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to use these reconstructed trajectories as enriched covariates for estimating conditional average treatment effects in survival outcomes, handling censoring explicitly. Evaluation will use benchmarks for offline RL with biased data to simulate imperfect observational settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new method for few-shot sequential learning tailored to clinical data sparsity, directly applicable to causal survival analysis. It will provide a principled, model-based alternative to ad-hoc imputation, potentially reducing bias in treatment effect estimation from real-world data. We will release a benchmark for evaluating causal estimators on sparsely sampled temporal data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of compressed sensing principles—exploiting inherent compressibility—into a causal inference pipeline for survival outcomes, moving beyond simple interpolation to structured reconstruction that benefits downstream effect estimation. This bridges signal processing and causal machine learning in a non-obvious way for clinical applications.

proposals.title: Causal Few-Shot Adaptation of Hidden Markov Models with Irregular State Supervision for Clinical Deterioration Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, predicting time-to-event outcomes like patient deterioration from sparse, irregularly sampled data is a core challenge. Models must adapt to new patient cohorts or disease subtypes with very few labeled examples, a classic few-shot learning problem. However, existing methods often fail to leverage the occasional, high-fidelity measurements (e.g., a definitive lab test) that provide partial glimpses of a patient's true latent health state, which could serve as powerful anchors for rapid adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential model trained with a novel objective that strategically integrates sparse, irregularly observed ground-truth states with censored time-series data will achieve significantly better few-shot adaptation performance in predicting heterogeneous treatment effects on survival outcomes than models using either data source alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep Hidden Markov Model (HMM) variant where the emission and transition networks are parameterized by neural networks. The core innovation is a hybrid, multi-task objective function. It combines a standard evidence lower bound (ELBO) on the fully observed sequences with a separate, strategically weighted maximum likelihood loss for the irregularly observed latent state snapshots. This framework will be integrated with a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) outer loop to enable few-shot adaptation to new tasks, and we will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees method to estimate conditional average treatment effects (CATE) on adapted model outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new methodological framework for few-shot learning in sequential decision-making under partial observability, directly applicable to clinical prognostication. It will provide a principled way to fuse high-quality but sparse supervision with abundant but noisy temporal data. We will release a benchmark for evaluating such methods on biased offline clinical datasets and imperfect physiological simulators.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning for sequences and learning with partial state information exist separately, their fusion via a probabilistically grounded, hybrid objective for rapid causal adaptation in survival settings is novel. This moves beyond simple fine-tuning or data augmentation by using the state snapshots as direct constraints on the latent dynamics during adaptation.

proposals.title: Causal Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects from Short Survival Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data is critical in clinical decision-making, but often relies on large-scale observational cohorts with long follow-up. In many real-world settings, such as rare diseases or novel interventions, only short, sparse, or few-shot sequential data are available, severely limiting the applicability of existing survival and causal methods. This gap hinders personalized treatment recommendations when data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which leverages shared structure across related but limited patient sequences, can enable robust estimation of conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for survival outcomes from few-shot data, outperforming methods that treat each short sequence independently.
proposals.method: We will develop a model combining a temporal convolutional network (TCN) or a compact transformer encoder to process short, irregularly sampled sequences of pre-treatment covariates and early outcomes. This encoder will be meta-trained across many simulated and semi-synthetic tasks, generated from pre-2026 benchmarks like the SUPPORT dataset, to learn a prior for HTE estimation. The adaptation to a new, few-shot task will use a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) scheme, fine-tuning the encoder's final layers with a doubly-robust or R-learner loss tailored for censored survival outcomes, building on Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical method for few-shot causal inference in survival analysis, expanding the reach of personalized medicine to data-scarce contexts. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating HTE models under data limitation and offer theoretical insights into the sample complexity of identifying treatment effects from short sequences.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of meta-learning principles specifically for the causal task of HTE estimation in survival settings, moving beyond standard prediction. It addresses the joint challenge of sequence brevity, censorship, and confounding with a unified, task-adaptive framework, which is underexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Learning Sparse Latent Causal Graphs for Efficient Estimation of Heterogeneous Time-to-Event Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from high-dimensional, time-to-event data is critical for personalized medicine but remains statistically and computationally challenging. Standard high-dimensional models often suffer from the curse of dimensionality, leading to poor generalization, while existing causal survival forests struggle with complex, structured latent confounders. A method that imposes structured sparsity on high-dimensional latent spaces could enable tractable and efficient inference from censored observational data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning a sparse dependency graph over high-dimensional latent variables and using adaptive, locally low-dimensional projections, we can achieve statistically efficient and computationally tractable estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes, even with many potential latent confounders.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework where the latent prior is a learnable, sparse Gaussian graphical model, enforcing that only a small subset of latents interact for any given patient. The encoder will produce adaptive, patient-specific masks to project onto locally relevant low-dimensional manifolds. For causal estimation, we will integrate this structured latent model with a treatment-specific survival outcome head, building on principles from doubly robust estimation and adapting the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) estimator to use the learned latent representations as deconfounded covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable framework for HTE estimation in survival analysis that explicitly models structured high-dimensional latent confounding. It will provide a new benchmark method for offline evaluation of treatment policies from biased observational data, directly applicable to clinical settings like IVF treatment optimization as studied in prior work.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a learnable sparse graphical model within the latent space of a deep survival model to induce adaptive sparsity, moving beyond simple L1 penalties or fixed latent dimensions. This structured approach to combating dimensionality for causal survival estimation is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Sparse Random Projection for Scalable Inference in High-Dimensional Hidden Markov Models of Clinical Trajectories
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, patient trajectories are modeled with high-dimensional latent states (e.g., evolving physiology, disease subtypes). Standard inference in Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) with many latent dimensions suffers from a computational curse of dimensionality, limiting their use with rich, time-varying covariates. Efficient methods are needed to scale these models to realistic clinical data without sacrificing the ability to capture complex heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: That sparse random projections, inspired by efficient coding in biological neural systems, can compress high-dimensional observational data for latent state inference while provably preserving distances critical for state discrimination, enabling accurate and scalable estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel HMM framework where high-dimensional observations at each time step are projected via a sparse, random binary matrix onto a lower-dimensional space. Inference (using a modified Baum-Welch algorithm) will be performed in this compressed space, leveraging Johnson-Lindenstrauss guarantees. For clinical application, we will integrate this with a Cox proportional hazards model on the inferred latent states to estimate time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects, building on prior work with recursively imputed survival trees.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a computationally tractable method for latent state inference in high-dimensional clinical time series, directly enabling more granular estimation of dynamic treatment effects from observational data. It contributes a new bridge between computational neuroscience-inspired dimensionality reduction and causal machine learning for healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While random projections are known in machine learning, their application with sparsity constraints for inference in structured latent variable models in clinical settings is novel. The core innovation is adapting a principle of neural coding—sparse, efficient representation—to solve a scaling bottleneck in causal inference with hidden states.

proposals.title: Structured Variational Inference for High-Dimensional Survival Models with Latent Confounders
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, unmeasured confounding is a critical threat to validity. Modern high-dimensional data (e.g., genomics, EHRs) allow for modeling confounders as high-dimensional latent states, but standard variational inference (VI) imposes restrictive Gaussian priors, failing to capture sparsity or multi-modality inherent in biological systems. This mismatch limits the realism and reliability of causal survival models in personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, non-Gaussian process prior (e.g., a spike-and-slab process or a deep generative prior) combined with a tailored variational family that enforces conditional independence structures from the causal model will enable scalable and accurate inference of high-dimensional latent confounders in survival analysis.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of hidden variable causal survival models by placing a structured, non-Gaussian process prior over the high-dimensional latent confounders. To achieve scalable inference, we will develop a structured variational family where the variational distribution factorizes according to the conditional independencies implied by the causal graph (e.g., between latent confounders, treatments, and observed covariates). This will be combined with stochastic gradient MCMC or normalizing flows to approximate the posterior. We will benchmark performance using semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts (e.g., MIMIC-IV) and the "Recursively Imputed Trees" framework for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological tool for robust estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes when high-dimensional latent confounding is suspected. It will advance the field of causal inference by bridging flexible, structured Bayesian nonparametrics with scalable computational techniques, directly applicable to clinical research questions like those in fertility treatment studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of structured, non-Gaussian process priors for high-dimensional latent states with computationally efficient, causality-informed variational approximations, moving beyond generic Gaussian latent variable models commonly used in pre-2026 variational causal inference.

proposals.title: High-Dimensional Latent State Inference for Causal Survival Analysis with Variational Autoencoders and Neural ODEs
proposals.motivation: Causal inference and survival analysis in clinical settings often rely on latent variable models to account for unmeasured confounding, such as patient physiology or disease progression. Pre-2026 methods, including hidden Markov models and structural models with low-dimensional latent states, struggle to capture the complexity of real-world clinical trajectories, limiting their accuracy and scalability to high-dimensional, time-varying data like EHRs or imaging biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining amortized variational inference with structured latent dynamics can efficiently infer high-dimensional, clinically interpretable latent states from censored time-to-event data, leading to more accurate estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Trees framework by replacing its low-dimensional latent state model. A variational autoencoder (VAE) with a convolutional or transformer-based encoder will map high-dimensional observed covariates (e.g., sequential lab values, notes) to a structured latent space. The temporal evolution of latent states will be governed by a neural ordinary differential equation (Neural ODE), regularized for smoothness. The survival and treatment effect models will be conditioned on these inferred trajectories, with optimization via the evidence lower bound (ELBO) adapted for right-censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, principled method for performing causal survival analysis with complex, high-dimensional observational data. It will enable the discovery of latent patient subtypes and more precise estimation of individualized treatment effects, directly advancing personalized medicine. We will release benchmark datasets and code to facilitate future research.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs and Neural ODEs were known pre-2026, their integration into a causal survival framework for high-dimensional latent state inference is novel. This moves beyond simple latent confounders to model rich, dynamic patient representations that are both statistically identified and clinically actionable.

proposals.title: Dynamic Memory for Streaming Survival Prediction: A Causal Framework with Explicit Forgetting Control
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient data streams are non-stationary due to evolving treatment protocols, disease variants, and hospital practices. This causes catastrophic forgetting in standard survival models, degrading predictions for past patient cohorts and biasing estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) over time. A method to learn continuously from such streams while preserving stable knowledge is critical for reliable decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic dual-memory architecture, which explicitly separates a stable consolidated model from a plastic working memory and uses distributional shift detection to gate updates, will enable more accurate and robust streaming survival prediction and HTE estimation by mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural survival model with a Dynamic Memory Architecture (DMA). The stable memory will be a Cox-based or deep survival model updated via elastic weight consolidation (EWC) or gradient episodic memory (GEM). The plastic memory will be a small buffer for recent data. A gating network, trained on features from a kernel-based or classifier-based distribution shift detector (e.g., using MMD), will decide whether to rehearse from a core set, update the stable model, or store an exemplar. We will evaluate on benchmarks of time-to-event data with simulated temporal shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for continual learning in survival analysis, directly addressing non-stationarity in clinical data streams. It will yield a method for producing stable HTE estimates over time and a benchmark for evaluating streaming survival models under distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While continual learning methods exist, their integration with causal survival analysis for HTE estimation is unexplored. The explicit gating mechanism driven by online shift detection to manage a dual-memory structure is a novel architectural approach for this domain.

proposals.title: A Causal Meta-Learning Framework for Online Survival Prediction with Compartmentalized Memory
proposals.motivation: Online learning from clinical data streams, such as electronic health records, is crucial for dynamic risk prediction but faces the stability-plasticity dilemma. Existing methods struggle to balance the retention of long-term, stable knowledge (e.g., established risk factors) with the rapid adaptation to new patterns (e.g., emerging disease subtypes or treatment effects). This limits their reliability for long-term deployment in clinical settings where data is non-stationary and subject to censoring.
proposals.hypothesis: An online learning architecture with biologically inspired, compartmentalized memory—separating stable long-term knowledge from a plastic exploratory component—will achieve superior performance in time-to-event prediction tasks by mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enabling efficient adaptation to concept drift.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural network with two core modules: a stable model with low learning rates and a plastic model with high learning rates. A meta-learner, acting as a gating mechanism, will route incoming patient data based on estimated uncertainty, using principles from causal inference to distinguish novel covariate shifts from stable patterns. Inspired by survival analysis techniques, we will handle censored data via recursively imputed loss functions. Periodic consolidation, triggered by performance-based criteria, will transfer robust weights from the plastic to the stable model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel online learning algorithm specifically designed for clinical time-to-event data, directly addressing the stability-plasticity trade-off. It will extend the causal survival analysis toolkit with a dynamic, memory-augmented method capable of learning continuously from biased, non-stationary data streams, offering a more scalable solution for longitudinal patient monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and continual learning exist, the explicit immunological analogy of compartmentalized memory, combined with a causal routing mechanism for survival data, is novel. This approach uniquely integrates Shalit's expertise in causal inference, survival analysis, and learning from biased data into a single, bio-inspired online framework.

proposals.title: Sequential Monitoring of Causal Assumptions for Robust Streaming Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: Streaming learning from electronic health records and adaptive trials offers a powerful paradigm for dynamic treatment effect estimation. However, standard methods assume stationarity of core assumptions like unconfoundedness, positivity, and covariate shift, which are often violated in clinical practice, leading to biased and unreliable predictions. There is a critical need for frameworks that can continuously audit these assumptions in real-time to ensure the validity of learned models for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding lightweight, sequential statistical tests for causal assumption validity within an online learning algorithm will create a self-diagnosing system that can detect violations early and trigger corrective actions, leading to more robust and trustworthy estimates of time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects from streaming data.
proposals.method: We will extend online survival and causal forests (e.g., Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) to incorporate sequential hypothesis tests on streaming data batches. For each batch, we will compute test statistics for covariate balance (positivity), distributional shifts, and hidden confounding proxies using pre-2026 methods like sequential two-sample tests and change-point detection on propensity score distributions. The algorithm will integrate a decision rule: if a test rejects an assumption, it will trigger a pre-defined action (e.g., data re-weighting via importance sampling, model refitting on a recent window, or alerting for human review). The core learner will be adapted for time-to-event outcomes with censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, closed-loop framework for assumption-aware streaming causal inference, moving beyond passive learning to active model auditing. It will deliver a concrete algorithm and theoretical guarantees on test error under monitored violations, directly enhancing the reliability of dynamic clinical prediction models. The resulting software will be a key tool for robust off-policy evaluation and learning in biased, non-stationary data streams.
proposals.novelty: While online learning and causal assumption testing exist separately, their fusion into a single, automated monitoring-and-correction loop for streaming HTE estimation is novel. This approach uniquely addresses the dynamic validity of causal structures, not just predictive performance, in a sequential decision-making context.

proposals.title: Streaming Survival Forests for Dynamic Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Clinical Data Streams
proposals.motivation: Clinical practice and patient populations evolve over time, leading to non-stationary data streams where treatment effect mechanisms may change. Existing methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival analysis, like the researcher's own Recursively Imputed Trees, are batch-based and cannot adapt to such temporal shifts. This limits their utility for real-time clinical decision support systems that must learn continuously from electronic health records or streaming trial data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming, ensemble-based algorithm that incrementally updates survival models and adaptively re-weights historical data can accurately estimate time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects from a non-stationary stream of censored time-to-event data.
proposals.method: We will develop a Streaming Recursively Imputed Survival Forest (SRISF), extending the batch Recursively Imputed Trees framework. The method will employ a reservoir of recent data and a fading factor mechanism to down-weight older, potentially irrelevant samples. We will integrate online gradient boosting for survival (e.g., an online adaptation of gradient boosting for the Cox partial likelihood) to grow trees incrementally. Concept drift detection, based on monitoring the distribution of imputed survival times or martingale residuals, will trigger model adaptation or retraining of specific tree branches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated method for online estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects with time-to-event outcomes, enabling continuous learning from clinical data streams. It will extend the causal inference toolbox for dynamic healthcare environments and offer a benchmark for evaluating streaming HTE methods in survival settings.
proposals.novelty: While online learning and survival analysis exist separately, and batch HTE estimation for survival is established, their synthesis for streaming, non-stationary causal estimation is novel. The key innovation is adapting recursive imputation and ensemble-based causal estimation to a data stream with concept drift.

proposals.title: Causal Model-Predictive Planning for Personalized Treatment Sequences in Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, planning optimal, personalized sequences of treatments over time is a critical challenge, especially for time-to-event outcomes like survival or disease progression. Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) offers a framework for such planning, but standard approaches often invert a learned world model to derive a policy, which can be unstable and data-inefficient. A robust, forward-simulation-based planning method that leverages modern generative models is needed for high-stakes, heterogeneous settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learned, causal dynamics prior—modeling patient state transitions under treatment—into a differentiable model-predictive control (MPC) loop will yield more robust and interpretable personalized treatment plans than policy-inversion methods, particularly when dealing with censored survival data and unobserved confounding.
proposals.method: We will develop a planning algorithm where a deep latent variable model, informed by techniques from causal inference and survival analysis (e.g., leveraging structural causal models and accounting for censoring), serves as the differentiable dynamics prior. This model will be trained on offline clinical data to predict patient state evolution and time-to-event risks. An outer-loop optimizer (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method or gradient-based shooting) will propose candidate treatment sequences, evaluate them through simulated rollouts in the learned model, and iteratively refine them to maximize a reward combining efficacy and safety. The system will re-plan at each decision point using updated patient information.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel planning framework at the intersection of causal inference, survival analysis, and model-based RL, specifically designed for clinical sequencing problems. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating treatment policies in offline settings with censored outcomes and demonstrate improved robustness over model-free and policy-inversion-based MBRL methods in simulated and real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a *causally-informed* dynamics model, designed to handle time-to-event data with hidden confounding, into a forward-rollout-based MPC planner, avoiding the pitfalls of direct policy inversion. This directly connects Uri Shalit's expertise in causal survival analysis with advanced model-based planning.

proposals.title: Inverting Learned Dynamics for Individualized Treatment Planning: A Causal Geophysics Analogy
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, planning optimal treatment sequences is a complex control problem under uncertainty. Model-based reinforcement learning and control often struggle with sample efficiency and bias in offline settings, particularly for high-stakes, heterogeneous populations. Geophysical inversion provides a powerful conceptual framework for solving such inverse problems by iteratively fitting a forward model to observed data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing treatment planning as an inverse problem—where a target patient trajectory is the 'observed data' and a learned causal survival model is the 'forward operator'—will yield more sample-efficient and interpretable individualized plans than standard policy search methods in offline, biased datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that first trains a deep, structured survival model (e.g., a recurrent neural network with latent time-varying covariates) to predict patient trajectories under treatments, incorporating techniques for handling censoring and hidden confounding. For a given target outcome (e.g., a desired survival profile), we will then treat the model as a differentiable simulator and use iterative gradient-based optimization (via automatic differentiation) to invert it, solving for the sequence of treatment assignments that minimizes the discrepancy between the simulated and target trajectory, subject to clinical feasibility constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel planning algorithm that bridges causal inference, survival analysis, and control theory. It will provide a framework for deriving individualized treatment strategies directly from observational data and mechanistic models, with improved interpretability through the inversion lens. We will release a benchmark for evaluating such inverse planning methods in simulated and real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit analogy to geophysical inversion applied to a learned clinical causal model, moving beyond standard policy optimization. This approach directly leverages the gradient of a prognostic model for planning, a technique not commonly used in heterogeneous treatment effect estimation for time-to-event outcomes.

proposals.title: Flow-Guided Variational Inference for Individualized Treatment Planning in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, optimal treatment plans are often complex and multi-modal, varying significantly across patient subgroups. Existing methods for dynamic treatment regimes, especially in time-to-event settings with censoring, struggle to capture this heterogeneity and the full distribution of plausible optimal strategies, often providing only point estimates. This limits their utility for personalized, risk-aware planning where clinicians must weigh multiple potential optimal pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing treatment planning as a variational inference problem, with a normalizing flow parameterizing the posterior over patient-specific optimal trajectories, will yield a more expressive and efficient model for discovering and characterizing heterogeneous optimal treatment strategies from observational survival data.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep generative model combining a structured survival model for time-to-event outcomes with a normalizing flow (e.g., RealNVP or MAF) as the variational posterior over sequences of treatment assignments and latent patient states. The model will be trained via amortized variational inference, maximizing an evidence lower bound that incorporates a reward signal based on counterfactual survival outcomes, inferred using pre-2026 causal adjustment techniques for time-varying confounding. The flow will enable direct sampling and density estimation of multi-modal optimal plans.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, probabilistically rigorous framework for inverse planning in clinical contexts, directly outputting distributions over personalized treatment strategies. It will extend the toolkit for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in survival analysis beyond static or simple dynamic regimes, providing a method to reason about uncertainty and multiplicity in optimal clinical paths.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of normalizing flows—an expressive tool for density estimation—into the inverse planning and causal survival framework, moving beyond estimating mean effects to modeling the full distribution of patient-specific optimal dynamic treatment regimes.

proposals.title: Inverse Causal Survival Planning: Optimal Treatment Assignment via Inversion of Heterogeneous Effect Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, models for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) on survival outcomes are used for prediction, but not directly for generating optimal treatment policies. Translating a learned causal model of time-to-event outcomes into a concrete, personalized intervention plan remains an open inverse problem. This is critical for precision medicine, where clinicians need actionable recommendations on who to treat, when, and for how long, based on complex, high-dimensional patient data with right-censoring.
proposals.hypothesis: That a causal survival model, trained to estimate conditional treatment effects, can be algorithmically inverted to solve for the optimal sequence of treatment assignments that maximizes a patient's expected survival time or event-free probability, subject to clinical constraints.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework for HTE estimation with censored data. The inverse planning problem will be formulated as a constrained optimization over the treatment variable, using the learned RIST model as a differentiable surrogate to approximate the expected survival function. We will develop a two-stage algorithm: first, train a RIST model on observational data to estimate counterfactual survival curves; second, for a new patient, use gradient-based optimization or Bayesian optimization to find the treatment assignment that maximizes the expected restricted mean survival time (RMST) from the model, potentially incorporating hidden covariate models to adjust for unmeasured confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between causal effect estimation and prescriptive analytics in survival settings. It will yield a concrete tool for personalized treatment planning directly from observational studies and randomized trial data. The resulting software package will enable clinicians to simulate and optimize intervention strategies for individual patients based on their dynamic risk profiles.
proposals.novelty: While causal survival forests exist for HTE estimation, their use as a differentiable objective for *inverse optimization* of treatment plans is novel. This approach moves beyond identifying who benefits to algorithmically determining the optimal intervention, integrating causal inference with optimal control under the specific challenges of censored time-to-event data.

proposals.title: Causal Latent State Inference with Partially Observed Confounders: A Variational Framework for Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, key patient states (e.g., disease progression, adherence) are often latent and only sporadically observed via imperfect tests or notes. Standard methods either ignore these noisy observations or treat them as fully reliable, biasing causal effect estimates and survival predictions. A principled framework to integrate this sporadic, biased evidence into latent variable models is needed for robust personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the observation mechanism of sporadic evidence as a separate, potentially biased sensor, a variational objective that conditions latent state inference on this evidence will yield more accurate and identifiable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects and survival functions in the presence of hidden time-varying confounding.
proposals.method: We will extend a variational recurrent neural network (VRNN) for time-to-event outcomes. The model includes a primary observation decoder and a separate explicit observation model for the sporadic evidence channel. The ELBO will be augmented with a conditional term: log p(partial observations | inferred latent state, sensor parameters). We will employ amortized variational inference, using techniques from prior work on hidden covariates and censored data, to learn the latent dynamics, treatment effects, and the evidence sensor's bias/noise parameters jointly.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a new method for leveraging sporadic clinical measurements to de-bias estimates of treatment effects and survival curves. It contributes a testable latent variable model that quantifies uncertainty about partially observed confounders, directly advancing the field of causal inference from longitudinal observational data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard latent variable models that treat sporadic evidence as just another observation, this framework explicitly models its generation process, preventing over-reliance and enabling formal sensitivity analysis to observation bias, which is novel for causal survival estimation.

proposals.title: Kriging for Causal Inference: A Latent State Model for Treatment Effect Estimation with Occasional Direct Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, patient health is often a latent state (e.g., disease severity) observed only through noisy, high-frequency proxies (e.g., lab tests, vitals). Rare, definitive measurements (e.g., biopsy, gold-standard assay) provide ground truth but are too sparse for direct modeling. Current latent variable models (e.g., hidden Markov models, state-space models) struggle to optimally integrate these heterogeneous data sources, potentially biasing causal estimates of treatment effects over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting geostatistical Kriging with External Drift to sequential latent variable inference will yield more accurate and calibrated estimates of latent health trajectories and heterogeneous treatment effects by formally modeling the spatio-temporal covariance between sparse direct measurements and dense proxy observations.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep latent variable model where the inference network is structured as a Gaussian Process (GP) with a kernel learned from the data. The occasional direct measurements of the latent state serve as the high-precision 'drift' points. During training, we optimize a dual objective: a reconstruction loss for proxies and a precision-weighted loss for direct measurements, regularized by the GP prior. For causal estimation, we extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to use this improved latent state representation as a time-varying confounder in a structural survival model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for fusing multi-fidelity longitudinal data in clinical settings. It will advance the estimation of time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects by reducing bias in latent state reconstruction. We will release a benchmark and software integrating the method with existing causal survival and reinforcement learning toolkits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of geostatistical data fusion techniques (Kriging) to the sequential latent variable and causal inference setting, creating a hybrid model that explicitly learns the reliability structure between data sources, which is non-standard in clinical ML.

proposals.title: Causal Hidden Semi-Markov Models: Estimating Treatment Effects from Irregularly Sampled Clinical Time Series
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient state (e.g., disease severity) is a latent process observed only at irregular visits, and treatment effects may depend on the duration spent in previous latent states, violating the Markov assumption. Existing methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from longitudinal data often rely on strong Markov or full observability assumptions, limiting their validity in chronic disease management where history matters and data is sparse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model combining a hidden semi-Markov model (HSMM) with a causal inference framework can consistently estimate time-varying treatment effects from irregular, partial observations by explicitly learning the non-Markovian duration dependencies in the latent health process.
proposals.method: We will develop a Causal HSMM, where the latent state duration distributions are modeled via flexible hazard models (e.g., using neural networks), and state transitions are influenced by treatment assignments. The model will be trained using a variational inference objective, integrating the evidence from sparse observations. The expectation-maximization algorithm will be adapted to handle right-censored time-to-event outcomes, akin to techniques in survival analysis, to estimate the posterior distribution of latent state paths and the associated treatment effect on state duration and transition probabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for estimating causal effects from noisy, irregular longitudinal data where treatment efficacy is duration-dependent. It will extend the toolkit for personalized medicine by enabling the estimation of how treatment heterogeneity unfolds over time in non-Markovian latent processes, directly applicable to domains like oncology or cardiology.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of causal estimands with a hidden semi-Markov model in the partially observed setting, moving beyond standard Markovian hidden Markov models (HMMs) or marginal structural models to capture duration-based treatment effects.

proposals.title: Dual-Phase Learning for Causal Survival Analysis with Intermittently Observed Latent States
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, patient trajectories are governed by latent health states (e.g., disease progression) that are rarely measured continuously. Standard survival and causal inference methods treat these states as fully hidden, discarding valuable information from the occasional lab tests or biopsies that partially reveal them. This leads to biased estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) over time, particularly when treatments affect the underlying progression rate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-phase learning framework, which explicitly models the joint distribution of intermittent direct observations and the latent state process, will yield more accurate and efficient estimates of time-to-event outcomes and associated treatment effects than methods ignoring the partial observations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the framework of hidden Markov models (HMMs) and state-space models for survival analysis. The method involves a dual-phase objective: 1) a reconstruction phase using a variational autoencoder (VAE) to impute the latent trajectory, trained on instances where the state is sporadically observed, and 2) an outcome phase using recursively imputed survival trees (RIST) or a deep Cox model to estimate the survival hazard conditional on the imputed latent state and treatments. Causal identification for HTEs will leverage the g-formula, adjusting for the estimated latent history.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method to incorporate sporadic direct measurements into causal survival analysis, improving the estimation of individualized treatment rules in chronic diseases. It will extend the RIST framework to handle partially observed latent covariates, offering a new benchmark for offline evaluation with biased, state-dependent censoring.
proposals.novelty: While HMMs for survival exist, the integration of intermittent direct state observations into a causal estimation pipeline for HTEs in time-to-event data is novel. The dual-phase approach moves beyond treating states as purely hidden or fully observed, a gap in pre-2026 latent variable causal inference.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Sensitivity to Unmeasured Confounding: A Framework for Evaluating Causal Survival and Treatment Effect Methods
proposals.motivation: Causal inference methods for treatment and survival outcomes, such as those for heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), rely critically on the unconfoundedness assumption. In practice, unmeasured confounding is ubiquitous, yet the evaluation of these methods is typically conducted on simulated or observational datasets where the true confounding structure is unknown. This creates a gap between methodological claims and real-world applicability, as we lack systematic benchmarks to quantify how performance degrades with known, increasing confounding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing benchmark datasets with systematically varied and known degrees of unmeasured confounding, we can create a rigorous sensitivity analysis framework that reveals the relative robustness of different causal estimation methods (e.g., causal forests, doubly robust estimators, instrumental variable approaches) under realistic violations of unconfoundedness.
proposals.method: We will design a competition framework generating semi-synthetic datasets based on real-world clinical data (e.g., from ICU time-to-event studies or electronic health records). We will embed known, parameterized confounding mechanisms—such as latent variables influencing both treatment assignment and outcome—to create a controlled spectrum of unconfoundedness violations. The evaluation will specifically test methods central to Shalit's recent work, including survival HTE estimation with recursively imputed trees and causal inference for clinical outcomes, measuring bias and variance in estimated treatment effects as a function of the confounding strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized benchmark suite for evaluating sensitivity to unmeasured confounding in causal machine learning. It will yield practical guidance for applied researchers on method selection under realistic data limitations and will drive methodological innovation by establishing a clear, quantitative challenge for developing more robust estimators.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis frameworks exist, the creation of a public, semi-synthetic benchmarking competition with *known* and *tunable* ground-truth confounding is novel. It shifts evaluation from verifying correctness under ideal assumptions to stress-testing robustness under controlled, realistic failures, directly addressing a core, unsolved problem in applied causal inference.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Estimation Under Severe Covariate Imbalance: A Model-Based Framework with Targeted Regularization
proposals.motivation: In observational studies, especially in clinical settings, severe covariate imbalance between treatment and control groups is common, violating the overlap assumption central to most causal inference methods. Pre-2026 methods like propensity score matching or weighting fail or become unstable in such regions, limiting the external validity and practical utility of estimated treatment effects. This problem is acute in precision medicine, where we seek to predict heterogeneous effects for all patients, not just those with balanced covariate profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the conditional outcomes and employing targeted regularization that prioritizes bias reduction over variance in regions of poor overlap, we can develop estimators for heterogeneous treatment effects that remain stable and consistent even under severe covariate imbalance.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, model-based framework building on recent advances in causal survival analysis and representation learning. First, we will learn a shared representation of the covariates using a neural network architecture regularized to enforce approximate balance only where data supports it. Second, for treatment effect estimation, we will adapt the R-learner framework, employing a specialized, adaptive regularization scheme. This scheme will down-weight the influence of samples in regions of extreme propensity score (near 0 or 1) on the regularization of the treatment effect function, while preserving their use for outcome model fitting. We will benchmark against methods like Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) and doubly robust estimators under simulated and real-world clinical data with limited overlap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, practical method for estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATE) in real-world datasets with prevalent covariate imbalance. It will extend the applicability of causal inference in clinical research by offering robust performance where standard methods fail. We will release a software package and establish new benchmarking protocols for evaluating methods under lack of overlap.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods either fail without overlap or resort to extrapolation, our approach novelly decouples the regularization for outcome prediction from the regularization for effect estimation, allowing controlled, model-based extrapolation informed by the underlying data structure.
