This moves beyond simple weighting or matching strategies dominant pre-2026.

proposals.title: Estimating Population Average Treatment Effects on the Treated (PATT) with Offline Data: Methods and Benchmarks for Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: In clinical and policy settings, decisions are often made for a treated population (e.g., patients receiving a new therapy) based on evidence from a non-random sample, such as a clinical trial or a specific hospital system. The Sample Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (SATT) is commonly estimated but may not generalize to the broader target population of all future treated individuals. Pre-2026, methods for estimating the Population Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (PATT) – which accounts for covariate shift between the study sample and the target population – are underdeveloped, especially for complex, high-dimensional data common in modern observational studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel doubly robust estimators, combined with flexible machine learning models for propensity scores and outcome regressions, can provide more accurate and robust estimates of PATT from biased offline data than existing SATT-focused methods. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the performance gain will be most significant under substantial covariate shift and in survival settings with censoring.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework for PATT estimation by extending doubly robust and targeted learning approaches to handle covariate shift, explicitly modeling the selection mechanism into the study sample. For survival outcomes, we will adapt our prior work on recursively imputed trees and survival forests to estimate PATT under censoring. We will create a benchmark suite using semi-synthetic data generators, building on our experience with imperfect simulators, to evaluate methods under known degrees of sample selection bias and covariate shift. The core comparison will pit our PATT-optimized estimators against standard SATT estimators (e.g., AIPW) and simple weighting schemes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the causal inference and machine learning communities with rigorously evaluated methods and open-source software for PATT estimation, filling a gap between general population effect (ATE) and sample-specific (SATT) estimation. It will deliver practical guidance and benchmarks for applied researchers in healthcare and policy who need to generalize treatment effect estimates from a study sample to a defined target population of treated individuals.
proposals.novelty: While methods for estimating the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) under covariate shift exist, a focused methodological toolkit for the PATT estimand – which is often the relevant quantity for policy – is lacking. Our approach uniquely integrates modern machine learning for high-dimensional covariates, addresses survival outcomes with censoring, and provides dedicated benchmarks for a competition-style evaluation of PATT estimation performance.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic-Driven Decision Framework for Causal Method Selection in Clinical and Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Applied researchers in clinical and epidemiological settings face a dizzying array of causal inference methods (e.g., propensity score matching, inverse probability weighting, g-methods, causal forests). The choice is often ad-hoc, driven by convention rather than systematic diagnostics of the specific dataset's properties, such as covariate overlap, censoring patterns, and non-linearity. This leads to fragile analyses and undermines the credibility of estimated heterogeneous treatment effects, which are critical for personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, data-driven framework that maps the results of formal diagnostic tests to a ranked set of appropriate causal estimation strategies will lead to more robust, defensible, and reproducible causal conclusions in observational studies, particularly for time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision tree framework grounded in pre-2026 causal theory. It will integrate diagnostic modules for covariate balance (e.g., standardized mean differences), overlap (via propensity score distributions), censoring informativeness (via tests for dependent censoring), and functional form complexity. Based on these diagnostics, the framework will recommend and justify a specific methodological pathway, such as using doubly robust estimators for moderate overlap or leveraging recent methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) for complex, high-dimensional survival data with heterogeneous effects. The framework will be implemented as an open-source R/Python package with interactive visual diagnostics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, principled guide for applied researchers to select and defend their causal methodology, moving beyond heuristic choices. It will synthesize and operationalize existing diagnostic literature into a single actionable workflow, directly supporting the rigorous estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects in clinical prediction and survival analysis.
proposals.novelty: While diagnostic tools exist in isolation, no pre-2026 framework synthesizes them into a prescriptive, domain-aware guide specifically for clinical and survival settings, nor does it integrate guidance for modern machine learning-based estimators like RIST that Shalit has developed for handling hidden covariates and complex censoring.

proposals.title: AutoML for Causal Response Surfaces: Automating Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Clinical Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational clinical data, especially time-to-event outcomes with censoring, requires complex modeling of the response surface. Pre-2026, this process is highly manual, requiring expert choices for model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and handling of high-dimensional, hidden covariates, which introduces variability and limits reproducibility in critical clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying modern AutoML frameworks to automate the entire modeling pipeline for causal response surfaces will produce more robust and accurate estimates of HTEs in survival outcomes compared to manually specified models, while reducing analyst-induced variability.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend AutoML systems like Auto-sklearn, H2O, or TPOT to the causal inference setting. The pipeline will automate the search over ensembles of base learners (e.g., gradient boosting, survival forests, neural nets) and hyperparameters for estimating potential outcomes, specifically targeting the nuisance functions in doubly robust estimators like the doubly robust learning (DR-Learner). We will integrate this with methods for handling censored survival data and hidden confounding, building on Shalit's prior work with recursively imputed trees and latent variable models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, open-source AutoML tool for causal HTE estimation, lowering the barrier to reliable analysis for clinical researchers. It will yield a systematic benchmark comparing automated vs. manual causal pipelines on real-world clinical datasets, establishing best practices for reproducible causal machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While AutoML is established for pure prediction, its systematic application to the full causal estimation pipeline—particularly for survival outcomes with confounding—is novel. It moves beyond automating a single model to automating the entire causal learning stack, a significant step toward democratizing robust causal inference.

proposals.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Causal Survival Forests: Flexible Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation with Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but is complicated by censoring and complex, unknown response surfaces. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's recursively imputed survival trees, offer flexibility but can be sensitive to model misspecification and provide limited uncertainty quantification for the estimated effect surfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating Bayesian nonparametric models, specifically Gaussian Processes (GPs) and Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART), into the causal survival framework will yield more robust and accurate estimates of HTEs while providing principled uncertainty intervals, especially when the underlying treatment-response relationship is nonlinear or involves high-order interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage Bayesian nonparametric model for causal survival analysis. First, we adapt the Bayesian Causal Forest (BCF) framework, using BART to flexibly model the prognostic and moderating functions of patient covariates. Second, for the outcome model, we will employ a Gaussian Process prior on the log-hazard function, allowing for a smooth, nonparametric response surface conditional on treatment and the BART-derived functions. Inference will be performed via Markov Chain Monte Carlo, leveraging existing pre-2026 software (e.g., `bartMachine`, `GPstuff`) to handle censored data using data augmentation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, fully Bayesian method for HTE estimation in survival settings with built-in uncertainty quantification for the entire response surface. It will extend the causal inference toolbox for clinical decision-making by offering a model that is both highly flexible and robust to complex confounding patterns, directly applicable to datasets like the fresh ICSI cycles study.
proposals.novelty: While GPs and BART have been used separately for causal inference or survival analysis, their integrated application to model the *causal* survival response surface—combining BART's strength in high-dimensional confounding with GPs' smooth function flexibility—is novel for pre-2026 HTE estimation with censored outcomes.

proposals.title: Stacking for Causal Inference: An Ensemble Framework for Robust Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is critical for personalized medicine but methodologically challenging. Pre-2026, numerous causal inference strategies exist—including propensity score methods, outcome regression, and doubly robust estimators—each with different strengths and failure modes depending on the underlying data-generating process. No single method is universally best, leading to variable performance and researcher uncertainty in real-world clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that model stacking, a supervised ensemble technique from predictive modeling, can be adapted to combine multiple base causal estimators, creating a meta-learner that consistently outperforms any single constituent method in terms of the precision of HTE estimates across diverse data scenarios.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Super Learner framework, using a cross-validated stacking algorithm to find an optimal convex combination of base causal learners. Base learners will include causal forests, Bayesian additive regression trees (BART) for causal inference, propensity score-based estimators, and doubly robust methods like targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE). The ensemble's loss function will be tailored for causal estimation, such as the expected precision of estimating heterogeneous effects (EPEHE) or a pseudo-outcome transformation. We will validate the approach using extensive semi-synthetic benchmarks based on real clinical data (e.g., from survival or IVF studies) where ground truth is known.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-adaptive framework for robust HTE estimation, reducing reliance on a priori method selection. It will deliver a practical software tool and establish benchmarks demonstrating when and how ensemble causal inference improves accuracy, directly impacting clinical prediction and treatment stratification research.
proposals.novelty: While stacking is established for pure prediction, its systematic application to combine *causal inference* strategies for HTE estimation is novel. This moves beyond simple benchmarking or one-off ensembles to a formal, generalizable meta-learning approach for causal parameters.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Causal and Survival Methods in Ultra-High-Dimensional Covariate Spaces: A Scalability Challenge
proposals.motivation: Modern clinical datasets, such as those from genomics and electronic health records, routinely contain thousands to millions of covariates (p >> n). Pre-2026, benchmarks for causal inference and survival analysis methods often use low-dimensional or moderately high-dimensional data, failing to stress-test scalability, computational efficiency, and variable selection under extreme sparsity. This gap limits the practical evaluation of methods designed for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in complex biomedical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in ultra-high-dimensional settings (p ~ 10^4-10^6), the relative performance ranking of state-of-the-art methods for causal survival analysis will shift dramatically, with methods incorporating structured regularization or two-stage variable selection outperforming those that naively scale.
proposals.method: We will generate a suite of semi-synthetic benchmark datasets with realistic censoring and treatment assignment mechanisms, embedding sparse, non-linear heterogeneous treatment effects within a vast covariate space. We will systematically evaluate methods from Uri Shalit's recent work—including Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST), causal forests, and doubly robust learners—alongside deep learning survival models (DeepSurv, DeepHit) and high-dimensional regularization techniques (LASSO, gradient boosting). Performance will be assessed on scalability, accuracy of CATE estimation, and variable selection fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, publicly available benchmark suite for ultra-high-dimensional causal survival analysis, establishing new standards for method evaluation. It will yield concrete guidelines for practitioners on which algorithms scale effectively and under what data-generating conditions, directly informing the analysis of complex biomedical data.
proposals.novelty: While high-dimensional benchmarks exist for pure prediction, a focused challenge for *causal* and *survival* outcomes with structured, sparse signals is novel. It moves beyond testing accuracy in low dimensions to stress-testing the computational and statistical limits of cutting-edge methods in a realistic, clinically-motivated regime.

proposals.title: The Longitudinal Causal Inference Challenge (LCIC): A Benchmarking Platform for Dynamic Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating causal effects from observational time-series data is critical for personalized medicine and policy evaluation, where treatments are administered sequentially and outcomes evolve dynamically. Pre-2026, while methods exist for static settings and some for longitudinal data, there is a lack of rigorous, standardized benchmarks to compare their performance on realistic, complex data with time-varying confounding, censoring, and hidden states. This hinders methodological progress and practical adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed open competition, featuring novel semi-synthetic datasets with known ground-truth effects and realistic generative models, will reveal significant performance gaps between existing causal inference methods and highlight the most promising algorithmic strategies for longitudinal settings.
proposals.method: We will design and host a public competition. First, we will generate benchmark datasets using a flexible data-generating process that incorporates time-varying hidden covariates, realistic censoring mechanisms (informed by Shalit's work on survival analysis), and complex treatment assignment policies. The core evaluation will focus on estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATE) over time. We will adapt and evaluate a suite of state-of-the-art methods, including R-learner extensions for time-series, hidden Markov models for latent confounding, and G-methods (like g-computation and marginal structural models) implemented with modern machine learning. Participant submissions will be rigorously scored on both precision in estimating heterogeneous effects and robustness to unobserved confounding.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce: 1) A public repository of high-quality, challenging longitudinal causal inference datasets with known ground truth, serving as a lasting community resource. 2) A comprehensive, empirical comparison of methodological families, identifying their strengths, weaknesses, and failure modes in dynamic settings. 3) The establishment of a clear benchmarking standard to guide future research in time-series causal inference.
proposals.novelty: While challenges exist for static causal inference (e.g., ACIC), this proposal uniquely focuses on the longitudinal setting, integrating time-varying hidden confounding and survival-type censoring into the benchmark's core design, which directly addresses gaps in pre-2026 evaluation paradigms.

proposals.title: Adversarial Data Generation for Stress-Testing Causal and Survival Estimation Methods
proposals.motivation: The reliability of methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) and survival outcomes from observational data is paramount for clinical decision-making. Current benchmarking relies on a limited set of canonical data-generating processes (DGPs), which may not expose subtle, critical failure modes of popular algorithms. A systematic framework to discover these failure modes is needed to improve methodological robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing the discovery of estimator failure modes as an inverse optimization problem, we can algorithmically generate adversarial DGPs that systematically expose and quantify the limitations of state-of-the-art causal and survival estimation methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative adversarial network (GAN) framework where the generator proposes DGPs (with specified treatment assignment, confounding, censoring, and outcome models), and the discriminator comprises a suite of target estimators (e.g., R-learner, Bayesian Causal Forests, Cox-based HTE models). The generator's objective is to maximize a disparity metric (e.g., error in ATE or CATE) between the discriminator's estimates and the known ground truth of the DGP. We will constrain DGPs to be clinically plausible, using domain knowledge to parameterize realistic confounding and censoring mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel benchmark suite of 'adversarial' DGPs that challenge current methods, 2) a taxonomy of failure modes for causal and survival estimators, and 3) an open-source framework for the community to stress-test new methods. This moves evaluation beyond static benchmarks to dynamic, targeted challenge generation.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial examples are known in predictive ML, applying this inverse problem paradigm to stress-test *causal inference and survival analysis* estimators is novel. It shifts the focus from improving estimators on fixed benchmarks to actively searching for the benchmarks that break them.

proposals.title: Causal Calibration Under Heterogeneous Case-Fatality: A Survival and Causal Inference Framework for Epidemic Model Assessment
proposals.motivation: Epidemic models, crucial for public health planning, are often calibrated using aggregated case-fatality rates (CFR), assuming stability across subpopulations and time. This assumption is frequently violated due to factors like age structure, pathogen strain evolution, and varying treatment access, leading to biased parameter estimates and unreliable projections. A principled method to account for this heterogeneity is needed to improve the accuracy and credibility of model-based policy decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling CFR as a function of latent, time-varying covariates (e.g., age distribution, strain prevalence) and treatment effects within a causal survival framework will significantly improve the calibration accuracy and uncertainty quantification of epidemic models compared to methods assuming a stable, aggregate CFR.
proposals.method: We will develop a semi-parametric survival model where the hazard of death is a function of infection time, individual-level covariates (age, comorbidity), and latent group-level variables (e.g., dominant strain). Using a causal inference approach for time-to-event outcomes, we will adjust for informative censoring and time-dependent confounding in observational data. The model will be integrated into a Bayesian calibration pipeline, using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for inference, to jointly estimate epidemic parameters and heterogeneous CFR drivers from surveillance and mortality data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, rigorous framework for calibrating complex epidemic models in the presence of realistic, heterogeneous fatality risks. It will yield new benchmarks for evaluating model calibration under covariate shift and produce open-source software integrating causal survival analysis with simulation-based inference for the epidemiological modeling community.
proposals.novelty: While CFR heterogeneity is acknowledged, existing calibration methods largely ignore its structural, time-varying causes. This proposal uniquely combines causal survival analysis—addressing time-dependent confounding—with simulation-based inference to formally account for this heterogeneity, moving beyond post-hoc adjustments.

proposals.title: Estimating Case Fatality Rates from Individual-Level Observational Data via Semi-Supervised Causal Survival Learning
proposals.motivation: In many clinical and epidemiological settings, such as emerging infectious diseases or novel treatments in specific subpopulations, individual-level patient data (e.g., time-to-event with covariates) is available, but the true population-level case fatality rate (CFR) is unknown due to selection bias, incomplete follow-up, or lack of a defined denominator. Pre-2026 methods for estimating treatment effects or CFRs typically assume either full population data or known external calibration targets, a constraint that limits real-world applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly modeling the latent population distribution and the censored individual-level survival process within a semi-supervised causal framework, we can consistently estimate the population CFR without requiring it as an input, provided the individual data contains informative covariates about the selection mechanism.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel semi-supervised deep survival model. The architecture will consist of a treatment-agnostic representation network, a survival time predictor, and a propensity model for the data selection process, all trained adversarially. The core innovation is a population-level moment-matching constraint: the model's implied marginal survival curve must be consistent with the empirical distribution of observed event times, while accounting for censoring via a recursively imputed pseudo-population, inspired by Shalit's prior work. We will extend Bayesian nonparametric models and leverage ideas from domain adaptation to identify the latent population distribution.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a new methodological tool for epidemiologists and clinical researchers to estimate crucial population-level risk metrics from biased, incomplete cohorts. It will advance the field of causal survival analysis by solving an inverse problem where the target is a marginal quantity, not a conditional treatment effect. The work will include a theoretical analysis of identifiability conditions and a benchmark on synthetic and real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: Existing survival and causal inference methods treat the population outcome distribution as either known or irrelevant; inverting this constraint to *estimate* it from biased individual data is a novel problem formulation. The integration of moment matching within a causal survival framework to recover an unknown marginal target is distinct from prior work on heterogeneous treatment effect estimation.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests with Augmented Data: Robust Risk Prediction Under EHR Missingness
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are foundational for clinical risk prediction but suffer from systematic missingness (e.g., socioeconomic factors, lifestyle data), leading to biased estimates and poor generalization. Pre-2026, methods for handling missing data often rely on strong, untestable assumptions like Missing Completely at Random, which are frequently violated in clinical settings, compromising the validity of personalized treatment effect estimates in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating structured, externally sourced alternative data (e.g., geocoded neighborhood indices, aggregated consumer data) with EHRs through a causal survival framework can yield more robust and transportable predictions of heterogeneous treatment effects, even when core EHR covariates are missing not at random.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework from Shalit's prior work. The model will be a multi-source survival forest where splits can occur on either EHR variables or imputed, privacy-preserving external features. We will treat the integration as a missing data problem, using a novel weighting scheme within the tree induction to down-weight splits on variables with high missingness correlation with the outcome, formalizing a missingness-aware causal forest. Estimation will target Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST) differences for treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open-source algorithm for survival analysis under informative missingness, 2) a benchmarking framework for evaluating prediction robustness when EHR data is incomplete, and 3) empirical evidence on the utility of specific alternative data sources for improving causal estimates in clinical cohorts, moving beyond simple imputation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-source data integration and survival forests exist, combining them within a *causal* framework that explicitly models the missingness mechanism of EHR variables as a bias source, rather than a nuisance, is a novel direction. This directly challenges reliance on EHRs as a sole, sufficient data source.

proposals.title: Generalizability of Hybrid Causal-Survival Models: A Multi-Cohort Benchmark for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Clinical prediction and treatment effect models developed on single-population data often fail to generalize across diverse healthcare systems and patient demographics, limiting their real-world utility. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to validate such models externally, but rigorous benchmarks for causal survival models across international cohorts are lacking. This gap hinders the deployment of personalized treatment recommendations in global health.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance and calibration of a hybrid causal-survival model for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects will degrade when applied to international populations with differing covariate distributions and censoring mechanisms, but this degradation can be systematically characterized and partially mitigated through domain adaptation techniques.
proposals.method: We will leverage Uri Shalit's prior work on Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) and hidden variable causal models as the core hybrid architecture. The model will be trained on a large, well-curated source dataset (e.g., Israeli EHR). We will then perform external validation on 3-5 independent, international time-to-event datasets (e.g., from US, EU, and Asian healthcare systems), evaluating performance on C-for-benefit and time-varying calibration metrics. We will employ causal domain adaptation methods, such as re-weighting based on propensity scores for study membership, to assess performance improvements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, multi-cohort benchmark for causal survival models, establishing bounds on their generalizability. It will produce a methodological framework for auditing model transportability and offer practical guidance on when and how to adapt models for new populations, directly informing future international clinical implementation.
proposals.novelty: While model validation is standard, a focused benchmark for *causal* survival models across heterogeneous international real-world data, combining Shalit's expertise in hidden variable modeling and survival analysis with domain adaptation, is a novel contribution to the field of clinical machine learning.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Offline-Simulator Framework for Early Clinical Risk Prediction and Treatment Effect Estimation in Future Pandemics
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for rapid, data-driven clinical decision support for emerging pathogens. Early in an outbreak, only biased, observational patient data (offline) is available, while later, imperfect disease progression simulators may be built. Pre-2026 methods for handling these data sources separately are insufficient for timely, reliable prediction of individual patient risks and heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in a novel pandemic context.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid strategy, combining a baseline predictor trained on biased offline data with calibration using an imperfect simulator, can provide robust and causally-informed clinical predictions for future pandemic pathogens, outperforming methods that use either data source alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'hybrid strategy' conceptualized in prior RL benchmarking work to the survival analysis and causal inference domain. First, we will train a baseline survival model (e.g., a Recursively Imputed Survival Tree or a deep Cox model) on early, censored observational clinical data to predict time-to-severe-outcome. Second, we will treat a separately developed pathogen-specific simulator (e.g., an agent-based model of disease progression) as a source of biased, synthetic data. We will then adapt calibration and debiasing methods, such as inverse propensity scoring or doubly robust estimation, to fuse the simulator outputs with the observational model, refining HTE estimates for candidate treatments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodological framework for fusing real-world evidence and mechanistic simulations during emerging disease outbreaks. It will deliver a practical tool for estimating patient-specific risks and treatment effect heterogeneity from fragmented early data, directly informing triage and resource allocation. The benchmarked approach will add to the literature on causal survival analysis and offline learning under distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid RL strategies exist, their application to causal survival tasks for pandemic preparedness is novel. The core innovation lies in formally adapting the 'offline + imperfect simulator' paradigm to the estimation of time-to-event HTEs, addressing the unique data challenges (censoring, confounding, sim-to-real gap) of a nascent pandemic.

proposals.title: Dynamic Calibration of Survival Models via Real-Time Case-Fatality Rate Updates for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks, particularly for time-to-event outcomes like mortality, model calibration degrades over time due to evolving treatment protocols, pathogen virulence, or population immunity. This drift is especially problematic for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), where miscalibrated baseline risks can bias causal estimates. Pre-2026, methods for handling temporal drift in survival models, especially within a causal framework, are underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic calibration procedure, which continuously updates a survival model's baseline hazard using streaming case-fatality rate (CFR) data, will maintain calibration over time and yield more stable and reliable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects compared to static models.
proposals.method: We will extend a causal survival forest framework (e.g., Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) to incorporate a real-time calibration module. The model will be trained on a static historical cohort with rich covariates and treatment assignments. A separate, lightweight calibration model will then continuously ingest aggregated, population-level CFR time-series data. This module will use a hidden Markov model or a Bayesian filtering approach to estimate a time-varying calibration parameter, which will adjust the baseline survival function of the primary HTE model. The performance will be benchmarked against static models using sequential validation on temporally held-out data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for maintaining the reliability of causal survival models in non-stationary environments. It will contribute a benchmarking framework for evaluating temporal robustness in HTE estimation and offer practical software for clinical risk prediction that adapts to evolving real-world evidence.
proposals.novelty: While model updating and calibration are known, integrating a real-time, population-level signal (CFR) to dynamically recalibrate a *causal* survival model for HTE estimation is novel. This approach moves beyond simple retraining to a principled, lightweight update of the underlying risk distribution.

proposals.title: Domain Adaptation for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Survival Outcomes Across Clinical Domains
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational survival data is crucial for personalized medicine but requires large, high-quality datasets. In practice, a model trained on a well-studied disease domain (source) may fail when applied to a new, related disease (target) due to covariate shift and differing baseline hazard functions. This limits the efficient transfer of predictive models across clinical contexts, a common challenge in multi-center or multi-disease studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying domain adaptation techniques, specifically designed to align feature representations between source and target domains, will significantly improve the accuracy and calibration of survival HTE models when applied to a new clinical domain with limited labeled data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework for HTE estimation to incorporate domain adaptation. The source domain will be a large observational dataset (e.g., oncology). The target will be a smaller dataset from a related but distinct clinical area (e.g., autoimmune disease). We will integrate an adversarial domain classifier or use Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) regularization within the RIST's representation learning layer to learn domain-invariant features. Model performance will be evaluated using targeted bias-reduced metrics for survival HTEs, such as the calibrated estimate of the C-for-benefit, comparing the adapted model to a naive transfer model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, causal inference-grounded method for transferring survival HTE models across clinical domains, reducing the data burden for new applications. It will yield a novel benchmark for evaluating domain adaptation in the context of time-to-event outcomes with censoring and hidden confounding, directly addressing a practical bottleneck in clinical predictive modeling.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is established in machine learning, its application to the specific problem of causal survival analysis with heterogeneous effects is novel. The integration of adversarial or distribution-matching techniques within a recursively imputed tree framework for censored data represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard covariate adjustment.

proposals.title: Transfer Learning for Survival Prediction in Emerging Outbreaks: A Causal Fine-Tuning Framework with Limited Data
proposals.motivation: Rapidly predicting patient outcomes (e.g., time to severe symptoms) during emerging disease outbreaks is critical for triage and resource allocation, but early data is extremely limited and heavily censored. Building accurate survival models from scratch is infeasible, while models from unrelated diseases may fail to capture relevant pathophysiology. This creates a pressing need for methods that can leverage rich data from related, well-studied diseases to bootstrap predictions for a novel, data-scarce outbreak.
proposals.hypothesis: A survival model pre-trained on a source disease with abundant data (e.g., severe influenza) can be effectively adapted to a target outbreak (e.g., a novel respiratory virus) via a transfer learning procedure that incorporates causal selection of shared prognostic covariates and adjusts for treatment heterogeneity, leading to more accurate and robust predictions than training from scratch or direct model application.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework, which handles censored data, to a transfer learning setting. First, we will use causal discovery methods (e.g., PC-algorithm) on the source domain to identify a stable set of prognostic covariates. A neural survival model (e.g., DeepSurv) will be pre-trained on the source. For the target outbreak, we will fine-tune only the final layers and a domain-adversarial component, using the limited, possibly biased, observational data. We will incorporate ideas from offline reinforcement learning evaluation to assess and correct for the bias in the target dataset, ensuring the model is robust to imperfect simulators of clinical progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, methodologically sound framework for rapid clinical prediction model deployment in emerging outbreaks. It will advance the field of transfer learning for survival analysis by integrating causal covariate selection and bias-aware fine-tuning. The resulting benchmark method and software will enable more reliable early-stage clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is common in vision/NLP, its application to censored survival outcomes with a causal, domain-adaptive lens is novel. The integration of Shalit's prior work on RIST, heterogeneous treatment effects, and biased offline data evaluation into a unified transfer pipeline for clinical prediction is a unique synthesis.

proposals.title: Dynamic, Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation for Mortality Risk Using Deep Survival Models with Time-Varying Covariates
proposals.motivation: Accurately predicting patient-specific mortality risk over time is critical for personalized treatment planning. Existing models often treat risk as static or fail to effectively integrate time-varying clinical data (e.g., lab results, treatment adherence) with complex, non-linear covariate effects, limiting their utility for dynamic decision support. This gap is pronounced in settings like oncology or chronic disease management, where treatment effects on survival are heterogeneous and evolve.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning survival model, which explicitly models time-varying covariates and their interaction with treatments, will outperform standard survival and static prediction models in estimating individualized, time-dependent mortality risk and heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture combining a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer encoder to process sequences of time-varying covariates with a survival loss function (e.g., DeepHit or a discrete-time proportional hazards variant). The model will output individual survival functions. To estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) on survival, we will adapt a causal forest framework (building on RIST, Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) using the model's representations as potential outcome surfaces, adjusting for time-dependent confounding via g-methods or targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) where appropriate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, state-of-the-art framework for dynamic mortality risk prediction that integrates complex temporal data. It will extend the causal survival analysis toolkit by enabling HTE estimation in the presence of time-varying confounders and non-proportional hazards, offering clinicians a more nuanced tool for prognosis and treatment comparison.
proposals.novelty: The integration of deep sequential models for survival outcomes with formal causal inference techniques for time-varying treatments addresses a key methodological challenge at the intersection of machine learning and biostatistics, moving beyond static covariate models and simple Cox-based HTE estimation.

proposals.title: Bayesian Melding for Individualized Survival Prediction: Integrating Patient-Level Covariates with Aggregate Case Fatality Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction, individual-level data (e.g., electronic health records) often contain rich covariate information but may have limited follow-up or rare outcomes, leading to high-variance estimates of absolute risk. Conversely, population-level data (e.g., case fatality rates from registries) provide stable outcome estimates but lack individual heterogeneity. Pre-2026 methods for causal survival analysis and treatment effect heterogeneity, like those developed by Shalit, struggle to formally integrate these distinct data sources for improved individual risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian melding framework, which coherently combines a likelihood from individual-level covariate data with a prior or constraint derived from population-level outcome data, will yield more accurate and calibrated estimates of individualized survival functions and treatment effects than models using either source alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Bayesian melding framework to survival settings with potential censoring. The individual-level model will be a flexible parametric survival model (e.g., Bayesian Cox with time-varying effects) or a Bayesian additive survival trees model, informed by patient covariates. The population-level case fatality rate (CFR) will be incorporated via a probabilistic constraint on the marginal survival function, ensuring the model-implied aggregate outcome matches the external evidence. Inference will be performed using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Stan) or variational inference for scalability, with evaluation on benchmarks for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, generalizable method for data fusion in clinical prediction, directly applicable to settings with fragmented data sources. It will extend the causal survival analysis toolkit by enabling more robust estimation of absolute risks and conditional average treatment effects, which are critical for personalized clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian melding has been used in ecology and epidemiology, its application to integrate individual-level covariate models with aggregate outcome constraints for *individualized* survival prediction, particularly within the context of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, is novel and addresses a key gap in pre-2026 clinical machine learning methodology.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests for Primary Care: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects with Sparse, Censored Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for time-to-event outcomes is crucial for personalized medicine, but state-of-the-art methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) rely on rich, high-dimensional data from tertiary care. This limits their applicability in low-resource primary care settings, where data is sparse, heavily censored, and comprises only basic clinical covariates. Adapting these models is essential to extend the benefits of precision medicine to broader, underserved populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal survival model, explicitly designed to handle strong feature sparsity and informative censoring, can reliably estimate HTEs from the limited data available in primary care, maintaining performance comparable to models trained on richer datasets.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RIST framework for sparse data by integrating a two-stage architecture. First, we will use a novel, lightweight imputation model based on a hidden Markov model to handle missing covariates and informative censoring patterns common in primary care records. Second, we will train a simplified survival forest for HTE estimation, using a causal splitting criterion (e.g., based on the difference in expected survival under treatment vs. control) but restricting splits to a small, clinically interpretable set of core covariates (e.g., age, basic labs, comorbidities). We will benchmark against standard RIST and Cox-based methods using semi-synthetic data derived from the UK Biobank and a primary care EHR dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical method for HTE estimation in low-resource clinical settings, directly enabling more personalized treatment decisions in primary care. It will also contribute methodologically by showing how to integrate causal inference and survival analysis under severe data constraints, a largely unexplored challenge.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a light-weight imputation mechanism for hidden covariate states with a pared-down causal survival forest, creating an end-to-end pipeline specifically for the high-sparsity, high-censorship regime of primary care, which existing HTE methods do not address.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Causal-Survival Framework for Real-Time Pandemic Risk Stratification and Early Warning
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for robust, real-time surveillance systems that can move beyond simple case counts to predict severe outcomes and healthcare strain. Existing platforms often lack formal causal and time-to-event (survival) modeling, limiting their ability to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (e.g., of interventions like vaccination) on hospitalization risk from noisy, censored, real-world data streams.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a hybrid model that combines causal inference for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation with flexible survival analysis for time-to-event prediction will significantly improve the accuracy and actionability of real-time pandemic risk forecasts at a national scale.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) method from Shalit's prior work to operate on high-frequency, streaming data with hidden confounders. The framework will integrate a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer module to model temporal dependencies in surveillance covariates, feeding into a causal survival forest for estimating region-specific effects of interventions (like mask mandates or booster campaigns) on ICU admission risk. The system will be designed for integration with platforms like the WHO's Epidemic Intelligence from Open Sources (EIOS), using online learning to update estimates as new data arrives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available software package for causal survival analysis in streaming data contexts and a validated benchmark for next-generation early warning systems. It will demonstrate a method to quantify the real-time impact of public health interventions on severe outcome risk, enabling more targeted resource allocation and policy responses.
proposals.novelty: While causal survival and offline hybrid models exist, their integration into a real-time, national-scale surveillance pipeline that handles streaming data with potential hidden confounding is unexplored. This bridges the gap between rigorous causal inference methodology and operational public health intelligence.

proposals.title: Inference of Population-Level Case-Fatality Rates from Individualized Risk Score Distributions: A Causal Deconvolution Framework
proposals.motivation: In clinical epidemiology and health policy, accurate population-level case-fatality rates (CFRs) are crucial for resource allocation and evaluating interventions. However, direct CFR estimation from observational data is often confounded by selection bias, incomplete follow-up, and heterogeneous patient populations. Pre-2026 risk prediction models provide individualized scores, but aggregating them to unbiased CFRs remains an unsolved inverse problem with significant practical implications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the population-level CFR can be robustly inferred from the observed distribution of individual risk scores by explicitly modeling the causal relationship between covariates, treatment assignment, and the censored time-to-event outcome, thereby deconvolving the selection mechanisms that bias direct estimation.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as estimating the average treatment effect (ATE) on survival from observational data where the 'treatment' is a latent class of fatal disease progression. Building on Shalit's work on survival heterogeneous treatment effect estimation with censored data, we will develop a deconvolution estimator. The approach will combine a survival score learner (e.g., a recursively imputed survival forest or a deep Cox model) with a causal inference framework for unobserved confounding, using techniques like sensitivity analysis or instrumental variable methods adapted for risk score distributions. Validation will use semi-synthetic benchmarks based on real-world ICU datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV) where ground-truth CFRs can be simulated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between individual risk prediction and population-level estimands, yielding a validated tool for CFR estimation from biased observational cohorts. It will extend causal survival analysis to settings where the primary outcome of interest is an aggregate statistic derived from a predictive model's output.
proposals.novelty: While risk scores are widely used for stratification, directly inverting their distribution to recover a causal population parameter is a novel task. The method's novelty lies in integrating survival prediction, causal deconvolution, and sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounding specific to this inverse problem.

proposals.title: From Individualized Risk to Population-Level Demand: A Causal Survival Framework for Forecasting Hospital Resource Needs
proposals.motivation: Accurate forecasting of hospital bed and ICU demand is critical for operational planning and public health response, as starkly highlighted by the COVID-19 pandemic. Existing epidemiological models often lack individual-level clinical granularity, while standard machine learning models for patient risk prediction do not aggregate predictions to account for treatment effect heterogeneity and competing risks, leading to suboptimal resource projections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aggregating individualized, time-to-event predictions of clinical deterioration—which incorporate heterogeneous treatment effects and competing risks—will yield more accurate and granular forecasts of short-term hospital resource demand than models based on population averages or simple risk scores.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage causal survival framework. First, we will adapt methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) or Cox-based models with time-varying covariates to estimate individualized Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE) on time-to-ICU admission or discharge, using large-scale electronic health records. Second, we will build a probabilistic forecasting model that simulates the future patient cohort, sampling event times from the estimated individualized survival distributions (factual and counterfactual) under different policy scenarios, and aggregate these to produce demand trajectories with uncertainty intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, clinically-grounded methodology for translating patient-level predictive models into operational forecasts. It will contribute a new benchmark for resource demand prediction that explicitly accounts for treatment heterogeneity and censoring, bridging the gap between causal machine learning for personalized medicine and healthcare operations research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation from causal survival analysis into a probabilistic forecasting model for aggregate resource demand, moving beyond risk stratification to simulate the outcomes of a heterogeneous population under dynamic clinical management.

proposals.title: Adversarial Concept Maximization: A Framework for Sensitivity Analysis in Survival and Treatment Effect Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and causal inference, understanding a model's sensitivity to protected or confounding variables (e.g., socio-economic status, hospital site) is crucial for fairness and robustness. Pre-2026 adversarial removal methods aim to create invariant representations, but they do not quantify how much a specific concept *could* influence predictions if it were not controlled for. A method to systematically maximize a concept's encoding would provide a powerful tool for sensitivity analysis, revealing the upper bound of a variable's potential impact on outcomes like survival or treatment effect estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the adversarial objective from removal to maximization, we can learn representations that are maximally informative of a target concept, and the performance gap between standard and concept-maximized models quantifies the latent sensitivity of the prediction task to that concept.
proposals.method: We will adapt the adversarial framework of concept-removal, replacing the adversarial classifier's minimization objective with a maximization one. For survival outcomes, we will integrate this into a deep Cox proportional hazards or DeepHit architecture, where the adversarial component strives to perfectly predict the sensitive concept from the learned representations. We will extend this to heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation for time-to-event data, building on prior work with recursively imputed trees, to see how maximally encoding a confounder inflates or alters the estimated Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for evaluating the vulnerability of clinical risk and causal models to specific variables. It will yield a concrete sensitivity metric—the 'adversarial influence score'—and demonstrate its utility in benchmarks involving biased observational data, such as electronic health records, where hidden confounding is a major concern.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing is well-established, systematically inverting its core objective to perform a worst-case sensitivity analysis is a novel and non-obvious direction. Applying this inversion to survival analysis and causal inference for time-to-event outcomes, key themes in Shalit's research, represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Trees: A Non-Linear Framework for Hierarchical Interventions in Concept-Based Model Explanations
proposals.motivation: Concept-based explanations, like those from Concept Activation Vectors (CAVs), often assume linear interventions to manipulate model behavior, which is a severe simplification. Real-world concepts are hierarchical and interact non-linearly (e.g., 'medical severity' influences 'treatment choice' and 'outcome'). This linearity assumption limits the fidelity and actionability of explanations for high-stakes domains like clinical prediction, where understanding complex feature interactions is critical for trust and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling interventions on learned concepts as non-linear, hierarchical functions will yield more accurate and causally faithful explanations of deep neural network predictions than standard linear concept interventions.
proposals.method: We will extend the adversarial framework of CAVs by developing a structured latent variable model. Concepts will be represented as nodes in a learned hierarchical graph, where edges encode non-linear relationships (using MLPs). Interventions will be performed via targeted perturbations to parent nodes, propagating effects through the graph. We will adapt survival and counterfactual inference techniques from Shalit's prior work (e.g., Recursively Imputed Trees) to estimate the heterogeneous effect of these hierarchical concept interventions on model outputs, benchmarking against linear baselines on clinical text and time-to-event data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for non-linear concept intervention, enhancing the realism of model explanations. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating causal concept-based explanations in NLP and healthcare AI. The resulting tools will allow researchers to ask more nuanced 'what-if' questions about model behavior.
proposals.novelty: While concept-based explanations exist, they lack a formal causal, non-linear intervention calculus. This proposal uniquely integrates hierarchical latent modeling from causal inference with the adversarial concept framework to move beyond linear additive interventions.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Disentanglement for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Clinical Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational clinical data, especially time-to-event outcomes, is crucial for personalized medicine. A core challenge arises when the clinical concept of interest (e.g., 'disease severity') is deeply entangled with other patient features in the data representation, leading to biased effect estimates. Pre-2026 methods for causal inference and concept-based explainability often assume concepts are separable, which fails in complex, high-dimensional medical records.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the entanglement of a target clinical concept with nuisance covariates within a latent representation, and performing targeted causal adjustment on this subspace, will yield less biased and more interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects on survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel multi-task neural survival model with a structured latent space. The architecture will use a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework with a concept-annotated auxiliary dataset to learn a latent representation where the subspace for the target concept is forced to be predictive of the concept label but is statistically regularized to be minimally informative of other observed covariates. For HTE estimation, we will adapt the RIST (Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) method to perform targeted regularization and adjustment using only the entangled concept subspace, treating other latent dimensions as hidden confounding proxies. The model will be trained with a survival loss and a causal regularization term derived from pre-2026 proximal causal inference literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new method for causal estimation when key concepts are not measurable in isolation, extending the toolkit for off-policy evaluation in clinical settings. It will provide a benchmark framework for evaluating concept entanglement in real-world tasks like analyzing the effect of extended embryo culture, where 'embryo quality' is entangled with lab conditions and patient factors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in bridging causal inference with representation learning for *non-separable* concepts, moving beyond simple concept bottleneck models. It uniquely combines the strengths of Shalit's previous work on RIST for survival HTEs and hidden covariate adjustment with a structured disentanglement objective for robust causal estimation.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests for Complex Mediation and Interaction: A Framework for High-Dimensional Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival analysis, such as Shalit's Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST), primarily address simple treatment-outcome relationships. However, real-world clinical decisions often involve complex causal pathways with mediators, time-varying confounders, and interactions between multiple treatments or patient concepts. The efficacy of modern causal survival forests in these richer, more realistic graph structures remains largely unexplored, limiting their practical utility for complex clinical prediction and treatment personalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling complex causal structures—specifically mediation and interaction between multiple latent concepts—within a forest-based framework, we can significantly improve the accuracy and interpretability of HTE estimation for time-to-event data compared to methods that ignore such complexity.
proposals.method: We will extend the RIST framework to incorporate structural causal models (SCMs) with multiple treatments and latent variables. The approach will involve: 1) Developing a novel splitting criterion that maximizes the reduction in expected variance of the conditional average treatment effect (CATE) function, informed by a user-specified causal graph (e.g., including mediators M). 2) Integrating targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust estimators within leaf nodes to adjust for high-dimensional covariates and complex confounding as implied by the graph. 3) Validating the method using semi-synthetic benchmarks generated from real-world datasets (e.g., SEER, MIMIC-IV) where the ground-truth graph is known, and comparing performance against baseline RIST and other causal survival learners.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, flexible framework for estimating survival HTEs under complex causal assumptions, directly addressing a gap in the clinical machine learning toolkit. It will yield a new open-source algorithm and a set of benchmark datasets for evaluating causal survival methods in structured settings, advancing the field towards more credible and actionable clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While existing causal forests handle simple confounding, the integration of explicit, user-defined causal graphs (with mediation/interaction) into the recursive partitioning process for survival data is novel. This moves beyond covariate adjustment to structural learning within a non-parametric, high-performance estimation framework.

proposals.title: Causal Explanation of Language Model Outputs for Question Answering and Summarization via Structured Covariate Adjustment
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 NLP models, especially large language models (LLMs), are opaque and can produce outputs influenced by spurious correlations in training data (e.g., demographic biases, dataset artifacts). Existing interpretability methods like attention or feature attribution lack a causal framework, making it difficult to robustly quantify the effect of specific concepts (e.g., a patient's age in a clinical note) on a model's generated answer or summary. This limits the safe deployment of NLP in high-stakes domains like clinical decision support or legal analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the CausaLM framework—which uses structured causal models and do-calculus for text—to conditional text generation tasks, we can causally attribute variations in model outputs (e.g., answer correctness, summary toxicity) to specific input concepts, moving beyond associative explanations to more robust causal estimates.
proposals.method: We will extend the CausaLM architecture, which treats text as a structured set of concept variables, to encoder-decoder and decoder-only models. For a task like medical QA, we define a structural causal model where concepts (e.g., 'symptom severity', 'patient age') are intervened upon via an adapted do-calculus using concept-swapping interventions in the embedding space. We will employ targeted masking and controlled generation to estimate the average causal effect (ACE) of a concept on output properties, validated using semi-synthetic datasets with known ground-truth causal structures and biased real-world benchmarks (e.g., BioASQ, CNN/DailyMail).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for causal explanation in open-ended NLP generation, yielding a new benchmark for causal interpretability. It will produce a method to audit and debias generative models by identifying concept-level drivers of undesirable outputs, directly contributing to more reliable and transparent NLP systems in clinical and other sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: While CausaLM and similar methods have been applied to classification, applying structured causal inference to explain the causal mechanisms behind generated text sequences—such as answers or summaries—is an unexplored and non-trivial extension, requiring new formulations of interventions and effect estimation for autoregressive outputs.

proposals.title: The Bias-Variance-Utility Trade-off in Causal Representation Learning for Clinical Risk Prediction
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks, models trained on observational data often learn spurious correlations from confounding variables, leading to biased estimates of treatment effects and poor generalization. While methods for learning counterfactual representations (e.g., TARNet, CFRNet) aim to remove this bias, they can degrade primary task performance, a critical trade-off not systematically quantified. Understanding this trade-off is essential for deploying reliable models in healthcare, where both accurate prediction and unbiased causal understanding are needed for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of bias removal enforced by counterfactual representation learning exhibits a predictable, task-dependent trade-off with primary prediction accuracy, and that this relationship can be characterized by a Pareto frontier influenced by the strength of confounding and the overlap in covariate distributions.
proposals.method: We will design a simulation framework to generate observational data with known, tunable confounding strength and overlap. We will benchmark a spectrum of representation learning architectures, from standard predictive models (e.g., deep Cox models for survival) to strongly regularized causal models (CFRNet, Dragonnet). For each, we will jointly measure primary task performance (e.g., time-dependent C-index) and causal bias (e.g., Precision in Estimation of Heterogeneous Effect) across simulation settings. We will then analyze this joint distribution to formalize the trade-off and propose a diagnostic for optimal model selection in real-world tasks, such as estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event outcomes from electronic health records.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic empirical characterization of the bias-accuracy trade-off in causal representation learning for clinical prediction. It will yield practical guidance for researchers and clinicians on selecting and tuning models based on the confounding structure of their data. The proposed diagnostic framework will advance methodology for robust machine learning in healthcare by making the cost of debiasing explicit and actionable.
proposals.novelty: While the bias-variance trade-off is classic, its formalization in the context of modern counterfactual representation learning for joint prediction and causal inference is novel. The systematic quantification of how enforced balancing of hidden covariates impacts concrete clinical prediction utility moves beyond evaluating causal estimators solely on their bias reduction.

proposals.title: Causal Estimation of Treatment Effects from Clinical Text Using Instrumental Variable Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Estimating causal treatment effects from observational clinical data is critical for personalized medicine but is often confounded by unobserved patient factors. Clinical text (e.g., physician notes) contains rich information on patient state and treatment decisions, but standard methods struggle with unmeasured confounding present in these high-dimensional, unstructured data. Pre-2026, instrumental variable (IV) methods from econometrics address unobserved confounding but are rarely integrated with deep learning for text, creating a significant methodological gap for clinical prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning architecture that jointly learns representations of clinical text and incorporates an instrumental variable (e.g., physician prescribing preference encoded in institutional patterns) can consistently estimate heterogeneous treatment effects in the presence of unobserved confounders.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel neural network architecture combining a text encoder (e.g., BERT or ClinicalBERT) with a Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) deep learning framework. The first stage will predict the treatment assignment from the text and a valid, pre-identified instrument. The second stage will predict the outcome (e.g., survival time, using a hazard network) using the text and the *predicted* treatment from the first stage, effectively purging confounding. We will adapt recent advances in deep IV and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation (e.g., building on DR-learner frameworks) to handle censored time-to-event outcomes, aligning with Shalit's prior work on survival and causal inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for causal inference from unstructured clinical text, a domain where valid instruments are often more plausible than the assumption of no unmeasured confounding. It will extend the toolkit for precision medicine by enabling the estimation of who benefits from a treatment based on textual patient histories, even with hidden confounding. The resulting software and benchmarks will advance the integration of econometric and machine learning causality research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of instrumental variable econometrics with deep text representation learning specifically for clinical causal estimation, moving beyond standard propensity score methods. Furthermore, adapting this framework to handle censored survival outcomes, a core challenge in clinical research, represents a significant extension of existing deep IV methods.

proposals.title: Learning Environment-Invariant Representations for Causal Survival Prediction
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event data is critical for personalized medicine but is confounded by spurious correlations, such as between patient demographics and care protocols across hospitals. Pre-2026, methods like Recursively Imputed Trees can handle censoring but may learn unstable predictors that fail under distribution shifts, limiting clinical applicability. Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) offers a principled framework to learn robust predictors, yet its application to complex, censored survival outcomes remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting IRM to survival analysis, we can learn representations of patient state that are invariant to spurious clinical environment features, leading to more robust and generalizable estimates of individualized treatment effects on survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend the IRMv1 penalty to the partial likelihood of a Cox proportional hazards model, regularizing the learned patient representation to have an invariant relationship with the hazard across pre-defined environments (e.g., hospitals). The model will be implemented as a neural network with a representation-learning backbone. We will benchmark against established methods like Bayesian Causal Forests and our prior Recursively Imputed Trees on semi-synthetic and real-world datasets (e.g., SEER, MIMIC) with simulated distribution shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for environment-invariant causal survival analysis, directly addressing the problem of spurious correlations in observational clinical data. It will yield a new, open-source benchmark for evaluating the robustness of treatment effect estimators under distribution shift, advancing the field towards more reliable clinical decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of IRM's theoretical guarantees for out-of-distribution generalization with the statistical challenges of right-censored survival data and causal inference, a combination not addressed in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond standard predictive modeling to learn causally relevant, stable representations for survival.

proposals.title: Concept-Aware Style Transfer for Counterfactual Generation in Clinical Text: A Causal Framework for Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks, estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) from observational text data (e.g., physician notes) is challenging due to confounding. Pre-2026, counterfactual generation for structured data is established, but methods for text are nascent. Generating realistic, concept-altered counterfactual text is crucial for explaining model predictions and performing causal inference on unstructured clinical narratives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting text style transfer models with explicit causal constraints can generate valid, realistic counterfactual clinical text by altering specific treatment or covariate concepts, thereby enabling more accurate estimation of treatment effects from observational text data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based style transfer architecture (e.g., inspired by pre-2026 models like Delete-Retrieve-Generate or controlled attribute models). The key innovation is to treat treatment assignment as a 'style' and condition generation on latent representations of confounding covariates (e.g., age, comorbidities) inferred via a variational autoencoder. We will integrate a causal regularization loss, based on the backdoor adjustment principle, to ensure generated counterfactuals adhere to the identified causal graph. The model will be trained on clinical text (e.g., MIMIC-III notes) paired with structured EHR data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for performing causal inference directly on clinical text, extending HTE estimation methods to high-dimensional unstructured data. It will yield a tool for generating interpretable, concept-altered counterfactuals to audit clinical prediction models and explore 'what-if' scenarios in treatment pathways.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of causal inference principles with text style transfer for counterfactual generation, moving beyond simple sentiment or formality changes to explicitly model and alter clinically-relevant treatment concepts while controlling for confounders.

proposals.title: SCALE: Scalable Causal Learning for Estimating Hundreds of Treatment Effects from Observational Survival Data
proposals.motivation: Modern clinical and epidemiological datasets contain rich, high-dimensional covariate information, enabling the estimation of causal effects for many potential interventions or patient subgroups simultaneously. However, existing methods for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, especially in time-to-event settings with censoring, are computationally intensive and designed for analyzing one or a few treatments. This bottleneck prevents the systematic, data-driven discovery of many potential treatment-concept interactions from large-scale observational data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel computational architecture, combining shared representation learning with a forest-based, recursively imputed survival model, can efficiently and accurately estimate hundreds of distinct conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) from a single observational dataset with censored outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task deep learning framework where a shared neural network encoder learns a low-dimensional representation from high-dimensional covariates. For each of hundreds of binary treatment concepts, a separate, lightweight output head will map this shared representation to an individual treatment effect, parameterized via a Recursively Imputed Survival Tree (RIST) model for time-to-event outcomes. The training will employ a targeted regularization scheme on the shared weights to prevent overfitting to any single treatment and leverage semi-synthetic benchmarking frameworks to validate performance across concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable software and methodological framework for mass multi-concept causal analysis in survival settings. It will enable large-scale screening for heterogeneous treatment effects across many medical interventions, patient characteristics, or environmental exposures from a single model fit, significantly accelerating hypothesis generation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a shared representation architecture—common in multi-task prediction—with the rigorous, censoring-aware RIST framework for causal survival analysis, scaling it to hundreds of treatment variables, a problem not addressed by prior single-treatment or low-dimensional propensity score methods.

proposals.title: Lightweight Counterfactual Representation Networks for On-Device Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized clinical decision support. However, state-of-the-art counterfactual representation models are computationally intensive, limiting their deployment to server-side analysis. For real-time, privacy-preserving use at the point-of-care (e.g., on mobile devices or embedded medical hardware), a highly efficient model is required.
