proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully distilled counterfactual representation network can retain robust performance for HTE estimation while achieving a >10x reduction in model size and latency, making it suitable for edge deployment.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CFRNet architecture, a prominent method for learning balanced representations for counterfactual inference. Using knowledge distillation, we will train a compact student network (e.g., a small multi-layer perceptron) to mimic the output distributions and, crucially, the latent representation space of a pre-trained, complex teacher CFRNet. We will incorporate a tailored distillation loss that penalizes discrepancies in the representation's ability to balance covariate distributions between treatment groups, preserving the causal property. The final model will be optimized for mobile frameworks like TensorFlow Lite.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first framework for compressing causal inference models for on-device use, enabling real-time personalized treatment effect predictions. It will provide a benchmark for model efficiency versus performance trade-offs in causal machine learning and open avenues for deploying causal algorithms in resource-constrained clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While model compression is well-studied for predictive tasks, its application to preserve the specific theoretical properties (e.g., covariate balancing) required for valid causal estimation from observational data is novel and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Clinical Concepts from Model Bias in Survival and Treatment Effect Predictors
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models for clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation often exhibit systematic bias, performing poorly on specific patient subgroups. Pre-2026, methods for algorithmic fairness and model debugging typically require pre-specifying protected attributes or concepts. In clinical settings, the underlying concepts causing bias—such as unmeasured comorbidities, social determinants of health, or latent disease subtypes—are often unknown, limiting our ability to audit and improve models.
proposals.hypothesis: The systematic bias patterns of a predictive model (e.g., in survival analysis or heterogeneous treatment effect estimation) can be decomposed to automatically infer the latent clinical concepts that are misrepresented or causally linked to the model's failure modes.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that treats the biased model's error distribution as an observation. First, we will use representation learning to disentangle the latent space of a deep survival or causal model (building on Shalit's previous work). Second, we will apply causal structure discovery algorithms (e.g., variants of the PC algorithm or recent differentiable causal discovery methods) to the joint distribution of inputs, latent representations, and model errors to identify latent variables that are parents of the error term. Third, we will validate inferred concepts by aligning them with external, partially observed clinical covariates using interpretability techniques.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel diagnostic tool for clinical AI, moving from detecting *that* a model is biased to explaining *why*. It will contribute a method for latent concept discovery in biased offline data, relevant for auditing models in electronic health records. The work will extend causal inference and survival analysis methodologies by formally linking model bias to latent causal structures.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior bias detection work, this inverts the problem: instead of testing for bias on known concepts, it infers the concepts themselves. It uniquely combines causal discovery with representation learning for model debugging in a clinical context, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Active Causal Learning: Strategic Data Collection for Unbiased Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine but is often biased by covariate shift and confounding. Standard data collection is passive, leading to inefficient use of resources and persistent bias in key subpopulations. A method to actively guide data acquisition using intermediate causal estimates could dramatically improve efficiency and reduce bias in real-world clinical studies.
proposals.hypothesis: That an active learning framework, which uses preliminary estimates of the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) and its associated uncertainty to prioritize the collection of new samples, will yield a final estimator with lower bias and variance than one trained on a passively collected dataset of equivalent size.
proposals.method: We will develop a sequential, two-stage algorithm. First, an initial CATE model (e.g., Causal Forest or a T-learner) is trained on available biased data. Second, we define an acquisition function based on the estimated CATE variance and the density of the covariate space, formalized via a Bayesian optimization or uncertainty sampling approach. This function will guide the selection of new patient covariates for targeted data collection (e.g., measuring their outcomes). The process iterates, refining the CATE model with the augmented dataset. We will benchmark against passive collection using semi-synthetic datasets based on real clinical covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel active learning framework specifically designed for causal inference tasks, moving beyond standard supervised learning. It will deliver a practical algorithm for optimizing data collection budgets in clinical and epidemiological studies. The resulting benchmarks will demonstrate clear reductions in estimation error for HTEs, particularly in underrepresented subgroups.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established for prediction, its application to guide data collection for *causal effect* estimation is largely unexplored. The key novelty is the acquisition function that jointly optimizes for reducing uncertainty in the CATE function and correcting for covariate imbalance, rather than just model prediction error.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Analysis from Clinical Notes: A Domain-Adapted Transformer for Low-Resource Medical Subspecialties
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects on time-to-event outcomes from real-world data is crucial for personalized medicine, but many clinical domains lack the structured, high-volume data required by modern methods. Clinical text (e.g., oncology or rheumatology notes) contains rich prognostic information, but subspecialty corpora are often small, making large pre-trained language models prone to overfitting and poor calibration. This limits the application of state-of-the-art causal survival estimators in these valuable, data-scarce settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based architecture, explicitly adapted for small domain-specific text corpora and integrated with a doubly-robust causal survival estimator, can reliably estimate heterogeneous treatment effects from clinical notes while outperforming both standard fine-tuning of large models and traditional structured-data approaches.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained clinical language model (like ClinicalBERT) using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) on the small target corpus to prevent catastrophic forgetting. The adapted model's embeddings will be used as high-dimensional covariates in a Recursively Imputed Survival Tree (RIST) framework, a method developed by Shalit's group for handling censored data. We will extend RIST to incorporate a targeted learning step using doubly-robust estimation for the survival outcomes, reducing bias from model misspecification. Evaluation will use benchmark clinical note datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III oncology subsets) with simulated and real treatment assignments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated pipeline for applying causal survival analysis to low-resource text domains, expanding the reach of personalized treatment effect estimation. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating causal inference methods on clinical text and demonstrate the synergy between parameter-efficient NLP adaptation and robust causal estimation.
proposals.novelty: While causal survival analysis and clinical NLP are active fields, their combination for low-resource domains is unexplored. The novelty lies in the tailored integration of parameter-efficient domain adaptation with a doubly-robust, tree-based causal survival estimator specifically designed for the high-dimensional, noisy covariate space derived from small text corpora.

proposals.title: Robust Concept-based Causal Estimation (Robust CaCE) for Treatment Effect Heterogeneity with Noisy or Weakly Supervised Concepts
proposals.motivation: Concept-based models like Causal Concept Embedding (CaCE) improve the interpretability and transportability of heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation by grounding predictions in human-understandable concepts. However, their reliance on perfectly annotated concept labels for each unit is a major practical bottleneck, as such annotations are often noisy, sparse, or derived from weak supervision (e.g., noisy heuristics, distant labels) in real-world clinical and observational datasets. This limits the applicability of CaCE in precisely the high-stakes domains where interpretable causal reasoning is most needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the noise process in concept annotations and integrating weak supervision signals, we can develop a robust CaCE framework that recovers accurate and interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects, even when perfect concept labels are unavailable.
proposals.method: We will extend the CaCE framework by treating the true concepts as latent variables. We will model the relationship between observed noisy/weak labels and the latent concepts using a probabilistic model (e.g., a confusion matrix or a variational autoencoder). For estimation, we will develop a multi-task learning objective that jointly learns a de-noised concept representation and the treatment effect function, potentially using architectures like concept bottleneck networks with a latent concept layer. The framework will be adapted for time-to-event outcomes with censoring, leveraging Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed survival trees, to handle clinical survival data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first method for concept-based causal estimation robust to annotation imperfections, significantly broadening the practical utility of interpretable HTE models. It will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating causal concept learning under label noise and a software tool that integrates with existing causal and survival analysis pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While methods exist for learning with noisy labels in standard prediction, and for causal estimation with unobserved confounders, the specific integration of noise-robust concept learning within a structured causal framework for HTE estimation is novel. This moves beyond the standard missing data or measurement error models by formally incorporating weak supervision sources common in applied clinical research.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Concept Effect Estimation via Concept-Aware Variational Latent Adjustment
proposals.motivation: Estimating the Causal Concept Effect (CaCE) is crucial for interpretable causal analysis in high-dimensional data like medical images. Current methods rely on generative models with perfectly disentangled latent spaces, an unrealistic assumption in practice. When latent representations are entangled, naive CaCE estimates are biased, leading to incorrect conclusions about the causal effect of concepts on outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the residual entanglement between a target concept and other latent factors, we can develop a robust estimator that corrects for this confounding, yielding consistent CaCE estimates even from imperfect generative models.
proposals.method: We will extend the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework by introducing a concept-aware encoder that outputs a target concept variable and a residual latent vector. A regularization loss will encourage disentanglement, but we will not assume it is perfect. For CaCE estimation, we will treat the residual latents as observed pre-treatment covariates and apply doubly robust estimators (e.g., using targeted maximum likelihood estimation or a causal forest adapted for survival outcomes) to adjust for confounding between the intervened concept and the outcome. The method will be validated on semi-synthetic medical image datasets with known ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for CaCE estimation under realistic, non-disentangled latent spaces. It will deliver a practical, robust estimator that integrates modern deep generative modeling with established causal adjustment techniques, enhancing the reliability of concept-based causal claims in clinical prediction and treatment effect heterogeneity studies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that assumes perfect disentanglement or focuses solely on representation learning, our approach directly addresses the resulting causal estimation bias, bridging causal inference and representation learning in a novel way specific to the CaCE task.

proposals.title: Direct Estimation of Causal Concept Effects via Targeted Regularization in Latent Space
proposals.motivation: Estimating the causal effect of high-level concepts (e.g., 'tumor texture' in an image) on outcomes is crucial for interpretable AI in medicine. Current methods often require training a separate generative model to simulate interventions on concepts, which is computationally expensive, prone to misspecification, and introduces new biases. A method that bypasses this generative step would be more robust and scalable for clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal effect of a latent concept on a downstream outcome can be directly estimated by learning a representation where the concept is aligned with a disentangled subspace and applying targeted regularization that mimics an intervention, without constructing a full generative model.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Causal Effect Variational Autoencoders (CEVAEs) prevalent pre-2026. The key innovation is a novel regularization loss that directly enforces the *do-operator* in the latent space. For a learned concept subspace Z_c and other covariates Z_x, we will minimize a contrastive loss that pushes the distribution of Z_c across treatment groups to be identical, simulating an intervention, while preserving the relationship between Z_x and the outcome. Estimation will use targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust estimators on the regularized latent representations to compute the causal effect of Z_c on time-to-event or binary clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more efficient framework for concept-based causal inference that is directly applicable to clinical survival analysis and treatment effect heterogeneity. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating concept effect estimation without generative models, advancing the field of interpretable causal machine learning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing approaches that rely on training an explicit interventional generative model, our method inverts this need by enforcing interventional constraints directly within the representation learning objective, a non-obvious fusion of causal regularization and latent variable models.

proposals.title: Generalized Concept-based Causal Explanation (gCaCE): A Framework for Continuous and Multi-Valued Concepts in Treatment Effect Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Causal concept effect (CaCE) estimation has emerged as a powerful tool for explaining heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) using high-level, interpretable concepts (e.g., 'tumor aggressiveness'). However, existing methods are largely restricted to binary concepts, which is a severe limitation. In clinical and biological settings, key explanatory factors—such as disease severity, biomarker intensity, or physiological reserve—are inherently continuous or ordinal. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in methods that can leverage these richer, non-binary concepts to provide more nuanced and accurate explanations of why treatments work better for some patients than others.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generalizing the CaCE framework to handle continuous and multi-valued concepts will yield more granular, clinically meaningful explanations of treatment effect heterogeneity without sacrificing identifiability or estimation performance, provided the concept measurement model is appropriately regularized.
proposals.method: We will extend the structural causal model for CaCE, defining the concept as a latent variable with a continuous or ordered categorical domain. Building on Uri Shalit's expertise in survival and causal methods, we will develop a multi-task deep learning architecture. This architecture will jointly learn a representation for predicting the treatment effect (via a TARNet or Dragonnet variant for time-to-event outcomes) and a regularized concept predictor. The key technical innovation will be the integration of a concept-consistency penalty, inspired by counterfactual invariance principles, to ensure the learned concept representation is causally meaningful with respect to the treatment. Estimation will use observed, pre-treatment covariates and leverage flexible semi-parametric models for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and estimation method for concept-based causal explanation with non-binary concepts. It will expand the practical utility of interpretable HTE analysis in domains like oncology and cardiology, where continuous biomarkers are prevalent. We will release an open-source software package and validate the method on both semi-synthetic benchmarks and a real-world clinical dataset involving continuous physiological measurements.
proposals.novelty: While recent work has focused on binary CaCE, the generalization to continuous concepts is non-trivial, requiring novel identifiability arguments and a tailored learning objective that balances predictive accuracy for the outcome with the causal validity of the continuous concept representation, moving beyond simple discretization.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Contrast Estimation Under Latent Confounding via Concept-Aware Sensitivity Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous causal effects from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine, but standard methods assume no unmeasured confounding. In clinical settings, confounders like disease severity or socioeconomic status are often unobserved, biasing effect estimates. This problem is acute for time-to-event outcomes, where censoring further complicates identification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the influence of unobserved, high-level concepts (e.g., 'frailty', 'care access') within a sensitivity analysis framework, we can produce more robust and interpretable bounds on Conditional Average Causal Effects (CaCE) in survival and other clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the concept of *hidden covariates* by integrating a sensitivity model for latent concepts into a survival causal forest framework. The method will first learn a representation of observed proxies for the unmeasured concept using variational autoencoders. Then, we will adapt the *R-learner* for survival outcomes, incorporating a sensitivity parameter that governs the residual correlation between the latent concept and the treatment assignment. Estimation will proceed via a novel, recursively imputed algorithm that iteratively updates the latent concept and the effect bounds under varying sensitivity assumptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for quantifying the robustness of CaCE estimates to unmeasured confounding in clinical studies. It will advance the field of causal survival analysis by moving beyond the 'no unmeasured confounding' assumption towards a more realistic, sensitivity-based paradigm, directly applicable to domains like IVF treatment evaluation or oncology.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis exists, its integration with machine learning methods for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, specifically for censored outcomes and framed around confounding *concepts*, is novel. This bridges causal inference with representation learning in a principled way to handle a core, unsolved limitation in observational clinical research.

proposals.title: Causal Effect Estimation with Generative Adversarial Networks and Normalizing Flows: Extending the Causal Effect Variational Autoencoder Framework
proposals.motivation: The Causal Effect Variational Autoencoder (CEVAE) provides a powerful deep learning framework for estimating individual treatment effects from observational data with hidden confounders. However, its reliance on the VAE architecture inherits known limitations, such as potentially blurry sample generation and a restrictive variational posterior family. Exploring alternative generative models, which have shown superior performance in density estimation and sample fidelity in other domains, could lead to more accurate and robust causal estimators, directly impacting clinical prediction and treatment effect heterogeneity analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the CaCE estimation framework with generative adversarial networks (GANs) and normalizing flows (NFs) will yield more accurate estimates of individual treatment effects, particularly in survival and time-to-event settings with complex, high-dimensional covariates, by better modeling the underlying data distribution and latent confounder space.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CaCE framework's core components—inference network, generative model, and outcome model—to GAN and NF architectures. For GANs, we will design a framework akin to the Causal Effect GAN (CEGAN) concept, using the discriminator to enforce consistency between the generated factual and counterfactual distributions. For NFs, we will employ invertible networks to model the exact likelihood of the latent confounder and data distribution, enabling precise sampling of counterfactuals. We will benchmark these models against CEVAE and standard causal methods on established semi-synthetic datasets (e.g., IHDP, ACIC) and real clinical data from studies on treatment effect heterogeneity in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with new, rigorously evaluated tools for causal inference with hidden confounding, extending the generative modeling toolbox beyond VAEs. It will deliver practical algorithms for clinical researchers to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects from complex observational data, potentially improving the personalization of treatment strategies. The benchmarks will clarify the relative strengths of VAE, GAN, and NF approaches in causal contexts.
proposals.novelty: While GANs and NFs have been explored for causal inference (e.g., for generating synthetic control data), a systematic extension and comparison of the CaCE framework using these models for *estimation* under hidden confounding is novel. The integration focuses explicitly on preserving the identifiable causal structure of CEVAE while leveraging the distinct advantages of adversarial training and exact likelihood models.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Interrogation and Intervention: A Survival Analysis Framework for Latent Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Intervening on latent concepts in generative models (e.g., VAEs, GANs) is crucial for interpretability, fairness, and controlled generation. Pre-2026 methods, like latent traversal or supervised concept vectors, are often correlational, failing to isolate causal concept representations from entangled, spurious features. This limits their reliability for high-stakes applications, such as generating counterfactual medical images for treatment effect visualization, where clean interventions are necessary.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing concept discovery as a causal inference problem with time-to-event (survival) outcomes will yield more robust, disentangled concept representations, enabling interventions that better preserve data integrity and isolate specific semantic attributes.
proposals.method: We will adapt concepts from Shalit's work on heterogeneous treatment effects with survival outcomes. First, we treat the latent space as a set of potential 'treatments' and train a survival model (e.g., a Deep Survival Machine or Cox-Time network) to predict a time-to-event outcome (e.g., time to a specific clinical event in medical data) from the latent code. We will then use a causal forest variant for survival data to identify latent dimensions with significant heterogeneous treatment effects on the outcome. These dimensions will be treated as candidate causal concepts. For intervention, we will develop a targeted regularization loss during model training that encourages the identified dimensions to be maximally informative of the causal effect while being minimally predictive of spurious covariates, drawing on ideas from counterfactual inference and hidden covariate balancing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, causality-grounded framework for concept discovery and intervention in generative models. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating the robustness of concept-based interventions, particularly for clinical and survival data. The resulting methods will enable more reliable generation of counterfactual scenarios for explaining model predictions and simulating treatment effects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging causal survival analysis—a core strength of the researcher—with latent concept intervention, a problem typically addressed with purely associational deep learning techniques. This approach explicitly models and mitigates hidden confounding in the latent space, a challenge largely unaddressed in pre-2026 concept-based interpretability literature.

proposals.title: Causal-Controlled Counterfactual Explanations for High-Stakes Decisions: A Validation Study in Medical Imaging and Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: High-stakes domains like healthcare and autonomous systems require models that are not only accurate but interpretable and robust to spurious correlations. Pre-2026, methods for generating counterfactual explanations often lack a causal grounding, potentially leading to misleading or non-actionable explanations based on image artifacts rather than true causal features. Validating a causally-aware explanation method in critical, real-world settings is essential for building trustworthy AI.
proposals.hypothesis: The VAE-CaCE (Causal-Controlled Counterfactual Explanations) framework, when applied to complex, high-dimensional data from medical imaging and autonomous driving, will generate counterfactual explanations that are more causally faithful, actionable, and robust to dataset biases compared to standard non-causal counterfactual generation methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the VAE-CaCE architecture, which uses a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) structured with a causal graph to separate latent variables into causal parents (e.g., disease pathology) and non-causal noise. For validation, we will use two benchmarks: 1) a medical imaging dataset (e.g., chest X-rays with diagnostic labels and known confounding variables like hospital ID), and 2) an autonomous driving scene understanding dataset (e.g., with object labels and weather conditions). We will quantitatively evaluate the generated counterfactuals using established metrics (e.g., proximity, validity) and introduce new domain-specific metrics for causal faithfulness, such as the alignment of changed features with known causal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first rigorous empirical validations of a causal explanation method in complex, high-dimensional domains. It will produce a benchmark for causally-faithful explanations in medical imaging and autonomous systems and offer practical insights into the challenges of deploying such methods with real-world, biased data.
proposals.novelty: While CaCE was proposed in simpler settings, its application and rigorous validation in high-dimensional, high-stakes domains with inherent confounding and complex latent structures is novel. This bridges a gap between causal interpretability theory and the practical demands of critical AI applications.

proposals.title: Automated Discovery of Interpretable Concepts for Causal Mediation Analysis in High-Dimensional Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Causal concept-based explanations (CaCE) provide a powerful framework for explaining heterogeneous treatment effects by grounding them in human-interpretable concepts. However, current methods require these concepts to be pre-specified by domain experts, which is a major bottleneck, especially in high-dimensional settings like genomics or medical imaging where relevant mediating concepts are unknown. Automating this discovery process is crucial for scaling causal explanation to complex, modern datasets and uncovering novel, actionable mediators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining representation learning for concept discovery with causal mediation analysis can automatically identify a sparse set of human-interpretable, data-driven concepts that significantly mediate the treatment-outcome relationship, outperforming methods reliant on pre-specified concepts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural architecture. First, a concept auto-encoder, regularized for sparsity and disentanglement, will learn a dictionary of candidate concept representations from high-dimensional covariates (e.g., clinical time-series or images). Second, we will integrate these learned concepts into a structural causal model for mediation analysis, building on recent advances in deep survival and treatment effect estimation. The model will be trained with a combined objective that encourages the discovery of concepts which are both predictive of the outcome and causally influenced by the treatment, using targeted regularization to ensure identifiability under standard causal assumptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first end-to-end method for automated concept discovery within a causal explanation framework, directly applicable to clinical survival and treatment effect estimation problems. It will extend the CaCE paradigm to domains where expert knowledge is incomplete, enabling data-driven hypothesis generation for underlying biological or clinical mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While concept-based explanation and causal mediation are established, their integration with *unsupervised* concept discovery from raw data is novel. This moves beyond simply testing pre-defined concepts to actively learning the relevant conceptual mediators from the data itself, a significant departure from existing causal explanation literature.

proposals.title: Bayesian Bootstrapping and Conformal Inference for Uncertainty Quantification in Causal Concept Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Causal concept effect (CaCE) estimation, which quantifies the causal effect of intervening on high-level interpretable concepts within a machine learning model, is a promising tool for explainable AI in clinical decision support. However, existing methods (e.g., based on TCAV or concept bottleneck models) primarily provide point estimates without measures of statistical uncertainty. For reliable deployment in high-stakes domains like treatment effect heterogeneity analysis, clinicians need confidence intervals to assess the reliability of these explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating Bayesian nonparametric bootstrapping with conformal prediction techniques, we can produce valid, model-agnostic confidence intervals for CaCE estimates that account for both uncertainty in the concept representation and in the downstream causal estimation.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation to incorporate conceptual interventions. Uncertainty will be quantified via a two-stage procedure: 1) A Bayesian bootstrap over the training data to propagate uncertainty into the learned concept encoder (e.g., a concept bottleneck network). 2) A conformal prediction layer applied to the causal forest (or RIST) outputs, using the bootstrapped concept distributions to generate prediction intervals for individual CaCEs. We will benchmark against parametric bootstrap and MCMC-based alternatives on semi-synthetic datasets derived from clinical covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, practical framework for uncertainty-aware causal concept explanation. It will deliver a software package integrating with existing causal and survival analysis toolkits, enabling researchers to report CaCEs with confidence intervals, significantly enhancing the trustworthiness of explanatory models for clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty quantification exists for both causal forests and concept-based models separately, their integration for causal concepts is novel. The proposed combination of Bayesian bootstrapping for representation uncertainty with conformal inference for final estimate coverage is a non-obvious and robust solution tailored for the complex, censored data common in clinical studies.

proposals.title: Decomposing Causal Concept Effects: A Mediation Framework for Interpretable Treatment Effect Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Causal concept effect (CaCE) estimation provides interpretable insights into how high-level concepts mediate treatment effects, but existing methods conflate direct and indirect pathways. In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, understanding whether a treatment acts directly or through specific intermediate concepts (e.g., a biomarker or a learned latent factor) is crucial for developing targeted interventions and improving model trustworthiness. A formal causal mediation framework is needed to decompose these effects, leveraging pre-2026 advances in causal inference and representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating causal mediation analysis with the CaCE framework, we can reliably decompose the total treatment effect on a time-to-event or continuous outcome into direct effects and indirect effects mediated through interpretable concept representations, even in the presence of censored data and hidden confounding.
proposals.method: We will extend the structural causal model framework for mediation to settings with high-dimensional covariates and learned concepts. Building on Shalit's prior work on survival analysis with recursively imputed trees and hidden covariates, we will develop a two-stage estimator: first, we will learn stable concept representations using variational autoencoders or concept bottleneck models adjusted for confounding; second, we will estimate natural direct and indirect effects using targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE) or g-computation, adapting to right-censored outcomes via inverse probability of censoring weighting. Sensitivity analyses for unmeasured mediator-outcome confounding will be incorporated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous method for quantifying pathway-specific effects in interpretable machine learning for healthcare, advancing beyond associative concept explanations. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating the mechanistic role of concepts in treatment effect heterogeneity, directly applicable to clinical domains like IVF treatment optimization as studied in prior work. The resulting software will integrate with existing causal survival and offline policy learning toolkits.
proposals.novelty: While causal mediation is well-studied, its integration with the CaCE framework to decompose effects through learned, high-dimensional concepts in survival and offline learning settings is novel. This moves concept-based explanations from associative attribution to a formal causal pathway analysis, addressing mediation in the presence of hidden confounders and censoring.

proposals.title: Estimating Causal Concept Effects with Unobserved Confounding via Instrumental Variable Regression
proposals.motivation: Estimating the causal effect of a learned concept (CaCE) on an outcome is crucial for interpretable and trustworthy machine learning in clinical settings, such as assessing how a latent disease progression concept influences survival. However, standard methods require strong ignorability, which is violated by unobserved confounding between the concept and the outcome, leading to biased estimates. Pre-2026, there is a gap in robust methods for CaCE estimation under such realistic, confounding-rich scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging a valid instrumental variable (IV) that influences the concept but is independent of the outcome given the concept and observed covariates, we can consistently estimate the CaCE even in the presence of unobserved confounders.
proposals.method: We will extend the recent concept bottleneck model (CBM) framework by integrating it with established IV methods for causal inference. Specifically, we will develop a two-stage deep IV estimator: first, a concept predictor network will be trained to model the relationship between the IV, observed covariates, and the latent concept representation. Second, an outcome model will regress the survival or clinical outcome on the predicted concept, using the IV's exogenous variation to isolate the causal effect. We will adapt methods like Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) and DeepIV for this structured setting, with evaluation on semi-synthetic medical datasets and the benchmark from Shalit's prior work on heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and practical algorithm for CaCE estimation under unobserved confounding, expanding the applicability of interpretable causal machine learning. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating concept-based causal claims in offline clinical data, directly advancing the themes of causal inference and prediction in Shalit's research.
proposals.novelty: While IV methods are standard in econometrics and some ML applications, their integration into the concept-based explanation paradigm to address unobserved confounding is novel. This bridges the gap between high-dimensional concept learning and rigorous causal identification strategies from the IV literature.

proposals.title: Structural Counterfactual Models for Robust Conditional Average Causal Effect Estimation in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects, especially for time-to-event outcomes, is crucial for personalized medicine but remains challenging due to censoring and unobserved confounding. Pre-2026 methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) advance CATE estimation under censoring but often rely on strong, non-parametric assumptions about the data-generating process, limiting their robustness to model misspecification and their interpretability for clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating explicit structural causal models (SCMs) and economic-style counterfactual reasoning into the estimation framework will yield more robust, interpretable, and theoretically grounded estimates of Conditional Average Causal Effects (CaCE) in survival settings, particularly when facing partially observed confounding.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel estimator that combines the strengths of RIST for handling censored outcomes with the formal framework of SCMs. The approach will specify a structural model for the treatment assignment and time-to-event process, potentially incorporating latent variables. Estimation will proceed via a multi-stage algorithm: first, using variational methods or EM to infer hidden covariates from proxies; second, employing the identified structural model to simulate counterfactual survival distributions under different treatments; and third, refining the CaCE estimates using a targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE) step to ensure robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled methodology for causal survival analysis that explicitly disentangles the data-generating mechanism, improving robustness to model misspecification. It will deliver not only point estimates of heterogeneous effects but also a full counterfactual distribution, enhancing clinical interpretability and enabling more reliable policy decisions from observational data.
proposals.novelty: While SCMs are established in economics and some machine learning domains, their integration with state-of-the-art, non-parametric survival forests for CaCE estimation is novel. This fusion creates a hybrid model that leverages both the flexibility of machine learning and the identifiability guarantees of economic counterfactual models.

proposals.title: Sensitivity Analysis for Conditional Average Causal Effects in Survival Outcomes: A Framework for Unobserved Confounding
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects, like Conditional Average Causal Effects (CaCE), from observational clinical data is crucial for personalized medicine but is vulnerable to unobserved confounding. Pre-2026, methods for sensitivity analysis in causal inference, such as Rosenbaum bounds for average effects, are not well-integrated with modern machine learning estimators for high-dimensional, time-to-event outcomes with censoring. This gap limits the reliability of CaCE estimates in clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally adapting and extending epidemiological sensitivity analysis frameworks to modern CaCE estimators for survival data, we can quantify the robustness of individualized treatment effect predictions to plausible degrees of unmeasured confounding.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Rosenbaum-type' sensitivity model, which parameterizes confounding strength via a Gamma parameter, to the CaCE setting. This framework will be integrated with Shalit's previously developed 'Recursively Imputed Trees' for survival outcomes and other benchmarked methods (e.g., causal forests). The core technical task is to derive bounds on the CaCE—the difference in potential survival probabilities—as a function of Gamma, developing efficient algorithms to compute and visualize these sensitivity contours for individual patients or subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general-purpose sensitivity analysis toolkit for CaCE in survival analysis, enabling practitioners to assess when heterogeneous effect estimates are robust. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating causal inference methods under realistic, non-ignorable missing data mechanisms in offline clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis for average effects exists, its application to complex, machine learning-based estimators of heterogeneous effects in censored time-to-event data is novel. The integration specifically targets the estimation objects (CaCE) and data structures (high-dimensional, censored covariates) central to Shalit's recent methodological research.

proposals.title: Active Concept Annotation for Causal Survival Analysis: An Active Learning Framework for Efficient VAE Latent Space Labeling
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation for time-to-event outcomes, learning interpretable latent representations from high-dimensional covariates (e.g., EHR data) is crucial. However, validating and interpreting these latent dimensions as clinically meaningful concepts requires expert annotation, which is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming at scale. This bottleneck limits the deployment of interpretable models like VAEs in real-world clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning strategy, which strategically queries expert labels for the most informative or uncertain latent concepts in a VAE's representation, can drastically reduce the annotation cost required to achieve a clinically validated and interpretable model for downstream causal survival tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a VAE is first pre-trained on high-dimensional clinical covariates. We then treat its latent dimensions as candidate concepts and employ a pool-based active learning loop. Using pre-2026 query strategies like uncertainty sampling (e.g., based on entropy of a concept classifier) or expected model change, we will iteratively select the most informative unlabeled latent examples for an expert to annotate. The annotated concepts will be used to fine-tune a concept classifier and to regularize the VAE's latent space, improving its alignment with clinical semantics. The final validated latent representation will be evaluated on downstream tasks from Shalit's prior work, such as estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in survival settings using the recursively imputed trees method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical methodology for building interpretable, concept-based models in clinical machine learning with minimal expert oversight. It will yield a benchmark for active concept annotation in medical AI and demonstrate how such efficient labeling improves the performance and trustworthiness of causal inference models on censored time-to-event data.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established for standard classification, its application to the novel problem of annotating *latent concepts* from a generative model's representation for causal survival analysis is unexplored. This bridges Shalit's expertise in causal survival methods with efficient label acquisition, moving beyond passive data collection.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Embedding for Sequential and Volumetric Data: Extending VAE-CaCE to Video and 3D Medical Image Classifiers
proposals.motivation: Interpretability of deep learning models for video (e.g., surgical videos) and 3D volumetric data (e.g., CT/MRI scans) is critical for clinical trust and actionable insights. Pre-2026, methods like VAE-CaCE provided causal concept-based explanations for 2D images, but cannot handle the temporal dependencies of video or the complex spatial structures of 3D data. Extending causal concept discovery to these domains is essential for understanding what spatiotemporal features drive clinical predictions, such as disease progression or treatment outcome forecasts.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating temporal and 3D-contextual awareness into the VAE-CaCE framework, we can identify stable, causally relevant spatiotemporal concepts (e.g., anatomical motion patterns or lesion growth) that explain the predictions of classifiers on sequential and volumetric data.
proposals.method: We will extend the VAE-CaCE architecture by replacing its core with a sequential VAE (using ConvLSTMs or 3D CNNs) to encode video/volumetric inputs. The concept bottleneck will be enforced on learned latent representations that capture temporal or 3D structural summaries. We will adapt the causal effect estimation stage, using targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust estimators, to handle time-to-event or longitudinal outcomes common in clinical data, ensuring robustness to observed confounding in the sequential context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for causal concept-based explanation of classifiers on video and 3D medical data. It will yield a novel benchmark for evaluating interpretability methods in these domains and deliver a practical tool for clinicians to identify and validate spatiotemporal biomarkers from complex predictive models.
proposals.novelty: While concept-based explanations exist for images, adapting them to model the causal effect of *spatiotemporal* concepts on predictions in the presence of clinical confounders is novel. This bridges Shalit's expertise in causal inference for treatment effect estimation with interpretability for high-dimensional sequential/volumetric data.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Embedding for Interpretable Self-Supervised Representations in Clinical Time-to-Event Prediction
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown great promise for learning rich representations from high-dimensional, unlabeled clinical data, such as longitudinal EHRs or medical images. However, these representations are often black boxes, limiting clinical trust and utility for causal discovery. Existing interpretability methods, like saliency maps, fail to explain how high-level clinical concepts (e.g., disease progression) are encoded and causally linked to outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Causal Concept Embedding (CaCE) framework can be adapted to decompose SSL representations into interpretable, causally relevant clinical concepts, and that these concept-based explanations will reveal heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event outcomes more reliably than standard feature attributions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CaCE framework to operate on the latent space of SSL models (e.g., a BERT-style encoder for sequences or a SimCLR-style encoder for images). First, we will train a concept predictor network to map SSL embeddings to a set of pre-specified, clinically meaningful concepts (e.g., 'renal function decline'). We will then fit a structural causal model to estimate the causal effect of these learned concepts on a survival outcome, using a targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) approach to handle censoring. The method will be validated on benchmark datasets with known ground-truth concepts and applied to a real-world dataset of ICU patient sequences to explain predictions of sepsis onset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for causal concept-based explanation of self-supervised clinical models, bridging SSL and interpretable causal inference. It will yield a novel tool for auditing SSL representations, identifying clinically actionable subgroups via heterogeneous concept effects, and potentially improving downstream survival prediction models through concept-based regularization.
proposals.novelty: While CaCE has been applied to supervised models, its application to unsupervised or self-supervised representations for causal time-to-event analysis is novel. This combines Shalit's expertise in causal survival analysis with the challenge of explaining representations learned without explicit task supervision.

proposals.title: Inverse Concept Discovery: A Causal Framework for Identifying Latent Drivers of a Target Treatment Effect
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, we often identify a heterogeneous effect but lack interpretable, actionable concepts explaining it. Existing concept-based models (e.g., Concept Bottleneck Networks) require pre-specified concepts, limiting discovery. An inverse method to find concepts that causally drive a pre-specified effect would bridge the gap between black-box effect estimation and clinically meaningful mechanistic understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing concept discovery as an inverse causal problem—where the target is a known heterogeneous treatment effect—we can identify a sparse set of latent concepts, represented as functions of observed covariates, that are both predictive of the effect and satisfy causal sufficiency conditions for being its drivers.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of hidden variable causal discovery and concept bottleneck models. Given an estimated Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) function (e.g., from a survival forest or meta-learner), we will learn a concept encoder network that maps observed covariates to latent concepts. The training objective will combine: 1) predictive loss of the CATE from the concepts, 2) a regularization enforcing the concepts to be functions of covariates via an auxiliary predictor, and 3) a causal constraint ensuring the concepts block backdoor paths between treatment and outcome, assessed via conditional independence tests on the learned representations. Optimization will use variational inference for latent concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological tool for post-hoc interpretation of heterogeneous treatment effects, moving from 'what is the effect?' to 'what underlying patient concepts cause this effect?'. It will yield a framework for generating clinically testable hypotheses from observational data, directly supporting precision medicine.
proposals.novelty: Unlike forward concept-based models, this inverts the problem to discover concepts for a given effect. It uniquely integrates causal constraints on latent variables with representation learning, going beyond purely predictive concept discovery to identify plausible causal mediators.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Embedding Estimation with Limited Supervision: A Semi-Synthetic Benchmark and Data-Efficient Adaptation Framework
proposals.motivation: Estimating Causal Concept Effect (CaCE) is crucial for interpretable causal analysis in high-dimensional settings like medical imaging, where concepts (e.g., tumor texture) mediate treatment effects. However, real-world clinical datasets often have very limited labeled concept data due to annotation cost, and limited image data for rare subgroups. Pre-2026 CaCE methods assume sufficient labeled concepts, failing in these low-data regimes prevalent in healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging semi-supervised learning and data augmentation within a causal estimation framework, we can develop a robust CaCE estimator that maintains low bias and variance even when labeled concept or image data is severely limited.
proposals.method: We will extend the concept bottleneck model framework for CaCE estimation. First, we will create a semi-synthetic benchmark using pre-2026 datasets (e.g., MIMIC-CXR) by simulating treatments, known concept mediators, and outcomes. For low labeled-concept data, we will employ a semi-supervised variational autoencoder to learn a rich concept representation from both labeled and unlabeled images. For low image data per subgroup, we will integrate causal data augmentation techniques, such as targeted generative oversampling based on propensity scores. Estimation will use a doubly-robust estimator (e.g., a modified R-learner) with the learned concept representation to handle confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a validated methodological framework for data-efficient CaCE estimation, 2) a publicly available semi-synthetic benchmark for evaluating causal concept learning under data scarcity, and 3) practical guidance for applying interpretable causal models in resource-constrained clinical studies where labeled data is sparse.
proposals.novelty: While semi-supervised learning and data augmentation are known, their integration into the specific causal identification task of CaCE estimation for handling dual scarcity (labels and images) is novel. This bridges the gap between causal inference methods and practical constraints in clinical machine learning.

proposals.title: Causal Concept Effect Debugging: A Framework for Identifying and Repairing Spurious Correlations in Clinical Prediction Models
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models for clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation often rely on spurious correlations present in observational data, such as hospital-specific protocols or socioeconomic proxies, which harm their robustness and generalizability. Pre-2026, methods for model debugging are largely correlational, lacking a causal framework to systematically identify and correct these problematic feature-concept relationships, especially in complex settings with time-to-event outcomes and hidden confounding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Causal Concept Effect (CaCE) framework can be operationalized to causally attribute a model's failure modes to specific spurious concepts, and that targeted model repair based on these attributions will improve out-of-distribution performance and causal validity more effectively than standard regularization.
proposals.method: We will extend the CaCE estimation framework to handle censored survival outcomes and hidden covariates, building on Shalit's prior work on recursively imputed trees and treatment effect heterogeneity. For a given trained model (e.g., a deep survival network), we will estimate CaCEs for human-interpretable concepts (e.g., 'hospital identity', 'test frequency') using targeted causal learning. Identified spurious concepts will guide a repair procedure: either through adversarial concept correction during training, or via a causal regularization term that penalizes the estimated CaCE. We will benchmark against standard robustness methods on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causally-grounded toolkit for model debugging in clinical machine learning, moving beyond performance metrics to diagnose *why* models fail. It will deliver a practical method to repair models by severing spurious causal pathways, leading to more reliable predictions and treatment effect estimates for real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While concept-based explanations exist, using CaCE for systematic model *repair* is novel. The integration of this approach with methods for survival analysis and hidden confounding directly addresses key challenges in clinical data, which have not been tackled by prior model debugging frameworks.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests with Latent Confounding: A Sensitivity Analysis Framework for Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is critical in clinical research, but standard methods rely on the strong ignorability assumption, which is often violated by unobserved confounders. This leads to biased estimates of treatment benefit or harm for different patient subgroups, potentially resulting in flawed clinical decisions. Pre-2026 sensitivity analysis methods for HTEs, especially in survival contexts with censoring, are underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by embedding a flexible sensitivity model within a recursive partitioning framework for survival outcomes, we can produce robust bounds on subgroup-specific treatment effects and identify populations where causal conclusions are most fragile to unobserved confounding.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Tree (RIST) framework to incorporate a sensitivity parameter, γ, quantifying the degree of residual confounding after adjusting for observed covariates. For each candidate split during tree construction, we will compute not a point estimate but bounds on the treatment effect (e.g., difference in restricted mean survival times) across a plausible range of γ. Splitting will prioritize maximizing heterogeneity in these bounded effects. The final model will output a 'causal survival forest' where each leaf provides an interval of possible HTEs, enabling a sensitivity analysis at the subgroup level.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated method for conducting sensitivity analysis of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes, directly addressing a major limitation in applying causal inference to clinical data. It will equip researchers with a practical tool to assess the robustness of personalized treatment recommendations derived from observational studies, moving beyond point estimates to conclusions that account for unmeasured confounding.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis for average treatment effects exists, integrating it into a non-parametric, machine-learning-based HTE estimation for censored outcomes is novel. The approach uniquely combines Shalit's prior work on recursive imputation for survival with a principled sensitivity framework, moving from a single 'best' tree to a forest characterizing uncertainty from both data and possible confounding.

proposals.title: Robust Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Under Practical Positivity Violations: A Targeted Learning and Survival Analysis Framework
proposals.motivation: The positivity assumption, requiring a non-zero probability of treatment for all covariate strata, is fundamental for causal inference but is often violated in observational clinical data. In such scenarios, standard estimators for heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) become unstable and biased, particularly for subpopulations that rarely receive a specific treatment (e.g., a high-risk patient group rarely given a novel therapy). Pre-2026 methods for handling positivity violations, like trimming or weight stabilization, often discard data or lack robustness for survival outcomes, limiting their utility for personalized treatment recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a doubly robust, targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE) framework, explicitly designed to manage practical positivity violations through flexible ensemble learning and data-adaptive trimming, will yield more stable and less biased estimates of conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) in time-to-event and other clinical outcomes compared to existing methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel estimator building on the TMLE framework. First, we will use an ensemble (Super Learner) to flexibly estimate the propensity score and outcome models, including survival-specific learners. Second, we will implement a data-adaptive covariate shift detection to identify regions of the covariate space with severe positivity violations. Third, we will integrate a targeted trimming or smoothing procedure within the TMLE updating step to control variance in these regions without introducing large bias. The approach will be evaluated using the semi-parametric theory of TMLE to derive influence curves and asymptotic properties under violations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, open-source software implementation for robust HTE estimation in the presence of practical positivity violations, directly applicable to clinical survival data. It will extend the causal inference toolkit for real-world evidence studies where treatment assignment is highly imbalanced, enabling more reliable subgroup analysis for personalized medicine.
proposals.novelty: While TMLE is known for double robustness, its explicit adaptation to handle severe practical positivity violations in HTE estimation for survival outcomes—by integrating data-adaptive diagnostics directly into the estimation process—is a novel contribution beyond standard weight clipping or outcome model extrapolation.

proposals.title: Learning Latent Treatment Policies from Observational Time-to-Event Data for Unbiased Causal Survival Estimation
proposals.motivation: In observational clinical studies, the treatment assignment mechanism is often unknown and confounded by hidden patient factors, leading to biased estimates of treatment effects on survival outcomes. Pre-2026 methods for causal survival analysis, like those in Shalit's work, typically assume the propensity score model is known or correctly specified, which is rarely true in practice. Inferring the treatment policy is crucial for valid off-policy evaluation and for understanding real-world clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly modeling the latent treatment assignment policy and the time-to-event outcome using a variational framework, we can recover a more accurate representation of the confounding structure, leading to less biased estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in the presence of censoring.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep latent variable model that treats the unknown treatment policy as a stochastic function of observed and latent covariates. The method builds on the CEVAE architecture but is adapted for survival outcomes with censoring, integrating a treatment assignment network, a survival network (using a Cox or AFT model), and a variational inference network for the latent confounders. We will use a tailored objective function that combines evidence lower bound (ELBO) terms for the treatment and outcome models with a survival-specific loss (e.g., negative log partial likelihood) to handle right-censored data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust framework for causal survival analysis when the treatment policy is unknown, extending the toolkit for observational clinical research. It will deliver a new benchmark method for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from biased offline data without relying on a known propensity score model, directly applicable to domains like IVF treatment evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used latent variable models for causal inference or focused on survival analysis separately, the novel integration to jointly infer the treatment policy and survival outcomes under censoring is unexplored. This moves beyond standard propensity score adjustment by learning the assignment mechanism directly from the data.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests for Clustered and Network-Dependent Observational Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine, but standard methods assume i.i.d. samples. Real-world clinical data often contain dependencies, such as patients clustered within hospitals or sharing physicians, which can bias estimates and invalidate confidence intervals if ignored. Extending HTE estimation to this non-i.i.d. setting is essential for reliable inference in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting recursive partitioning and survival-based causal forests to explicitly model data dependencies—through random effects, network-regularized losses, or within-cluster resampling—will yield unbiased estimates of conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) with valid uncertainty quantification in clustered and network data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Generalized Random Forest (GRF) framework and the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) method. For clustered data, we will integrate mixed-effects models into the splitting criterion and node estimation. For network data, we will employ graph-based regularization on the loss function to penalize differences in treatment effect estimates between connected units. Estimation will leverage targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust scores, adapted for the dependent data setting, to ensure robustness to model misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for estimating survival-based HTEs from non-i.i.d. observational data, accompanied by open-source software. It will deliver valid statistical inference (confidence intervals) for CATEs in settings with clustered or network dependencies, directly addressing a key limitation in applying causal machine learning to real-world clinical registries and electronic health records.
proposals.novelty: While mixed models exist for marginal effects and some network-adjusted regressions, their integration into flexible, non-parametric causal forest algorithms for time-to-event outcomes is novel. This moves beyond simple covariate adjustment for clustering to a full causal estimation and inference procedure tailored for complex dependencies.

proposals.title: Learning Disentangled Representations for Heterogeneous Effects of Multiple Treatments on Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: In clinical practice, patients often face multiple treatment options, not just a binary choice. Estimating individualized treatment effects (ITEs) for multiple treatments from observational time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but methodologically challenging. Pre-2026 work, including Shalit's on recursively imputed trees for survival outcomes, primarily focuses on binary treatments, leaving a significant gap for multi-arm settings where confounding and censoring interact with complex treatment assignments.
proposals.hypothesis: A representation learning framework that enforces balancing of pre-treatment covariates across multiple treatment arms while disentangling treatment-specific prognostic effects can yield consistent estimates of conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) for survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend the binary-treatment balancing representation framework (e.g., based on Wasserstein distance or moment matching) to the multi-treatment case. The model will learn a shared representation Phi(X) and treatment-specific heads for each of K>2 treatments. We will integrate this with a survival loss (e.g., Cox partial likelihood or a discrete-time model) and a novel multi-treatment balancing penalty, adapting recursively imputed trees or deep neural networks to handle censoring. Estimation will use a counterfactual regression architecture regularized to ensure the representation's distribution is similar across all treatment groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable method for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects across multiple interventions from observational survival data. It will extend the causal inference toolbox for clinical decision-making where several viable therapies exist, such as choosing between different drug regimens or surgical interventions. We will release a software package and benchmarks based on real-world clinical datasets and semi-synthetic data.
proposals.novelty: While representation learning for causal effect estimation is established for binary treatments, its extension to multiple treatments with time-to-event outcomes is non-trivial and requires new balancing constraints and architectural designs. This integrates Shalit's prior work on survival causal inference with the underdeveloped area of multi-treatment effect estimation.

proposals.title: Estimating Time-Varying Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Outcomes: A G-Computation Framework with Hidden State Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings like chronic disease management, treatments and patient states evolve over time, creating complex longitudinal confounding. Pre-2026 methods for heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation in survival analysis, including Shalit's own work, largely assume static treatments or baseline confounders, limiting their applicability to real-world dynamic decision-making. Extending these models to handle time-varying treatments and confounders is crucial for personalized sequential intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal estimation framework combining g-computation with models for hidden patient states can accurately estimate the causal effect of time-varying treatment strategies on time-to-event outcomes, even in the presence of unmeasured time-varying confounding.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to longitudinal data. The core method will integrate a structural nested mean model or g-computation formula to account for time-varying treatments. To handle potential hidden confounding, we will incorporate a recurrent neural network or a hidden Markov model to infer latent patient states from observed longitudinal covariates. Estimation will proceed via a multi-step algorithm: first, modeling the treatment and outcome processes conditional on observed and inferred hidden states; second, using g-computation to simulate potential outcomes under different treatment sequences; and third, applying RIST to estimate heterogeneous effects based on patient profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, robust method for estimating personalized dynamic treatment regimes from observational longitudinal survival data. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating time-varying HTE estimation and deliver an open-source software package, advancing the field of causal machine learning for clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of g-computation for time-varying treatments with a hidden state model to address unmeasured confounding within a tree-based heterogeneous effect estimation framework, a combination not explored in pre-2026 HTE literature.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Analysis with High-Dimensional Outcomes: A Deep Latent Variable Framework for Imaging and Text Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical research, key outcomes like disease progression or treatment response are increasingly captured in high-dimensional forms (e.g., longitudinal medical images, clinical notes, or genomic pathways). Pre-2026 methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival settings, such as Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees, primarily handle low-dimensional, scalar outcomes. This limits their applicability to modern, data-rich clinical studies where the outcome itself is complex and informative of patient heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating deep latent variable models for outcome representation with established causal survival frameworks, we can reliably estimate treatment effect heterogeneity from time-to-event data with complex, structured outcomes, leading to more granular and clinically actionable insights.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Recursively Imputed Survival Trees' framework to handle high-dimensional outcomes Y (e.g., image sequences, text reports). The core innovation is a two-stage model: (1) A variational autoencoder (VAE) or a contrastive learning model (e.g., SimCLR) will learn a low-dimensional, semantically meaningful latent representation Z of the complex outcome. (2) The latent representation Z, along with baseline covariates X, will be used within a causal survival forest that recursively imputes potential outcomes under treatment and control, adjusting for censoring. We will benchmark against methods that naively reduce outcomes to summary scalars.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first methodological bridge between deep representation learning for structured data and non-parametric causal survival analysis. It will yield a practical software tool for clinical researchers to discover HTEs from rich data sources. The benchmark results will establish best practices for a new class of problems in personalized medicine.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning for representation is established and causal survival methods exist, their integration for HTE estimation is novel. The key non-obvious challenge is ensuring the latent representation is causally valid for counterfactual prediction and robust to censoring, moving beyond mere predictive performance.

proposals.title: Provable Representation Balancing for Deep Survival and Treatment Effect Networks
proposals.motivation: In causal inference for time-to-event outcomes, balancing representations of confounders is critical for unbiased treatment effect estimation. While recent work (e.g., Shalit et al.'s BART extensions) addresses this for tree-based models, deep neural networks offer superior capacity for complex, high-dimensional data but lack formal guarantees on the balance properties of their learned representations. This gap limits their reliable application in high-stakes clinical prediction tasks where robustness is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating specific architectural constraints and regularization objectives inspired by causal optimal transport into deep survival networks, we can derive formal, non-asymptotic bounds on the imbalance of the learned latent representations across treatment groups.
proposals.method: We will extend the Deep Survival Machines and Deep Cox Proportional Hazards architectures by incorporating a representation layer regularized via Integral Probability Metric (IPM) minimization, specifically using Wasserstein or Maximum Mean Discrepancy distances. Theoretical analysis will employ tools from Rademacher complexity and stability of learning algorithms to bound the expected imbalance. We will validate the bounds empirically on semi-synthetic benchmarks derived from real-world clinical datasets (e.g., SUPPORT, SEER) and compare against existing methods like Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) for survival and treatment effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework for certifying balance in deep non-linear models for survival analysis, bridging causal inference and deep learning theory. It will yield a new class of 'certifiably balanced' deep survival models with provable robustness properties, directly enhancing the reliability of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation from observational clinical data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deriving explicit imbalance bounds for deep, non-linear architectures in the survival context, moving beyond empirical regularization and heuristic balancing methods common in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely combines causal representation learning, algorithmic stability theory, and survival analysis.

proposals.title: Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Balanced Representation Learning in Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation from Observational Survival Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but is confounded by selection bias, where treatment groups differ systematically in their baseline covariates. Pre-2026 methods for handling this, such as propensity score weighting, can be unstable under model misspecification and lead to high-variance estimates, particularly in high-dimensional settings common in modern clinical datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using adversarial domain adaptation to learn patient representations that are invariant to treatment assignment will reduce selection bias more effectively than standard covariate balancing methods, leading to more accurate and robust estimates of individualized treatment effects on survival outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Shalit et al.'s 'Balanced Representations for Causal Inference' to censored survival outcomes.
