The core architecture will be a neural network with a shared representation layer, whose output is fed to three adversarial heads: (1) a treatment predictor (adversarially trained to be unable to predict treatment from the representation), (2) a survival outcome predictor (e.g., a Cox proportional hazards network), and (3) a treatment effect predictor for the log hazard ratio. We will adapt adversarial training techniques from domain adaptation (e.g., gradient reversal layers) to enforce the treatment-invariant representation, while jointly optimizing for accurate survival prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, deep learning-based method for estimating HTEs on survival outcomes that directly targets the covariate shift problem via representation learning. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the robustness of causal survival estimators under strong selection bias. The resulting software will be a valuable tool for clinical researchers analyzing electronic health records or registry data.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial balancing has been explored for continuous/binary outcomes, its application to censored survival data for HTE estimation is novel. The integration of adversarial domain adaptation with a survival-specific architecture directly addresses a key limitation in pre-2026 causal survival analysis methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Deep Learning for Uncertainty-Aware Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Time-to-Event Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data is critical for personalized medicine but is challenged by right-censoring, hidden confounding, and model misspecification. Existing methods, including Shalit's recursively imputed trees, often provide point estimates without reliable measures of epistemic uncertainty, which is crucial for clinical decision-making where risk stratification depends on confidence intervals. Bayesian methods naturally quantify such uncertainty but have not been fully integrated with deep learning for counterfactual survival outcomes in high-dimensional settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian deep learning framework, which models epistemic uncertainty via approximate posterior distributions over neural network parameters, will produce more reliable and clinically actionable estimates of individualized treatment effects in survival analysis, outperforming deterministic models in calibration and uncertainty quantification, especially under covariate shift.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture combining Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) with a treatment-specific representation learning framework for survival outcomes. Specifically, we will extend the TARNet and Dragonnet architectures by placing variational distributions over their weights, using Monte Carlo Dropout or deep ensembles as scalable approximations for Bayesian inference. The model will be trained to minimize a survival-specific loss (e.g., negative log-likelihood of a Cox partial likelihood or an accelerated failure time model) while incorporating a counterfactual balancing regularizer. Uncertainty in the conditional average treatment effect (CATE) for survival will be derived from the posterior predictive distribution of potential outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for uncertainty quantification in causal survival analysis, enabling clinicians to assess the confidence in personalized treatment effect estimates. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating the calibration of uncertainty estimates in HTE discovery from censored data, advancing the field towards more reliable and trustworthy machine learning for clinical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian neural networks and causal survival analysis exist separately, their integration for uncertainty-aware counterfactual prediction in time-to-event outcomes is novel. This approach moves beyond point estimates to full posterior distributions for HTEs, addressing a key gap in pre-2026 clinical prediction methodology.

proposals.title: Causal Representation Learning for Survival Analysis: Integrating Structure Learning to Identify Latent Confounders
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but is confounded by unobserved variables. Pre-2026 methods for causal survival analysis, like Shalit's recursively imputed trees, rely on pre-specified or observed covariates, which can lead to bias if key confounders are latent or the covariate set is high-dimensional and unstructured.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating causal discovery with representation learning can identify a latent, low-dimensional representation of relevant confounders from high-dimensional observational data, leading to less biased estimates of survival-time treatment effects compared to methods using the raw observed feature set.
proposals.method: We will develop a model combining a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) for representation learning with a structure learning module (e.g., NOTEARS or a differentiable DAG learner) applied to the latent space. The loss function will jointly optimize reconstruction fidelity, the DAG penalty on the latent variables, and the accuracy of a downstream treatment-specific survival model (e.g., a Deep Cox Proportional Hazards network). This forces the latent representation to adhere to plausible causal structures among confounders, treatment, and outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for robust causal inference in survival settings with complex, high-dimensional data. It will extend the toolkit for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation by automating the identification of relevant confounding structure, directly addressing a key limitation in pre-2026 clinical prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While representation learning for causal inference and causal discovery for feature selection exist separately pre-2026, their integration specifically for censored survival outcomes is novel. This moves beyond using representations for mere predictive performance to explicitly learn a causally-structured latent space for confounding adjustment.

proposals.title: Online Causal Representation Learning for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Streaming Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Modern clinical registries and electronic health records generate massive, continuously streaming datasets. Existing methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs), including Shalit's work on survival outcomes with censored data, are predominantly batch learners. They cannot efficiently update their representations with new evidence, leading to outdated models and missed opportunities for real-time clinical decision support as practice evolves.
proposals.hypothesis: An online learning framework that incrementally updates patient representations and HTE models from streaming, potentially censored, data will yield more accurate and timely estimates of treatment effect heterogeneity compared to static batch methods, especially in non-stationary clinical environments.
proposals.method: We will develop an online variant of the TARNet/CFRNet architecture, where the representation network is updated via stochastic gradient descent on mini-batches of streaming data. To handle time-to-event outcomes, we will integrate a continuously updated Breslow estimator for the baseline hazard within an online Cox partial likelihood framework. We will employ a reservoir sampling or replay buffer strategy to mitigate catastrophic forgetting of earlier data patterns, and use adaptive propensity score re-estimation to track evolving treatment policies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for online causal estimation of HTEs from streaming survival data, enabling real-time model updates. It will extend the theoretical understanding of non-stationarity in causal representation learning and deliver a practical software tool for dynamic clinical risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While online learning exists for prediction, its principled integration with causal HTE estimation for censored, streaming outcomes is novel. The key innovation is maintaining unbiased, incremental updates of the representation space critical for causal identification while managing distribution shift.

proposals.title: Inferring Treatment Assignment from Observational Data via Balanced Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: In observational studies, understanding the factors that lead to a specific treatment assignment is crucial for assessing confounding and the validity of causal effect estimates. Pre-2026, methods like propensity score modeling are used to adjust for confounding, but they often assume the model is correctly specified. A model's ability to accurately infer the treatment from patient data serves as a direct diagnostic for residual confounding and hidden biases in the treatment assignment mechanism, which is a core challenge in causal inference from clinical data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a representation learning framework, explicitly optimized to create a balanced feature space that removes treatment-specific information, will, when inverted, reveal the most predictive yet previously obscured covariates for treatment assignment, providing a more sensitive diagnostic for confounding than standard propensity score models.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural network architecture with a shared encoder and two adversarial heads. The primary head will be trained to predict the treatment assignment from the encoded representation. Simultaneously, an adversarial discriminator head will be trained to predict the original, potentially confounding, covariates from the same representation. The encoder will be trained to maximize treatment prediction accuracy while minimizing the discriminator's ability to reconstruct the covariates, creating a 'balanced' representation. We will specifically adapt the TARNet and CFRNet architectures, integrating this adversarial loss, and evaluate on benchmark datasets (e.g., IHDP, ACIC) and real clinical data from electronic health records.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for causal inference studies, quantifying the degree to which treatment assignment can be predicted after attempting to balance confounders. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the quality of learned representations in causal tasks and offer insights into hidden biases in clinical decision-making processes that standard propensity models might miss.
proposals.novelty: While representation learning for treatment effect estimation is established, using the *inverse* problem—predicting treatment from a deliberately balanced representation—as a diagnostic for confounding is novel. This approach directly links the performance of a predictive model to the quality of causal adjustment, moving beyond standard model checking for propensity scores.

proposals.title: Learning Sparse, Interpretable Representations for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, complex machine learning models for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data often act as 'black boxes,' limiting clinical trust and actionable insight. While methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) can handle censored data, they lack inherent interpretability. There is a critical need to bridge the gap between the predictive power of modern causal survival methods and the simplicity required for clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a sparsity-inducing, scale-down framework into established causal survival learners, we can learn simple, interpretable patient representations—such as concise rule sets or linear combinations of key covariates—that accurately capture HTEs without significant loss in predictive performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the RIST framework by incorporating a regularization path for representation complexity. The method will jointly optimize for accurate treatment effect estimation (using a causal loss like R-learner adapted for censored data) and for representation sparsity (via L1/L0 penalties or a post-hoc distillation step). We will develop an algorithm that iteratively prunes a deep survival forest or neural network to produce a final model defined by a small set of clinical subgroups or a sparse linear projection of covariates, validated on semi-synthetic and real clinical datasets (e.g., from IVF studies).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method to 'scale down' complex causal survival models into interpretable formats, directly addressing the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability in HTE estimation. It will deliver both a new algorithmic tool and benchmark results demonstrating that simple, causally-valid representations can be extracted from biased, censored observational data.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the causal survival estimation expertise from Shalit's prior work (RIST) with an explicit focus on learning *simple* representations for HTEs, moving beyond black-box prediction or standard model-agnostic explanation tools. It targets the structure of the learned representation itself for causal insight, not just post-hoc explanation of a complex model.

proposals.title: Non-Parametric State Transitions for Causal Survival Estimation with Hidden Confounders
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, estimating heterogeneous treatment effects on time-to-event outcomes with unobserved confounding is critical. Existing methods, like those using hidden Markov models or structural state-space models, often rely on restrictive linear Gaussian assumptions for state transitions, which fail to capture complex, non-linear disease or patient progression dynamics. This limitation biases effect estimates and reduces the utility of models for personalized treatment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the linear Gaussian state transition model with a flexible, non-parametric function approximator will significantly improve the accuracy of counterfactual survival predictions and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in the presence of hidden time-varying confounders.
proposals.method: We will extend a causal state-space framework (e.g., building on the 'Recursively Imputed Trees' concept) by modeling the latent state transition using a deep neural network, such as a recurrent neural network (RNN) or a transformer encoder. The network will be trained via variational inference, integrating techniques from recent advances in deep latent variable models (pre-2026) to maintain identifiability. Estimation will target survival curves under different treatment regimes, leveraging pre-2026 doubly robust or targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE) techniques for final causal effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more flexible and accurate framework for estimating treatment effects from longitudinal observational data with censoring, directly applicable to clinical problems like implantation outcome prediction. It will contribute a new benchmark method for offline reinforcement learning and causal inference where system dynamics are complex and non-linear.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning has been applied to survival analysis, its integration into identifiable causal models for time-to-event outcomes with hidden confounders, specifically replacing the core linear transition assumption, remains underexplored. This bridges Shalit's work on causal survival analysis with modern non-parametric sequence modeling.

proposals.title: Structured Variational Inference for Time-to-Event Outcomes: Capturing Temporal Dependencies in Latent Treatment Effect Models
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data with censoring is crucial for personalized medicine. Standard variational inference methods for survival analysis often use factorized approximate posteriors, which may fail to capture complex temporal dependencies between latent states, such as evolving patient physiology and treatment response. This structural misspecification can bias effect estimates and reduce predictive accuracy, limiting clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a more structured variational posterior, explicitly modeling temporal dependencies across latent states, will yield more accurate and less biased estimates of time-varying heterogeneous treatment effects compared to factorized approximations.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Trees framework by developing a structured variational autoencoder (SVAE) for survival data. The model will employ a recurrent neural network (RNN) or a temporal transformer within the inference network to parameterize a non-factorized posterior over latent confounders and effect modifiers. This will be integrated with a causal survival likelihood, using targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or doubly robust loss for final effect estimation, ensuring robustness to model misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel inference method for time-to-event causal estimation that better handles complex temporal dependencies. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating structured vs. factorized posteriors in survival contexts and deliver a practical tool for more reliable clinical decision support from longitudinal observational data.
proposals.novelty: While SVAEs exist, their application to causal survival estimation with censored data is novel. The integration of a temporally structured posterior within a proven, interpretable tree-based effect estimation framework (RIT) represents a significant methodological advance over existing factorized approaches.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects with Non-Additive Action Interactions via Latent State Causal Survival Models
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, treatments often interact with a patient's latent health state in complex, non-additive ways, where the effect of an action depends non-linearly on the underlying disease progression. Standard causal effect estimators, which assume additive separability of treatment and covariates, fail to capture these dynamics, leading to biased estimates of individualized treatment rules, especially in time-to-event settings with censored data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling non-additive interactions between actions and a latent representation of patient state within a causal survival framework will yield more accurate and interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to incorporate a structured latent variable model. The latent state will be inferred via a variational autoencoder (VAE) from high-dimensional, time-varying covariates. Treatment effects will be modeled by a neural network that takes the latent state and treatment as input, using a non-additive architecture (e.g., a bilinear layer or a transformer-based cross-attention module) to capture their interaction. The model will be trained with a counterfactual survival loss, leveraging pre-2026 advances in causal regularization and off-policy evaluation for time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for personalized treatment effect estimation that captures complex action-state interactions, directly applicable to clinical survival analysis. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating non-additive effect models in offline reinforcement learning and causal inference settings with biased data.
proposals.novelty: While latent variable models exist for survival analysis, their integration with *non-additive* causal effect estimation for actions is novel. This moves beyond standard additive structural models and hidden confounder adjustments, explicitly parameterizing the interaction mechanism within a causal framework.

proposals.title: Discrete Latent State Deep Kalman Filters for Heterogeneous Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and causal inference for time-to-event outcomes, patient trajectories are often governed by discrete, unobserved clinical states (e.g., disease subtypes, treatment adherence regimes). Pre-2026 deep state-space models like the Deep Kalman Filter (DKF) excel with continuous latent states but lack principled frameworks for discrete or mixed discrete-continuous states, limiting their ability to model these heterogeneous, regime-switching processes common in electronic health records.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the DKF to incorporate discrete latent variables via a structured variational approximation will yield more interpretable and accurate models for survival analysis and treatment effect estimation, by explicitly capturing distinct patient subpopulations with different risk profiles.
proposals.method: We will develop a Switching Deep Kalman Filter architecture, where the discrete latent state evolves as a Markov chain and governs the parameters of a continuous DKF. Inference will be performed using a structured variational posterior that factorizes over the discrete and continuous states, building on the VRNN and SRNN frameworks. We will integrate this model with established survival likelihoods and treatment modeling from Shalit's prior work (e.g., recursively imputed trees) to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects in the presence of censoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, deep learning-based tool for modeling time-series with regime changes, directly applicable to clinical survival data. It will advance causal inference for longitudinal treatments by enabling the discovery of latent patient subtypes that respond differently to interventions, moving beyond continuous latent representations.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid state-space models exist in classical time-series, a deep learning integration for discrete-continuous states tailored to censored survival and treatment effect estimation is novel. It uniquely combines Shalit's expertise in causal survival analysis with the expressive power of deep sequential latent variable models.

proposals.title: Estimating Long-Term Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Sequential Clinical Regimes: A Causal Survival Framework for Complex EHR Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHR) capture rich, longitudinal patient trajectories involving sequential treatment decisions and time-to-event outcomes. Pre-2026 methods for counterfactual inference often focus on single, static treatments or short-term outcomes, failing to address the complexity of chronic disease management where treatment effects are dynamic and depend on evolving patient state. This gap limits the ability to personalize long-term treatment strategies from observational data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, structured causal model combining deep representation learning for time-varying confounding with a gated recurrent survival architecture can provide valid and clinically interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects for multi-step treatment regimes over extended horizons.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to sequential settings. The approach will involve: 1) Learning a latent patient state representation from high-dimensional, irregularly sampled EHR covariates using a transformer or GRU-based encoder, accounting for informative censoring. 2) Modeling the treatment assignment mechanism and potential outcomes for sequential actions using a gated recurrent network that conditions on this latent history. 3) Estimating counterfactual survival curves under different treatment policies via targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) to ensure double robustness, with heterogeneity explored via tree-based stratification on the latent space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated methodological framework for estimating long-term, heterogeneous effects of dynamic treatment regimes from observational EHR data. It will provide a new benchmark for offline policy evaluation in healthcare and deliver a software tool for simulating counterfactual survival trajectories under alternative clinical strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a recurrent survival architecture designed for time-to-event outcomes with a formal causal model for sequential decisions, moving beyond static or short-term effect estimation to address the core challenge of long-term personalization in chronic care.

proposals.title: Structured Recognition Networks for Causal Survival Models: Improving Posterior Inference in Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data with censoring is crucial for personalized medicine. Variational inference (VI) enables scalable Bayesian inference for complex models like deep survival counterfactual networks, but standard mean-field recognition networks often fail to capture complex posterior dependencies, leading to biased uncertainty estimates and suboptimal treatment effect predictions. This limits the reliability of clinical decision support systems built on these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating structured, problem-specific inductive biases into the variational recognition network architecture—such as factorization reflecting the causal graph or temporal dependencies in survival data—will yield more accurate and efficient approximate posteriors, leading to improved estimation of uncertainty and heterogeneous treatment effects compared to generic black-box recognition networks.
proposals.method: We will develop and benchmark novel recognition network architectures within a causal deep survival model framework. Specifically, we will design: 1) Graph-structured recognition networks that factorize according to the assumed causal model (e.g., separating confounder, treatment, and outcome latent variables), 2) Temporal recognition networks using recurrent or attention-based modules to handle time-varying covariates and censoring mechanisms more explicitly. We will integrate these into a variational inference pipeline for models like Deep Survival Machines or CTM, evaluating them on synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., from IVF studies) using metrics for posterior approximation quality (ELBO, ESS) and HTE estimation accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic evaluation of structured VI architectures for causal survival analysis, delivering a practical toolkit for more reliable Bayesian inference in high-stakes clinical prediction problems. It will advance methodological research at the intersection of causal inference, survival analysis, and approximate Bayesian deep learning.
proposals.novelty: While VI is widely used, tailoring recognition network structure to explicitly model the dependencies inherent in causal survival graphs—combining Shalit's expertise in causal HTE estimation with advanced VI design—is a novel direction not extensively explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Deep Temporal Generative Models for Causal Survival Analysis with Hidden Confounders
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, time-to-event data with censoring is ubiquitous. Deep generative models for sequences, like RNNs and LSTMs, offer promise for handling complex temporal dependencies and hidden confounding, but their comparative performance for causal survival tasks remains unclear. A systematic, standardized benchmark is needed to guide method selection and development in this critical area.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep temporal generative models with explicit latent variable structures for hidden confounders will outperform standard RNN/LSTM baselines on tasks of counterfactual survival prediction and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation from observational data.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark suite using semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III) with known or plausible ground truth. We will implement and compare models including: 1) standard RNN/LSTM with adversarial balancing, 2) Deep Survival Machines, 3) a structured latent variable model (e.g., a variational recurrent neural network), and 4) a novel adaptation of the CEVAE (Causal Effect Variational Autoencoder) framework for survival outcomes. Evaluation will focus on time-dependent ATE/CATE estimation error and predictive calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark for deep generative models in causal survival analysis, establishing best practices for handling temporal hidden confounding. It will yield a public codebase and dataset suite to facilitate future method development and robust clinical model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for either causal inference or survival analysis separately, a unified evaluation focusing on the intersection—temporal data with hidden confounders and censoring—using a generative model lens is novel. The benchmark specifically targets architectures capable of joint modeling of observed and latent temporal processes.

proposals.title: Sequential Monte Carlo for Causal Survival Analysis: Flexible Inference for Time-to-Event Treatment Effects with Hidden Confounders
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine, but existing methods often rely on restrictive parametric assumptions or variational approximations that can bias results. Real-world clinical data is further complicated by potential hidden confounding and complex, non-linear covariate effects, which standard survival models struggle to capture accurately. A more flexible, non-parametric inference framework is needed to provide robust estimates of individualized treatment effects in survival settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) methods, specifically particle filtering, for inference in causal survival models will yield more accurate and robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects compared to variational approximations, especially in the presence of complex hidden confounder structures and non-proportional hazards.
proposals.method: We will develop a particle filtering framework for a latent variable model that jointly represents hidden confounders, treatment assignment, and time-to-event outcomes. The method will use a bootstrap particle filter to sequentially update the posterior distribution of latent states (e.g., hidden covariates, individual frailties) as censored event times are observed. We will integrate this with established causal identification strategies (e.g., g-methods, marginal structural models) to estimate conditional average treatment effects. The approach will be benchmarked against variational inference methods (e.g., VAEs) and recursive imputation trees on semi-synthetic and real-world clinical datasets, such as electronic health records for cardiovascular outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible toolkit for causal inference in survival analysis that avoids the mean-field and parametric constraints of variational methods. It will enable more accurate uncertainty quantification for individualized treatment effect estimates from censored data. The resulting methodology will be directly applicable to critical clinical questions, such as evaluating the long-term effect of treatments where confounding is suspected.
proposals.novelty: While particle filters are established in state-space models, their application for inference in causal models with hidden confounders and censored survival outcomes is novel. This approach moves beyond the dominant paradigm of variational autoencoders for latent variable causal inference, offering a fundamentally different and more exact approximation to the posterior.

proposals.title: Attention-Based Survival Transformers for Dynamic Treatment Effect Estimation with Time-Varying Confounding
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) in time-to-event settings with longitudinal, high-dimensional patient data is a core challenge in precision medicine. Existing methods, including Shalit's own work with recursively imputed trees, often struggle to model complex, long-range dependencies in patient histories and to identify which past clinical states are most prognostic for future treatment response. This limits their ability to provide reliable, individualized survival predictions under dynamic treatment regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating neural attention mechanisms, specifically transformer-based architectures, into survival analysis frameworks will improve the estimation of dynamic treatment effects by enabling the model to selectively focus on the most relevant past patient states and treatment decisions, thereby better adjusting for time-varying confounding.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Attention-Based Survival Transformer (ABST) model. The architecture will encode a patient's longitudinal record of treatments and time-varying covariates using a transformer encoder with multi-head self-attention, creating context-aware representations of clinical history. These representations will be used within a counterfactual outcome framework, building on pre-2026 advances in representation learning for causal inference, to estimate individual survival curves under different treatment sequences. The model will be trained with a loss function combining a time-to-event objective (e.g., Cox partial likelihood) with a balancing regularizer for time-varying confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, flexible deep learning method for dynamic survival analysis that explicitly models long-range dependencies in patient trajectories. It will provide a software tool and benchmarking results for estimating HTE in longitudinal settings, advancing the methodological toolkit for causal inference with survival outcomes using complex observational data.
proposals.novelty: While transformers are established in NLP, their application to dynamic causal survival analysis is nascent. The novelty lies in the specific integration of attention mechanisms to weight historical patient states for the explicit purpose of estimating time-varying treatment effects on censored outcomes, a synthesis not fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Streaming Causal Survival Forests for High-Frequency Time Series: Application to Real-Time Treatment Effect Estimation in ICU Video and Financial Data
proposals.motivation: The rise of high-frequency data streams, such as video feeds in intensive care units and tick-level financial data, presents a critical challenge for causal inference in time-to-event settings. Pre-2026 methods for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, like recursively imputed survival trees, are designed for static or low-frequency datasets and cannot handle the massive volume, velocity, and high-dimensionality of streaming data. This gap limits real-time, data-driven decision-making in critical domains like dynamic treatment regimes in medicine or algorithmic trading.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating online learning, efficient representation learning for high-dimensional inputs, and a novel censoring-aware update rule, we can adapt recursive imputation survival forest frameworks to provide consistent, real-time estimates of conditional treatment effects from non-stationary, high-frequency data streams.
proposals.method: We will develop a Streaming Causal Survival Forest (SCSF) model. The core is an ensemble of online survival trees, updated via a modified Mondrian Forest process for streaming data. To handle high-dimensional inputs (e.g., video frames), we will incorporate a pre-trained, frozen convolutional encoder (e.g., ResNet) to extract low-dimensional feature representations. A novel 'censored importance weight' will be used to update leaf node statistics, ensuring unbiased updates in the presence of streaming censored outcomes. The model will be implemented using the Vowpal Wabbit online learning framework and evaluated on simulated high-frequency survival data and a pre-2026 ICU video benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source framework for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from censored, high-frequency data streams. It will extend the causal survival analysis toolkit into the streaming domain, enabling new applications in real-time precision medicine and quantitative finance. We will also release a new benchmark dataset for streaming causal survival inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of online learning, deep representation learning, and non-parametric causal survival analysis into a single, scalable framework designed explicitly for streaming data, moving beyond the static-dataset paradigm of pre-2026 survival causal forests.

proposals.title: Lightweight, Real-Time Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects for Survival Outcomes on Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: Personalized medicine requires estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data, but existing methods like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time clinical decision support at the point of care. The proliferation of mobile health platforms and embedded clinical devices creates a pressing need for efficient, interpretable HTE models that can run with limited compute and memory, enabling immediate, data-driven treatment recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified survival model, combining a pruned, shallow tree structure for HTE identification with a lightweight parametric survival baseline (e.g., Weibull or piecewise exponential), can provide real-time, clinically meaningful estimates of conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for survival outcomes with minimal loss in accuracy compared to state-of-the-art ensemble methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RIST framework for efficiency. First, we will use a causal forest for preliminary feature selection to identify key treatment effect modifiers. A single, shallow (depth ≤ 4) decision tree will then be grown on the estimated CATEs to partition patients into interpretable subgroups. Within each leaf, a simple accelerated failure time (AFT) model with Weibull distribution will be fit separately for treated and control groups, leveraging pre-2026 work on efficient survival analysis. The entire pipeline will be optimized for embedded deployment using model quantization (e.g., 8-bit integers) and compiled via TensorFlow Lite or ONNX Runtime for mobile CPUs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first openly available framework for real-time survival HTE estimation on edge devices, validated on clinical benchmarks. It will bridge the gap between rigorous causal inference methodology and the practical constraints of mobile health, directly enabling point-of-care decision aids. The resulting model will be highly interpretable due to its tree-based structure.
proposals.novelty: While model compression exists for deep learning, applying these principles to causal survival forests—specifically by distilling a complex ensemble into a shallow tree with parametric survival models—is novel. This approach explicitly trades off the high predictive performance of ensembles for the speed, interpretability, and deployability required in clinical settings.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimal Control for Personalized Treatment Planning: Inferring Action Sequences from Desired Survival Trajectories
proposals.motivation: In clinical decision-making, physicians often have a desired outcome trajectory in mind (e.g., maintaining a patient's survival probability above a threshold). However, determining the sequence of treatment actions to achieve this trajectory is a complex inverse problem. Existing methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) or optimal policies do not directly solve for the action sequence required to follow a specific, user-defined outcome path over time, especially under censoring.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a learned causal survival model with inverse optimal control, we can reliably infer sequences of clinical actions that are most likely to drive a patient's observed data to match a desired time-to-event trajectory, accounting for time-varying confounding and censoring.
proposals.method: We will build on the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework for estimating HTE with time-to-event outcomes. First, we will train a RIST-based model to estimate counterfactual survival functions under sequences of actions. We will then formulate the inverse control problem as a constrained optimization: given a target survival probability curve, we search for the action sequence that minimizes the divergence between the predicted and target trajectories. This will be solved using a combination of gradient-based methods (where the model is differentiable) and tree-based policy search, incorporating robustness checks via simulation from biased offline data benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for personalized treatment planning that translates clinician intent (desired outcome paths) into actionable intervention sequences. It will extend the causal survival analysis toolkit beyond effect estimation to prescriptive sequence generation, with direct application to dynamic treatment regimes in chronic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While forward estimation of HTE and policy learning are established, inverting a learned survival model to solve for the action sequence that produces a specific outcome trajectory is a novel synthesis of causal survival analysis and inverse optimal control, moving from 'what works?' to 'what actions achieve this specific goal?'

proposals.title: Interpretable Distillation of Heterogeneous Temporal Treatment Effects via Causal Survival Rule Sets
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, complex machine learning models for survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation, such as recursively imputed survival trees, offer high predictive accuracy but are often black boxes. Clinicians need simple, interpretable rules to understand *when* and *for whom* a treatment is effective over time, especially in the presence of censored data and time-varying covariates, to facilitate trust and actionable decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex temporal dynamics learned by a state-of-the-art causal survival forest can be accurately distilled into a small set of human-readable, temporally-aware decision rules without significant loss of fidelity in estimating conditional average treatment effects (CATE) over time.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will train a high-capacity model for time-to-event HTE, such as a Recursively Imputed Survival Forest (RIST), on observational data with potential hidden confounding, using pre-2026 doubly robust or instrumental variable techniques for sensitivity. Second, we will treat this model as a 'teacher' and use a novel rule distillation procedure. This involves generating synthetic patient trajectories, querying the teacher for time-varying CATE estimates, and fitting a sparse, interpretable 'student' model in the form of a Survival Rule Set (SRS). The SRS will use logical conditions on covariates to assign patients to strata with distinct, parsimonious survival curve predictions under each treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method to translate black-box temporal causal predictions into auditable clinical decision pathways. It will contribute a new benchmark for interpretable causal survival analysis and offer a practical tool for generating hypotheses about treatment effect heterogeneity over time from complex observational datasets.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation and rule-based interpretability exist, applying them specifically to distill *time-varying heterogeneous causal effects* from censored survival data, while accounting for potential hidden confounding, is a novel synthesis of Uri Shalit's prior work on causal inference, survival analysis, and offline evaluation.

proposals.title: Overlap Engineering: A Diagnostic and Data-Design Framework for Reliable Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Reliable estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data critically depends on the overlap (common support) assumption. In clinical settings, this assumption is often violated, leading to model extrapolation and biased estimates for key patient subgroups. Pre-2026 methods like propensity score trimming or weighting can discard data or increase variance without a principled strategy to diagnose the problem's root cause or actively improve the data foundation for inference.
proposals.hypothesis: That the 'extrapolation load' for a causal query can be rigorously quantified, and that this diagnostic can guide the strategic design or synthesis of data—through targeted sampling or integration of auxiliary studies—to engineer sufficient overlap, enabling robust HTE estimation where traditional methods fail.
proposals.method: We will develop a diagnostic suite building on pre-2026 influence function-based metrics and propensity score distributions to quantify overlap deficiency and its impact on specific estimands. For engineering, we will formulate a constrained optimization framework to design a sampling strategy that minimizes extrapolation load, potentially integrating data from imperfect simulators or biased offline sources (leveraging Shalit's RL benchmark work). The core estimation will use flexible, overlap-aware models like recursively imputed trees for survival outcomes or doubly robust estimators with engineered weights.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent pipeline for causal inference that moves from diagnosing overlap problems to actively resolving them. It will deliver new benchmarks for evaluating estimators under engineered support and practical guidelines for data collection. This directly advances the goal of reliable personalized medicine by ensuring HTE estimates are grounded in empirical evidence.
proposals.novelty: While existing work diagnoses or mitigates overlap problems, the proactive 'engineering' of support through a principled data-design framework is novel. It shifts the paradigm from accepting data limitations to systematically guiding how data should be augmented or combined to answer causal questions reliably.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Survival Estimation Under Distributional Shift: A Latent Space Transport Approach
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for time-to-event outcomes from combined experimental (RCT) and observational data is crucial for personalized medicine, but existing methods rely heavily on covariate overlap between the datasets. In practice, such as when an RCT has strict eligibility criteria, overlap can be minimal, violating a core assumption and leading to biased or unstable estimates. This problem is particularly acute in clinical settings where observational data is abundant but systematically different from the experimental cohort.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning a shared latent representation where the covariate distributions are aligned, we can reliably transport survival effect estimates from an experimental dataset to a broader observational population, even when their raw feature distributions have minimal overlap.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel multi-task neural survival model with a domain-invariant variational autoencoder (VAE) to learn a latent space where the RCT and observational distributions are aligned via a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) penalty. Within this space, we will adapt the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to estimate censored survival outcomes and heterogeneous treatment effects. The model will incorporate a treatment-aware decoder and use importance weighting derived from the latent space to debias estimates for the target observational population.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for leveraging small, high-quality RCTs to infer treatment effects for broader real-world populations where experimental data is absent, directly addressing a key limitation in applied causal survival analysis. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating HTE estimation under distribution shift and practical software for clinical researchers.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation and causal survival analysis exist separately, their integration for HTE estimation under near-zero overlap is novel. The approach uniquely combines a latent alignment strategy tailored for censored outcomes with a robust, tree-based effect estimation framework, moving beyond simple weighting schemes.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Estimation from Imperfect Experiments: A Sensitivity Framework for Hidden Confounding in Randomized and Quasi-Experimental Data
proposals.motivation: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and small-scale experiments are the gold standard for causal inference, but they are not immune to bias. Minor protocol deviations, non-compliance, or unmeasured post-randomization variables can introduce hidden confounding, even in experimental settings. Pre-2026 methods for sensitivity analysis are primarily designed for observational studies, leaving a gap for quantifying robustness in ostensibly clean experimental data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending sensitivity analysis frameworks to explicitly model the potential for minor hidden confounding in experimental data, we can produce more reliable and interpretable confidence intervals for treatment effects, particularly for heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation in time-to-event and other clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel sensitivity model that parameterizes the degree of hidden confounding as a violation of the ignorability assumption within the experimental sample. Building on Shalit's work on survival HTE estimation with recursively imputed trees and hidden covariate models, we will integrate a sensitivity parameter into the loss function or splitting criterion of causal forest algorithms. This will allow the estimation of a robustness value—the minimum strength of confounding required to nullify a significant effect—for both average and subgroup-specific estimates. The framework will be validated on benchmark datasets and applied to re-analyze clinical trial data, such as the ICSI cycle study, where subtle confounding in the 'extended culture' intervention is plausible.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical toolkit for data analysts and clinical researchers to formally assess the fragility of causal conclusions from RCTs and small experiments. It will extend the applicability of sensitivity analysis from purely observational to mixed data settings, enhancing the credibility of evidence-based medicine. The method will directly contribute to more honest reporting of treatment effect estimates in the presence of real-world experimental imperfections.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis for observational studies is well-established, applying and tailoring these principles to protect inferences from randomized or quasi-experimental data is a novel direction. The integration of a continuous sensitivity parameter directly into tree-based HTE estimation algorithms for time-to-event outcomes represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard post-hoc analysis.

proposals.title: Diagnostic Testing and Adaptive Estimation for Treatment Effect Homogeneity in Observational Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: In observational clinical studies, researchers often assume constant treatment effects to simplify estimation, but this risks severe bias if heterogeneity exists. Conversely, defaulting to complex heterogeneous effect models can reduce efficiency and obscure clear conclusions when effects are truly homogeneous. A principled, data-driven framework to diagnose this assumption is missing, leading to either misleading simplifications or unnecessary complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage framework that first formally tests the constant treatment effect assumption and then adaptively selects an estimator based on the diagnostic result will yield more robust and statistically efficient estimates of causal effects in time-to-event and other clinical outcomes compared to approaches that assume the answer a priori.
proposals.method: Stage 1 (Diagnostic): We will develop and compare diagnostic tests, including a targeted maximum likelihood-based comparison of models with and without treatment-covariate interactions, analysis of residual dispersion after fitting a constant effect model, and placebo tests on negative control outcomes. Stage 2 (Adaptive Correction): Based on the diagnostic p-value, the framework will select either an efficient, doubly robust estimator (e.g., AIPW) valid under a constant effect, or a robust, regularized estimator for heterogeneous effects (e.g., a modified R-learner or causal survival forest) to prevent overfitting. The method will be validated using benchmark semi-synthetic datasets derived from real-world clinical cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, rigorous tool for applied researchers to justify or reject simplifications in effect modeling, improving the reliability of causal conclusions. It will advance methodological practice by moving from assumption-based to test-based model selection for treatment effect estimation, specifically within clinical prediction and survival analysis contexts.
proposals.novelty: While diagnostic tests exist for specific causal assumptions (e.g., balance), a formal, general-purpose test for treatment effect homogeneity is not standard. The novelty lies in inverting the standard workflow by testing the assumption first and creating an adaptive, end-to-end pipeline that links diagnosis to corrected estimation.

proposals.title: Calibrated Causal Measurement: Efficient Experimental Design and Estimation for Constant Treatment Effects
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in causal machine learning focuses on estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (CATE), often requiring complex models and large samples. However, for many foundational medical interventions (e.g., basic surgical techniques, standard drug doses), the average effect may be plausibly constant across broad, well-defined populations. In such settings, current methods are inefficient, as they expend resources modeling non-existent heterogeneity. A principled framework for validating constancy and then optimally estimating a single effect is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally testing for effect constancy and, when validated, treating the treatment effect as a metrological standard, we can design highly efficient, calibration-optimized experimental protocols that yield more precise and less biased estimates than standard methods applied to the same sample size.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we adapt high-sensitivity tests for treatment effect heterogeneity (e.g., using residual-based tests from survival analysis or model-agnostic CATE validation) to formally assess the constancy hypothesis. Second, if constancy is not rejected, we derive optimal experimental designs (e.g., tailored randomization probabilities, covariate-adjusted stratification) that minimize the variance of the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) estimator, treating it as a single calibration parameter. We will extend this to time-to-event outcomes with censoring, building on RIST (Recursively Imputed Survival Trees) for the heterogeneity test but using its structure to inform efficient, calibrated estimation under the constant-effect constraint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous methodology for scenarios where treatment effect heterogeneity is negligible, shifting the focus from prediction to precise measurement. It will yield concrete sample size savings and variance reduction formulas for randomized trials and observational studies under a constant-effect assumption, directly benefiting clinical trial design. The metrology analogy offers a new perspective on causal estimation as a calibration problem.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the core assumption of modern causal ML, proposing efficiency gains through simplification rather than complexity. The explicit linkage of causal estimation to metrological calibration and the development of optimal design rules conditional on a constancy test is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Leveraging Constant Treatment Effect Assumptions for Robust Causal Survival and Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Much of modern causal machine learning, including Shalit's work on heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) in survival and clinical settings, focuses on modeling complex effect variations. However, in many real-world scenarios, such as well-established medical interventions or standardized policies, the assumption of a constant (or near-constant) average treatment effect (ATE) may be plausible. Pre-2026 methods for HTE estimation, like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) or Causal Forests, can be inefficient or unstable when applied to such settings, potentially overfitting to noise. This project addresses the need for robust, simplified procedures that gain statistical power when effect homogeneity holds.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly assuming a constant treatment effect in specific, identifiable domains (e.g., certain clinical protocols or policy rollouts) will lead to more statistically efficient and robust estimators for time-to-event outcomes and offline policy evaluation, compared to generic heterogeneous effect models, without significant bias.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage testing and estimation framework. First, we will adapt pre-2026 heterogeneity tests (e.g., based on causal forest splitting or score-based tests) to formally assess the plausibility of a constant effect within a given dataset. For settings where the null of constant effect is not rejected, we will derive novel estimators: 1) For survival analysis with censoring, we will extend RIST by constraining the treatment effect to be constant across leaves, simplifying the recursive imputation. 2) For offline reinforcement learning with biased data, we will develop a weighted inverse propensity score estimator that targets the ATE, leveraging the constant-effect assumption to stabilize learning and reduce variance in policy value estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for domain scientists to validate constant-effect assumptions and apply simpler, more powerful causal models when appropriate. It will yield new, efficient estimators for causal survival analysis and offline policy evaluation that are robust in homogeneous settings. The results will clarify when complex HTE modeling is necessary versus when it constitutes over-engineering.
proposals.novelty: While most research seeks to model ever more complex heterogeneity, this work inverts the trend by rigorously justifying and exploiting homogeneity, a direction under-explored in pre-2026 causal machine learning. The integration of a formal homogeneity test with tailored constant-effect estimators for survival and RL domains is novel.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Latent Confounding in Observational Studies via Multi-Instrument Multi-Outcome Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Causal inference from observational data relies critically on the ignorability assumption, which is often unverifiable. In clinical and epidemiological studies, unmeasured confounding remains a primary threat to validity, leading to biased treatment effect estimates. Existing sensitivity analyses are often parametric or require strong assumptions, lacking a formal data-driven test for the presence of a latent confounder.
proposals.hypothesis: The pattern of covariation between multiple candidate instrumental variables and multiple measured outcomes can be used to detect the presence of a shared latent confounder. If such a latent structure is inferred, it formally invalidates the ignorability assumption based on observed covariates alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt principles from seismic tomography inverse modeling to a causal inference framework. The method will construct a network where nodes are candidate instruments (sources) and outcomes (sensors). We will develop a latent variable model, potentially using a variational autoencoder or a factor analysis structure, to test if the instrument-outcome covariance matrix is best explained by a low-rank latent confounding space. The core algorithm will involve fitting competing models (with and without latent confounders) and using a likelihood-ratio or score-based test to reject the null of no confounding.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel diagnostic tool to assess the plausibility of the ignorability assumption in practice, complementing existing sensitivity analyses. It will extend the toolkit for validating observational study designs, particularly in clinical settings with rich secondary measurements that can serve as candidate instruments or auxiliary outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in translating geophysical inverse problem techniques to causal diagnostics and in leveraging networks of multiple, potentially weak instruments and outcomes to gain power for detecting latent confounding, moving beyond single-instrument methods.

proposals.title: Robust Survival and Treatment Effect Estimation Under Latent Confounding via Sensitivity-Aware Deep Latent Variable Models
proposals.motivation: In observational clinical studies, standard causal and survival analysis methods rely on the strong ignorability assumption, which posits that all confounders are captured in the observed covariates. This assumption is frequently violated in practice due to unmeasured variables (e.g., socioeconomic factors, disease severity nuances), leading to biased estimates of treatment effects and survival curves. Pre-2026, methods for handling latent confounding in high-dimensional, time-to-event data with complex, non-linear relationships remain underdeveloped, posing a significant barrier to reliable evidence generation from real-world data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the latent confounding process within a deep latent variable framework and integrating a sensitivity analysis paradigm, we can produce more robust and interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival outcomes, even when ignorability is violated.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep generative model where the treatment assignment, high-dimensional observed covariates, and time-to-event outcome are jointly modeled as functions of a latent confounder. The architecture will combine a variational autoencoder (VAE) to infer the latent space with a neural network for the survival hazard (e.g., a DeepSurv variant). Crucially, we will integrate a sensitivity parameter that explicitly governs the strength of the dependency between the latent confounder and treatment assignment, building on pre-2026 sensitivity models like Rosenbaum's Gamma. Estimation will proceed via a tailored variational inference objective that accounts for censoring, enabling quantification of how conclusions vary with the degree of assumed residual confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for survival and treatment effect estimation that is robust to violations of ignorability, moving beyond point identification to a sensitivity-based framework. It will contribute a software tool and benchmarking suite for the causal survival analysis community, enabling practitioners to assess the robustness of their findings to latent confounding. The method will directly extend Uri Shalit's prior work on heterogeneous treatment effects with time-to-event outcomes and causal inference with imperfect data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of deep latent variable models for flexible confounder representation with formal, interpretable sensitivity analysis for survival outcomes, a combination not fully explored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond standard latent adjustment or instrumental variable methods by not requiring proxies and providing a continuous assessment of robustness.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Estimation via Constrained Adversarial Confounding Models for Time-to-Event Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data, especially in survival analysis, is critically hampered by unmeasured confounding. Pre-2026 methods often assume linear confounding bias or rely on unconstrained black-box models, which can lead to non-identifiable estimates or overfitting. There is a pressing need for a framework that explicitly models flexible, non-linear confounding while incorporating domain knowledge to ensure identifiability and robustness in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That imposing theoretically justified constraints (e.g., smoothness, monotonicity with respect to the propensity score) on a flexible model for non-linear confounding bias will yield identifiable, robust, and clinically interpretable estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in time-to-event data.
proposals.method: We will develop a penalized adversarial learning framework. A primary model (e.g., a survival neural net or recursively imputed tree) estimates the treatment effect, while an adversarial network models the non-linear confounding bias. This adversary will be constrained via architectural choices (e.g., spectral normalization) and penalty terms enforcing assumptions like Lipschitz smoothness and monotonic dependence on the estimated propensity. We will adapt the sensitivity model selection procedure from recent work on hidden covariates to test the robustness of the causal estimate across a spectrum of allowed flexibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for causal survival analysis that is more robust to complex confounding than standard linear models, yet more interpretable and stable than fully unconstrained alternatives. It will deliver a principled sensitivity analysis toolkit, allowing practitioners to understand how causal conclusions depend on plausible confounding mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The integration of constrained adversarial networks for bias modeling directly tackles the non-identifiability problem in non-linear settings, moving beyond linear sensitivity models or doubly robust methods that assume no unmeasured confounding.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Confounding Bias Correction for Causal Survival Analysis with Deep Propensity Networks
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is adjusting for confounding bias. Pre-2026 doubly robust methods often assume the bias is a linear function of the propensity score, which can be violated in complex clinical settings, leading to biased estimates. This is particularly critical for time-to-event outcomes, where censoring adds another layer of complexity, and non-linear relationships between treatment assignment and covariates are common.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the confounding bias as a flexible, non-linear function of the estimated treatment propensity will yield more accurate and robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects in survival and other clinical outcomes, especially when the underlying selection mechanism is complex.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of the R-learner and related doubly robust estimators by replacing the linear propensity-based bias correction term with a non-parametric function. Specifically, we will model this function using a feed-forward neural network that takes the estimated propensity score (from a logistic regression or a propensity network) as input. This network will be trained jointly with the outcome model within a deep learning architecture for survival analysis, such as a DeepSurv or a Deep Cox Proportional Hazards network, using appropriate loss functions that incorporate censoring. We will validate the method on benchmark datasets and the clinical IVF data from Shalit's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more flexible and theoretically grounded method for causal inference from observational data, directly improving the accuracy of HTE estimation in clinical prediction tasks. It will contribute a new, practical algorithm to the causal machine learning toolbox that relaxes a key modeling assumption, with software for the research community.
proposals.novelty: While non-linear outcome models are common, explicitly parameterizing the *bias function* as a non-linear network of the propensity score is a novel extension of the R-learner paradigm. This integrates deep learning for bias correction directly into the causal estimation objective, moving beyond simple linear residualization.

proposals.title: Generalizing Experimental Grounding for Multi-Treatment, Multi-Outcome Causal Estimation with Survival and Continuous Endpoints
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision-making often involves choosing among multiple treatment options while balancing multiple patient outcomes, such as survival, quality of life, and cost. Pre-2026 causal inference methods for heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) are predominantly developed for binary treatments and single outcomes, limiting their applicability to complex, real-world clinical settings. A unified framework for multi-treatment, multi-outcome HTE estimation is needed to provide comprehensive evidence for personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the experimental grounding framework, which integrates observational data with experimental evidence or biased simulators, can be successfully generalized to estimate individualized treatment rules when treatments are multi-valued (e.g., drug A, B, or C) and outcomes are multivariate (e.g., time-to-event and continuous measures).
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) method and related causal survival forests to handle multi-arm treatments by adapting the splitting criterion to maximize the variance in expected outcomes across treatment arms. For multiple outcomes, we will develop a multi-task learning architecture within the forest framework, using shared representations for efficiency and employing a composite loss function that weights survival (via Cox partial likelihood) and continuous outcomes. We will incorporate ideas from off-policy evaluation with biased data to handle confounding across multiple treatments, using propensity score stratification or doubly robust estimators within the tree nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source software tool for estimating personalized treatment recommendations from complex observational data with multiple interventions and endpoints. It will contribute a benchmark dataset and evaluation protocol for multi-treatment causal survival analysis, filling a critical gap in the HTE methodology landscape.
proposals.novelty: While multi-outcome prediction exists, jointly modeling causal effects of multiple treatments on multiple structured outcomes (especially survival) within a single, grounded forest-based framework is novel. The integration of biased simulator data for regularization in this multi-dimensional setting is also unexplored.

proposals.title: Estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects from Censored Survival Data with High-Dimensional Image Confounders
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, critical prognostic confounders are often embedded in high-dimensional data like medical images (e.g., histopathology slides, MRI scans). Pre-2026 methods for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) in survival analysis, such as Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST), primarily handle structured, low-dimensional covariates. This limits their applicability to modern, data-rich clinical studies where image-based phenotypes are key drivers of treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating deep representation learning for images into the RIST framework, we can robustly estimate HTEs from time-to-event data with censoring, maintaining calibration and improving accuracy over methods that ignore or crudely summarize high-dimensional visual confounders.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) method. A convolutional neural network (CNN) or vision transformer (pre-trained on natural or medical images) will be used to encode image-based confounders into a low-dimensional representation. This representation will be concatenated with traditional structured covariates and fed into the RIST algorithm, which recursively implicates potential outcomes to handle censoring. We will benchmark against alternatives: (1) using handcrafted image features, and (2) applying a separate survival model to image-derived representations before HTE estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for causal survival analysis with image confounders, directly applicable to oncology and radiology studies. It will yield a benchmark for how high-dimensional data integration affects the stability and performance of HTE estimation under censoring, a critical gap in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning for survival analysis exists, its integration into a causal HTE estimation framework designed for censored outcomes, specifically extending the RIST methodology, is novel. The focus on the robustness and scalability of the causal estimate, not just predictive performance, is a key advance.

proposals.title: A Prescriptive Framework for Robust Causal and Survival Estimators Under Data Scarcity and Measurement Noise
proposals.motivation: In clinical and biomedical research, estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) and survival outcomes from observational or small-scale experimental data is critical but fraught with challenges. Pre-2026, methods for causal inference and survival analysis are known to degrade under data scarcity, censoring, and noisy covariates, yet there is no systematic guidance on which robustification strategies work best for specific estimator families and data-quality profiles. This gap hinders reliable inference from the imperfect datasets common in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative efficacy of robustification techniques—such as informative Bayesian priors, synthetic data augmentation, and transfer learning from biased simulators—is predictable based on the interaction between the estimator's inductive biases, the degree of data scarcity, and the structure of the measurement noise.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive benchmarking suite simulating clinical scenarios with varying sample sizes, censoring rates, and noise models (e.g., hidden confounding, covariate measurement error). For each scenario, we will evaluate the performance of base estimators (e.g., recursively imputed survival trees, doubly robust learners) enhanced with specific robustification strategies. We will use pre-2026 causal learning and survival analysis metrics (e.g., precision-in-estimation-of-heterogeneous-effect, integrated Brier score) to measure performance preservation. A meta-learning analysis will then distill the results into a decision framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, evidence-based guide for researchers to select optimal robustification strategies for their specific data challenges, directly improving the reliability of HTE and survival estimates. It will also deliver open-source benchmark datasets and software implementing the prescriptive framework, filling a gap between methodological development and applied practice.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis is common, this work proactively engineers and prescribes mitigations, creating a systematic map from data pathology to solution. It uniquely integrates Shalit's expertise in causal survival analysis with robust ML strategies, moving beyond benchmarking to actionable guidance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Estimator Robustness in Causal Survival Analysis: A Geophysical Signal Processing Framework for Data Degradation Profiles
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, data is often limited, noisy, and subject to censoring. Pre-2026, while methods for handling such data exist, there is no systematic framework to quantitatively characterize how specific estimators degrade under controlled, worsening data conditions. This makes it difficult for practitioners to choose robust methods or allocate resources for data collection effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting geophysical signal processing evaluation paradigms, we can generate quantitative, estimator-specific degradation profiles that reveal non-linear failure modes and critical data quality thresholds for causal and survival learning methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the geophysical evaluation paradigm by constructing a benchmark suite of semi-synthetic datasets from high-quality clinical sources (e.g., MIMIC-IV, SEER), embedding known causal structures and survival mechanisms. We will then apply precise, graded degradation to sample size, censoring rate, and covariate noise. A panel of estimators from Uri Shalit's recent work—including Recursively Imputed Survival Trees, causal survival forests, and doubly robust learners—will be evaluated across this continuum. Their performance will be mapped to create 'degradation surfaces' analogous to seismic resolution plots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative framework for benchmarking estimator robustness under data scarcity in clinical causal inference. It will yield concrete guidance on which methods fail gracefully and under what specific data conditions, directly informing study design and analysis choices in resource-limited settings.
