proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary adaptation of a controlled degradation framework from geophysics to causal machine learning, moving beyond point-in-time benchmarks to a continuous characterization of estimator performance against a multi-dimensional data quality space.

proposals.title: Adaptive Sensitivity-Weighted Online Learning for Robust Causal Estimation in Non-Stationary Clinical Streams
proposals.motivation: In real-world clinical data streams, such as from wearables or EHRs, data quality (noise, bias, missingness) is volatile and non-stationary. Pre-2026 online learning and causal inference methods often assume stable data quality or use fixed robustness heuristics, leading to poor performance during periods of degraded data or missed opportunities during high-quality periods. This limits reliable, real-time estimation of treatment effects or survival risks in dynamic clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: An online learning algorithm that dynamically weights or rejects incoming data points based on a formal, instantaneously assessed sensitivity model will achieve more robust and efficient long-term estimation performance in non-stationary streams with volatile quality than static or quality-agnostic online methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an online gradient-based learning framework where each data point's contribution to the model update is scaled by an adaptive sensitivity weight. This weight is computed by a secondary online meta-learner that continuously estimates local data quality metrics (e.g., noise via prediction residuals, potential bias via covariate shift detection, and effective sample size). The meta-learner will be informed by a formal sensitivity model for causal estimation, building on Shalit's previous work with hidden confounding and survival analysis, allowing the primary learner to be conservative (low weight) when quality is low and aggressive (high weight) when quality is high.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled online learning algorithm with built-in, adaptive robustness to data quality fluctuations. It will deliver a new benchmark for online causal and survival prediction in volatile real-world environments. The method will be validated on existing biased offline clinical benchmarks and imperfect simulators, extending their use to streaming contexts.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a formal, continuously updated sensitivity model directly into the online learning update rule, enabling real-time trade-offs between robustness and learning efficiency, is a novel fusion of causal sensitivity analysis and adaptive online optimization not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Sensitivity of Causal Survival Estimators to Experimental Data Quality: A Systematic Benchmarking Study
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine, but real-world experimental datasets (e.g., from clinical trials) are often limited in size and subject to noise from measurement error or imperfect adherence. Pre-2026 methodological advances, like recursively imputed survival trees, provide powerful tools for HTE estimation, but their robustness to degraded data quality is poorly understood. This gap hinders reliable application in practice, where researchers must decide if their data is sufficient for a given method.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance degradation of state-of-the-art causal survival estimators (e.g., based on recursive imputation, survival forests, and doubly robust methods) is non-linear and method-dependent, with some estimators being more sensitive to sample size reduction and others to noise in treatment assignment or censoring mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will design a comprehensive simulation study, generating semi-synthetic data with known ground-truth HTEs. We will systematically degrade data quality along axes: sample size, noise in treatment assignment (e.g., via propensity score miscalibration), and informative censoring. We will benchmark estimators including Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST), causal survival forests, and Cox-based doubly robust estimators. Performance will be evaluated using calibrated metrics like the C-for-benefit and RMSE in estimated treatment effects across heterogeneity subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a principled sensitivity analysis framework for causal survival estimation, providing clear guidance on the data requirements for reliable HTE discovery. It will yield practical recommendations for method selection based on anticipated data limitations, directly informing the design and analysis of clinical trials and observational studies with survival outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analyses exist for standard causal estimators, a focused, systematic investigation of data quality degradation for *survival* HTE methods, integrating realistic experimental flaws like imperfect randomization and censoring, is novel. This bridges Uri Shalit's prior work on causal survival methods and benchmarking with biased data.

proposals.title: Robustness Profiles for Causal Survival Analysis: A Sensitivity Framework for Unmeasured Confounding and Model Misspecification
proposals.motivation: Causal inference from observational data, especially for time-to-event outcomes, relies on strong untestable assumptions like unconfoundedness and correct model specification. Current practice often involves binary sensitivity checks or ad-hoc robustness tests, which fail to quantify how conclusions degrade with assumption violations. This is a critical gap for clinical applications, where treatment effect estimates inform high-stakes decisions, and a more nuanced, quantitative assessment of result credibility is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly modeling the influence of assumption violations on causal estimates, we can construct interpretable robustness profiles that quantify the 'tipping point' at which substantive conclusions change, providing a more practical and informative assessment than binary sensitivity analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that extends sensitivity models (e.g., Rosenbaum bounds, tilting models) and integrates them with machine learning estimators for survival outcomes (building on our prior work with Recursively Imputed Survival Trees). The core technical approach involves defining a parameterized class of violations (e.g., strength of a latent confounder, degree of non-linearity) and using combined observational and experimental data (where available) to fit a meta-model that maps violation magnitude to the resulting distribution of the target causal estimand (e.g., Conditional Average Treatment Effect) and its confidence intervals. This will be implemented via a targeted adversarial optimization procedure that perturbs the data-generating process to find the minimal violation needed to alter the conclusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for applied researchers to move from asking 'are my assumptions valid?' to 'how robust are my conclusions to plausible violations?'. It will contribute a formal methodology for generating robustness profiles (e.g., contour plots) for causal survival analysis, directly enhancing the credibility and communication of findings in clinical and epidemiological studies.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis exists, the novelty lies in the integrated, quantitative profiling approach that jointly handles multiple assumption types (unconfoundedness, functional form) and is specifically tailored for complex, censored time-to-event outcomes using modern machine learning estimators, moving beyond linear models and binary outcomes.

proposals.title: Joint Inversion Tests for Causal Assumptions: Validating Ignorability with Combined Observational and Experimental Data
proposals.motivation: Causal inference from observational data relies on untestable assumptions like ignorability. While experimental data (e.g., RCTs) can provide gold-standard evidence, it is often limited in size or scope. A critical pre-analysis challenge is determining whether the core assumptions required to combine or transport estimates across these data modalities are tenable, a problem lacking formal statistical tests. This creates a risk of biased conclusions in high-stakes domains like clinical treatment effect estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: That formal hypothesis tests, analogous to cross-gradient tests in geophysical joint inversion, can be developed to statistically assess the compatibility of observational and experimental datasets under shared causal assumptions, thereby providing a data-driven validation or falsification mechanism for assumptions like ignorability and consistency.
proposals.method: We will adapt the geophysical cross-gradient framework, which tests for structural consistency between models derived from different physical data (e.g., seismic vs. gravity). Our method will treat potential outcome surfaces or propensity score distributions as the underlying 'structures.' We will develop a test statistic based on the discrepancy between estimates (e.g., of baseline risks or covariate distributions) derived solely from the experimental data and those derived from the observational data under the assumed causal model. This will involve using machine learning for nuisance parameter estimation (e.g., via Bayesian Additive Regression Trees or targeted maximum likelihood estimation) and deriving the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic under the null hypothesis of assumption compatibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a principled, quantitative tool to 'stress-test' their causal assumptions before committing to a combined-data analysis. It will advance methodological rigor in fields like healthcare, where leveraging both RCT and electronic health record data is increasingly common. The output will be a practical testing procedure with software implementation.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analyses for unobserved confounding exist, this approach is novel in directly testing dataset compatibility for assumption validation, drawing a non-obvious analogy from geophysics. It moves beyond qualitative checks or single-dataset diagnostics to a formal joint test using multiple data types.

proposals.title: Validating Latent State Models in Healthcare: A Testable Framework for Partially Observed Causal and Survival Systems
proposals.motivation: Latent variable models, such as hidden Markov models for disease progression or latent confounder models for causal inference, are widely used in clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation. Their validity hinges on untestable assumptions about the latent state's structure and relationship to observed data. In healthcare, sporadic ground-truth measurements (e.g., a definitive diagnostic test) provide rare but critical opportunities to audit these assumptions, yet current methods use this data only for model fitting, not for formal assumption testing.
proposals.hypothesis: That sporadic, direct observations of a latent state can be leveraged to construct statistically rigorous hypothesis tests for the model's core assumptions (e.g., conditional independence, parametric emission distributions, Markov transitions), thereby transforming partial observability from a modeling challenge into a validation resource.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that treats the partially observed latent state as a "testable unit" within a dual learning system. For a given assumed model (e.g., a hidden Markov model with a specific emission distribution), we will derive the implied joint distribution of the sporadic ground-truth observations and the routinely observed covariates. Using influence function-based semiparametric theory and selective inference techniques, we will construct targeted goodness-of-fit tests, such as conditional Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests or score-based tests, that are valid under censoring and missing data mechanisms common in survival and observational studies. The methodology will be integrated into recursive imputation trees and doubly robust causal estimation pipelines to audit latent confounder models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal methodology for statistically testing the foundational assumptions of latent variable models using partial ground truth, moving beyond predictive performance metrics. It will strengthen causal claims in settings with unmeasured confounding by enabling validation of latent structure. The resulting open-source software will allow practitioners to audit model assumptions before relying on them for clinical decision-making or treatment effect heterogeneity estimation.
proposals.novelty: Existing work uses partial observations for model training or imputation, but not for direct, statistical testing of the latent model's core structural assumptions. The approach uniquely bridges causal inference, survival analysis, and latent variable model checking by formalizing the validation process within a semiparametric, testable framework.

proposals.title: Testing the No Unobserved Confounding Assumption in Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation: A Combined Observational-Experimental Framework
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data relies critically on the assumption of no unobserved confounding, which is fundamentally untestable with that data alone. This severely limits the reliability of HTE models for clinical decision support. The increasing availability of combined datasets, where a smaller randomized trial is nested within a larger observational cohort, provides a unique opportunity to formally assess this core assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic discrepancies in estimated HTEs between the observational and experimental subsets, after adjusting for observed covariates, can be used to construct a statistical test for the presence of residual confounding, thereby validating or invalidating the no unobserved confounding assumption for specific patient subgroups.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage testing procedure. First, we will estimate HTEs using state-of-the-art methods like R-learner or causal survival forests on the observational data, assuming unconfoundedness. Second, we will use the experimental data to compute a 'bias function' for these estimates across the covariate space. We will then formulate a hypothesis test where the null is zero bias, using a bootstrapping or permutation-based approach that accounts for the nested data structure and censoring in time-to-event outcomes, extending ideas from our prior work on recursively imputed trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, data-driven test for the plausibility of the unconfoundedness assumption in HTE estimation within a combined data setting. It will deliver a practical diagnostic tool for applied researchers in clinical epidemiology and comparative effectiveness research, increasing the credibility of HTE models derived from observational data.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analyses for unobserved confounding exist, they are not formal statistical tests. Our approach is novel in leveraging the unique information in combined observational-experimental data to construct an actual hypothesis test, moving from qualitative plausibility arguments to quantitative rejection of the core assumption.

proposals.title: Disentangling Confounders via Blind Source Separation Priors in Deep Latent Variable Models for Causal Effect Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data requires adjusting for confounding, but standard methods often assume confounders are fully observed. In practice, high-dimensional proxies contain a mixture of true confounding sources and independent noise, leading to biased estimates. Pre-2026, deep latent variable models (e.g., VAEs) have been used to infer hidden confounders, but they lack identifiability guarantees, risking that learned representations mix independent factors of variation.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating structural constraints inspired by geophysical blind source separation (BSS) techniques, specifically Independent Component Analysis (ICA), into the training of deep latent variable models will enforce the disentanglement of independent latent sources, yielding more identifiable and accurate representations of true confounding for downstream causal adjustment.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep generative model (e.g., a structured VAE) where the prior and loss function are designed to promote statistical independence among latent subgroups, mirroring ICA's core objective. The architecture will include a multi-head encoder to separate latent factors, with a regularization loss based on measures of statistical independence (e.g., maximum mean discrepancy or contrastive learning). This model will be integrated into a causal inference framework, such as a treatment-effect VAE, where the disentangled latents are used for propensity score estimation or direct outcome modeling in survival and continuous treatment settings relevant to Shalit's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for learning more robust and identifiable latent confounder representations from high-dimensional observational data. It will advance the field of causal inference with hidden confounding by offering a principled, BSS-inspired approach to source separation, potentially improving the reliability of heterogeneous treatment effect estimates in clinical and survival applications.
proposals.novelty: While ICA is established in signal processing, its adaptation as a structural prior for identifiability in deep causal latent variable models is novel. This moves beyond generic variational inference by explicitly enforcing a separation of independent sources, a constraint not typically used in causal representation learning pre-2026.

proposals.title: Deconfounding Survival Outcomes with Deep Latent Variable Models and Causal Grounding
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational time-to-event data is critical for personalized medicine but is confounded by unmeasured variables. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees, often rely on observed proxies, which may inadequately capture complex latent confounding structures, leading to biased effect estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a deep latent variable model (e.g., a Variational Autoencoder) with a causal grounding framework can learn richer, structured representations of confounding from high-dimensional observed covariates, leading to less biased estimates of individualized survival treatment effects compared to methods using only observed data.
proposals.method: We will develop a model combining a VAE to infer a latent confounder distribution from high-dimensional pre-treatment covariates with a treatment-specific survival network (e.g., a Deep Survival Cox model). The grounding approach will be implemented via a regularization objective that enforces balance in the latent space, such as minimizing the Maximum Mean Discrepancy between treated and control latent distributions. We will adapt the recursive imputation logic for censored outcomes within this architecture, using the learned latent representation for more robust imputation and effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new method for causal survival analysis that explicitly models latent confounding, extending the grounding principle to deep generative settings. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating latent confounder adjustment in high-dimensional observational survival datasets.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs have been used for domain adaptation and representation learning, their integration with causal grounding techniques for time-to-event outcomes, specifically to structure the latent space for deconfounding, is a novel synthesis of Shalit's prior work on causal survival analysis and deep generative models.

proposals.title: Domain Adaptation for Causal Survival Analysis: Correcting Covariate Shift Between Experimental and Observational Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine but is confounded by covariate shift between the experimental trial population used for model development and the broader clinical population where models are deployed. This shift, if unaddressed, leads to biased predictions and poor generalization of survival and time-to-event outcomes in real-world settings, undermining clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying domain adaptation techniques to explicitly align the feature distributions of experimental (source) and observational (target) domains will significantly reduce bias in the estimation of conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for survival outcomes, leading to more generalizable and reliable models.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of recursively imputed survival trees (RIST) for HTE estimation by integrating a domain-adversarial neural network (DANN) component. The model will learn a shared representation that is predictive of the time-to-event outcome while being invariant to the domain (experimental vs. observational), as enforced by a gradient reversal layer. We will benchmark this DANN-RIST model against standard RIST and other causal survival methods on semi-synthetic datasets simulating realistic covariate shifts and on real clinical data, such as the ICSI cycle dataset, where trial eligibility criteria induce such shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated method for robust off-policy evaluation of treatment effects under distributional shift. It will contribute a new benchmark for causal survival analysis that explicitly accounts for domain mismatch, advancing the field towards more deployable clinical prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is established in machine learning and causal inference methods exist for HTE estimation, their integration for censored survival outcomes under covariate shift is novel. This directly addresses a key, often-overlooked practical barrier in translating causal findings from trials to observational clinical practice.

proposals.title: Bayesian Hierarchical Data Fusion for Causal Survival Analysis: Integrating RCTs and Observational Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for time-to-event outcomes is crucial for personalized medicine but is hampered by data limitations. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) provide unbiased causal estimates but are often small and homogeneous, while large observational datasets offer broader patient representation but are confounded. Pre-2026, methods to robustly fuse these data types for HTE estimation remain underdeveloped, limiting the generalizability of findings from either source alone.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian hierarchical model that treats RCT and observational data as related but distinct sources can share statistical strength to produce more accurate and generalizable estimates of individualized survival treatment effects than models using either dataset in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical model where the prior distribution for the HTE function in the observational data is centered on the HTE function learned from the RCT, with the degree of shrinkage controlled by a hyperparameter learned from the data. The base learner for the HTE function will be an extension of recursively imputed survival trees (RIST), adapted within a Bayesian additive regression trees (BART) framework for flexible non-parametric estimation. Inference will be performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), allowing for full uncertainty quantification of the fused estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, uncertainty-aware framework for combining experimental and real-world evidence in survival analysis. It will extend the RIST methodology for causal survival analysis to the data fusion setting, delivering a software tool for clinical researchers. The method will be validated on benchmark tasks and a clinical case study on fertility treatment outcomes using electronic health records and trial data.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian hierarchical models exist for meta-analysis, their application for fusing individual-level RCT and observational data to estimate *individualized* survival treatment effects is novel. This approach directly addresses the core challenge of trading off internal validity (from RCTs) and external validity (from observational data) within a single causal inference model.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests with Partial Instrumental Variables: Estimating Treatment Effects from Imperfectly Randomized Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical settings, treatment assignment is often influenced by unobserved confounding, such as physician preference or patient frailty, biasing standard observational analyses. While instrumental variable (IV) methods can address this, finding a perfect instrument is rare. However, modern healthcare data often contains 'partial experiments'—subsets of patients from randomized trials or protocol-driven care—which can act as imperfect instruments, but existing methods fail to leverage this structure for time-to-event outcomes with censoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally modeling the assignment mechanism in a partially experimental dataset as a weak or invalid instrument, we can develop a causal forest-based estimator that consistently estimates heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) on survival outcomes, even in the presence of unmeasured confounding in the observational portion of the data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Generalized Random Forest (GRF) framework to incorporate an IV-like splitting rule that prioritizes splits where the local covariance between the treatment and the partial instrument (e.g., trial participation indicator) is strong, adapting the 'instrumental forests' approach to censored data. The method will use a two-stage survival model: first, a forest predicts treatment assignment using the instrument and covariates; second, a forest estimates the effect on a transformed survival outcome (e.g., using IPCW or AFT models) using the predicted treatment. We will benchmark against standard causal survival forests and two-stage IV survival models using simulated and real-world clinical datasets with known partial randomization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, robust method for estimating HTEs from hybrid experimental-observational datasets, common in pragmatic trials and electronic health records. It will advance the field of causal inference for survival analysis by integrating IV theory with non-parametric, machine learning-based estimation, directly addressing a key challenge in clinical prediction and treatment heterogeneity research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of instrumental variable concepts with recursive partitioning for censored outcomes, specifically designed for the increasingly common but under-utilized data structure of partial experiments, moving beyond the assumption of a perfect instrument or completely randomized data.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Correction of Causal Effect Estimates from Observational to Experimental Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational data is crucial for personalized medicine but is confounded by unmeasured variables. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) provide gold-standard evidence but are expensive and small. A critical challenge is efficiently adapting a model trained on large, biased observational datasets using the limited, unbiased data from a small RCT.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework can learn a prior from diverse observational datasets that enables rapid, data-efficient adaptation to the experimental distribution, yielding more accurate and calibrated HTE estimates than standard fine-tuning or deconfounding methods when experimental samples are few.
proposals.method: We propose a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework where each 'task' is defined by a distinct observational dataset (with simulated or known confounding). The inner-loop adaptation will be trained to minimize observational loss, while the outer-loop meta-objective will be designed to optimize performance after adaptation to a small, held-out 'experimental' set (simulated from the task's ground-truth model). We will extend this to survival outcomes using a meta-learning variant of the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) estimator from Shalit's prior work, ensuring compatibility with time-to-event data and censorship.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for integrating large observational and small experimental studies in causal inference. It will yield a benchmark for few-shot causal adaptation and theoretical insights into the conditions under which meta-learned priors improve data efficiency for debiasing.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for domain adaptation, its application for few-shot correction of *causal* estimators, specifically bridging the observational-experimental gap for HTEs and survival outcomes, is novel and directly addresses a core bottleneck in translational clinical research.

proposals.title: Robust Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Survival Analysis via Distributionally Robust Optimization
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from observational time-to-event data relies on strong assumptions like unconfoundedness and correct model specification. In clinical settings, these assumptions are often violated in subtle ways, leading to biased estimates and unreliable treatment recommendations. Pre-2026 methods, including Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees, lack formal guarantees against such mild violations, limiting their robustness in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating HTE estimation in survival analysis as a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) problem will produce estimates that are stable and reliable under mild violations of core causal and censoring assumptions, compared to standard non-robust methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of recursively imputed survival trees (RIST) by embedding it within a DRO formulation. Specifically, we will define an uncertainty set around the empirical data distribution using a divergence metric (e.g., f-divergence or Wasserstein distance). The estimation objective will be to minimize the worst-case expected loss over this uncertainty set, guarding against perturbations that represent assumption violations. We will develop an efficient algorithm that iterates between robust loss minimization and tree-based prediction, building on Shalit's prior RIST architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, theoretically grounded method for robust HTE estimation in survival analysis that is directly applicable to clinical decision support. It will deliver a software tool and benchmarks demonstrating improved performance under simulated violations of unconfoundedness and censoring mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While DRO has been applied in predictive modeling, its integration into the specific causal estimation task of HTEs for censored survival outcomes, particularly within a tree-based recursive imputation framework, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond standard sensitivity analysis by building robustness directly into the estimation objective.

proposals.title: Causal Survival Forests with Semi-Synthetic Data Fusion: Scaling Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation to Massive Observational Cohorts Using Mini-RCTs
proposals.motivation: In healthcare and tech, massive observational datasets exist, but causal inference requires expensive randomized trials (RCTs). Conducting large RCTs for every treatment or subgroup is infeasible. Pre-2026 methods for combining observational and experimental data often struggle with high-dimensionality, complex censoring in time-to-event data, and the 'small n, large p' problem when the RCT is tiny relative to the observational data.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel data fusion architecture, which treats the small RCT as an unbiased but incomplete 'seed' and uses it to recursively debias a high-dimensional observational survival model, can produce robust estimates of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) that are scalable and statistically efficient.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework to a semi-synthetic data setting. The core algorithm will be a two-stage forest: first, a representation learning layer (e.g., a survival neural net or multi-task forest) trained on the massive observational data to model complex, hidden covariates and baseline hazards. Second, a causal forest layer, trained primarily on the small RCT data, will use the learned representations as privileged covariates. We will develop a targeted regularization loss that penalizes divergence between observational and RCT-based predictions in the representation space, akin to domain adaptation, ensuring the observational data guides without dominating the causal estimate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source method for leveraging web-scale observational data (e.g., electronic health records, user logs) corrected by minimal RCTs. It will advance the field of causal inference with biased offline data by offering a statistically sound solution for HTE estimation in survival outcomes when experiments are extremely limited. The resulting benchmarks will set a new standard for data-scarce causal evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods like RIST or doubly robust estimation combine data sources, our approach uniquely focuses on the 'massive observational, tiny RCT' regime, explicitly using representation learning to compress high-dimensional confounding into a low-dimensional space that can be reliably corrected by a minuscule experiment, a non-trivial extension of current causal survival forests.

proposals.title: Streaming Causal Survival Forests: Real-Time Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects from Embedded Experiments
proposals.motivation: In clinical and digital health settings, embedded experiments (e.g., adaptive trials, A/B tests) generate a continuous stream of time-to-event data with right-censoring. Pre-2026 methods for estimating Heterogeneous Treatment Effects (HTEs) in survival analysis, like Recursively Imputed Survival Trees, are batch-oriented, requiring full dataset retraining. This creates a lag in learning, delaying personalized treatment decisions in real-time care streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a survival forest algorithm can be adapted to process data in an online fashion, enabling consistent, real-time estimation of HTEs from a trickle of new experimental data points, without retraining from scratch.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Tree framework to a streaming context. The core technical innovation is a "downscaled" forest update rule: as new (potentially censored) data points arrive, we will perform a selective update of leaf nodes in existing trees using a censoring-aware loss, inspired by online gradient boosting. We will incorporate a hidden state to model evolving baseline hazards and use a causal targeting score to debias the streaming data, akin to techniques from offline policy evaluation. The algorithm will maintain a fixed computational footprint suitable for embedded systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated method for real-time causal survival analysis from embedded experiments. It will contribute a publicly available benchmark for streaming HTE estimation and offer a practical tool for continuous learning in adaptive clinical trials and digital health interventions.
proposals.novelty: While online learning and survival analysis exist separately, their fusion for real-time causal effect estimation from censored data is unexplored. The novelty lies in the censoring-aware, incremental update of a causal forest structure, moving beyond batch-processing paradigms.

proposals.title: Experimental Design via Confounder-Aware Subpopulation Targeting: An Active Learning Framework for Causal Inference
proposals.motivation: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are the gold standard for causal inference but are expensive and ethically constrained. Observational data is abundant but confounded. Current methods use observational data to estimate treatment effects or to debias estimates, but they do not systematically inform how to collect new experimental data more efficiently. There is a critical gap in using learned confounding structures to actively plan future experiments, especially for time-to-event outcomes common in clinical studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model of hidden confounding, fitted on observational data, can be used to design a subsequent experimental data collection strategy—such as a sequential RCT—that maximizes information gain per sample for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs), leading to more statistically efficient and cost-effective trials.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage active learning framework. First, we will fit a flexible survival model with latent confounders (e.g., a variational autoencoder for survival data with treatment assignment) to the observational dataset. Second, we will use this fitted model to compute an information-theoretic acquisition function, such as the expected reduction in variance of Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) estimates. This function will guide the sequential enrollment of patients into a prospective study by prioritizing subpopulations where the observational model is most uncertain due to unresolved confounding. We will benchmark our method against standard RCT designs using simulated data and a real-world clinical dataset on infertility treatment (extending Shalit's prior work).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between observational causal inference and optimal experimental design. It will yield a concrete algorithm for experimenters to prioritize recruitment in clinical trials, potentially reducing required sample sizes. The contribution is both a new formal framework and practical software for confounder-aware experimental planning in survival and general outcome settings.
proposals.novelty: While active learning for experimental design exists, its integration with models specifically designed to uncover hidden confounding from observational data is novel. The approach inverts the standard causal inference pipeline: instead of just correcting observational estimates, it uses the inferred confounding structure as a prior to actively seek the most informative experimental data.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Hidden Confounding in Survival and Treatment Effect Models via Observational-Grounding Divergence
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data is central to personalized medicine but critically relies on the unverifiable assumption of no unmeasured confounding. Pre-2026 methods for sensitivity analysis often require specifying a confounding strength parameter, which is unknowable. A model-based diagnostic that can flag the likely presence of hidden confounding without such specification would greatly enhance the reliability of causal conclusions in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: The divergence between a model trained on purely observational data and a model regularized or informed by grounded, mechanistic knowledge or experimental data (even if partial) can serve as a statistically powerful diagnostic for the presence of hidden confounding in the observational dataset.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-model framework. First, train a high-capacity observational model (e.g., a recursively imputed survival forest or deep Cox model) on the target observational data. Second, train a 'grounded' model using the same architecture but incorporating bias-reducing techniques from offline reinforcement learning and imperfect simulators, such as pessimism or doubly robust regularization, applied to any available experimental fragments or mechanistic constraints. We will then formalize a hypothesis test where the test statistic is a carefully chosen divergence (e.g., a calibrated discrepancy in predicted treatment effect heterogeneity or survival curves) between the two models' outputs, with its null distribution established via tailored resampling under the no-hidden-confounding scenario.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, model-agnostic tool for data-driven confounding detection, moving beyond qualitative sensitivity plots. It will yield a novel benchmark for evaluating causal inference methods on observational data where hidden confounding is suspected. The resulting methodology will directly inform the validity of clinical prediction models for treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: While model mismatch is used for domain adaptation, its systematic application as a formal diagnostic for hidden confounding in causal inference, particularly by leveraging Shalit's expertise in bridging causal survival analysis and offline RL with biased data, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: Federated Causal Survival Forests: Privacy-Preserving Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects from Censored Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from time-to-event data is crucial for personalized medicine but requires large, diverse datasets. Pre-2026, clinical data is often siloed across institutions due to privacy regulations like HIPAA and GDPR, preventing pooled analysis. Federated learning offers a solution, but existing methods lack robust integration with causal survival analysis and the handling of censored outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, built upon recursive imputation and tree-based survival models, can accurately estimate HTEs from censored data across multiple sites without sharing raw patient records, while maintaining privacy guarantees comparable to centralized analysis.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) method into a federated architecture. The core algorithm will use a federated averaging scheme where each site locally grows survival trees based on its private data, sharing only model parameter updates (e.g., tree splits and hazard estimates) secured via differential privacy or secure multi-party computation. We will adapt the recursive imputation procedure for censored outcomes to operate within this federated setting, ensuring consistent treatment effect estimation across partitions. The framework will be validated on biased offline clinical datasets, simulating imperfect real-world data silos.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first federated learning method specifically designed for causal survival analysis, enabling multi-institutional HTE studies previously infeasible due to privacy constraints. It will deliver an open-source software package and benchmarking protocols for federated causal inference, advancing the application of personalized treatment rules in oncology and cardiology using real-world data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of Shalit's prior work on RIST for HTE estimation with a privacy-preserving federated framework, specifically addressing the challenges of right-censoring and confounding in a distributed data setting, which pre-2026 federated learning literature has not comprehensively solved.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Multi-Domain Calibration for Survival and Treatment Effect Prediction
proposals.motivation: Clinical prediction models trained on multi-center data must be calibrated across latent, unlabeled domains (e.g., hospitals, patient subgroups) to ensure reliable deployment. Pre-2026 domain adaptation and calibration methods typically require explicit domain labels, which are often ambiguous, unreliable, or unavailable in real-world observational data, especially in survival analysis and causal inference settings. This limits the practical application of robust predictive models in heterogeneous clinical populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly learning latent domain representations and performing calibration adjustments within these inferred groups, we can achieve multi-domain calibration without requiring known domain labels, leading to more reliable and transportable predictions of time-to-event outcomes and heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that integrates a latent variable model (e.g., a mixture model or a variational autoencoder) to infer soft domain assignments from high-dimensional covariates. These assignments will then inform a multi-domain calibration layer, extending Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ) or Platt scaling to operate on weighted, probabilistic domain memberships. The framework will be specifically tailored for survival data, incorporating the calibration of predicted survival curves and conditional average treatment effect (CATE) estimates under potential censoring, building on concepts from recursively imputed survival trees and causal inference for time-to-event outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic framework for unsupervised multi-domain calibration, directly applicable to survival and treatment effect prediction from biased, multi-source observational data. It will extend the toolkit for robust machine learning in healthcare by relaxing a key practical bottleneck—the need for clean domain labels. We will release benchmark datasets and evaluation protocols to assess calibration under latent domain shift.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation and calibration are established fields, the combination for unsupervised domain discovery specifically in the context of censored survival outcomes and causal effect estimation is novel. The approach moves beyond methods that require pre-specified, known domain partitions, addressing a more realistic and challenging data scenario.

proposals.title: When Calibration is Not Enough: Characterizing Distributional Shifts that Break OOD Generalization Despite Multi-Domain Calibration
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction and treatment effect estimation, models are often validated for calibration across multiple source domains, which is assumed to support out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. However, pre-2026 literature shows that calibration, even across domains, may be insufficient for reliable deployment in new clinical settings. This gap poses a significant risk for models used in survival analysis and heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, where failures can have serious consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exist specific, structured distributional shifts—particularly in hidden covariates or causal structures—that preserve multi-domain calibration but lead to systematic OOD prediction failure, and that these failures can be characterized by violations of specific causal invariance conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework and simulation testbed to generate distributional shifts that preserve calibration but break generalization. Using methods from Shalit's recent work, we will employ causal survival forests and hidden variable models to create scenarios where calibration holds across source domains. We will then rigorously test OOD performance under target shifts in hidden confounders, mediator distributions, and instrumental variable strengths, using benchmarks for offline RL with biased data to simulate imperfect clinical environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical caveat to the use of multi-domain calibration as a proxy for robustness, offering a formal characterization of its insufficiency. It will yield practical diagnostic tools for evaluating clinical prediction models and contribute a new benchmark for testing OOD generalization in causal and survival ML.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links calibration and generalization, the explicit construction and analysis of shifts that decouple them—despite perfect multi-domain calibration—is novel. This directly challenges an emerging pre-2026 assumption in reliable machine learning for healthcare.

proposals.title: Causal Links Between Invariance and Calibration: Does Out-of-Domain Generalization Improve Multi-Domain Probability Calibration?
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction models, multi-domain calibration—ensuring accurate probability estimates across diverse patient populations—is critical for trustworthy decision support. Current methods often treat calibration as a post-hoc correction or a regularization target. However, the fundamental causal relationship between a model's ability to generalize out-of-domain (OOD) and its calibration properties remains unexplored, limiting principled method development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning representations invariant to domain shifts, a core objective for OOD generalization, inherently leads to improved multi-domain calibration, not merely a correlative relationship. This posits a causal direction from invariance to calibration.
proposals.method: We will frame this as a causal structure learning problem using multi-source observational data with domain indicators. Building on Shalit's work on hidden covariates and heterogeneous effects, we will employ Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) and Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) to enforce invariance. We will then test for the causal link using instrumental variable approaches within a potential outcomes framework, measuring calibration error (e.g., Expected Calibration Error) across held-out domains as the primary outcome. Survival analysis benchmarks will be a key application area, extending recursively imputed trees to assess calibration over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel causal perspective on the link between generalization and reliability, potentially yielding new model training objectives that jointly optimize for both. It will contribute a formal framework and empirical benchmarks for evaluating this relationship in clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical assumption that calibration techniques aid generalization, instead testing if invariant representation learning is a root cause of good calibration. It uniquely combines causal inference for structure discovery with modern OOD generalization methods in a healthcare context.

proposals.title: Finite-Sample Domain-Calibrated Survival Prediction: A Causal Meta-Learning Framework
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction, models must be calibrated—their predicted risks must match observed event rates—across diverse patient domains (e.g., hospitals, demographics). Current calibration methods assume abundant data per domain, but real-world clinical datasets are often small and censored per site. This creates a critical gap: we lack theoretical and practical guarantees for achieving multi-domain calibration with limited domain-specific samples, risking unreliable predictions in new clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging causal structure and shared information across domains via meta-learning, we can derive finite-sample generalization bounds for multi-domain calibration that are substantially tighter than naive per-domain learning, especially for time-to-event outcomes with censoring.
proposals.method: We will extend the concept of calibration to a multi-domain setting with survival outcomes. Building on Shalit's work on recursively imputed trees and hidden covariate models, we will develop a meta-learning algorithm that learns a shared representation to improve sample efficiency. The core technical approach will involve: 1) Formalizing domain-specific calibration as a causal constraint, linking it to hidden covariate shift; 2) Developing a meta-algorithm that adapts a base calibrator (e.g., Platt scaling or isotonic regression) using gradient-based meta-learning (MAML) on censored data; 3) Deriving finite-sample PAC-style guarantees for the calibrated predictor by analyzing the stability of the meta-learning procedure across domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework and practical algorithm for sample-efficient multi-domain calibration in survival analysis. It will yield concrete sample complexity bounds, informing data collection needs for reliable clinical deployment. The resulting method will be benchmarked on heterogeneous treatment effect datasets, directly extending Shalit's recent causal inference work.
proposals.novelty: While calibration and meta-learning are studied separately, their intersection for finite-sample guarantees in causal survival settings is unexplored. The novelty lies in integrating causal hidden variable models with meta-learning to formally bound domain calibration error, moving beyond empirical fixes to a principled sample complexity analysis.

proposals.title: Distributionally Robust Calibration for Survival Predictions Under Domain Shift
proposals.motivation: Clinical risk models, particularly for survival analysis, often fail to maintain calibration when deployed on populations with different covariate distributions than the training set. This miscalibration can lead to unreliable treatment effect estimates and poor clinical decisions. Pre-2026 methods for handling distribution shift, like standard DRO, focus on predictive performance but do not provide formal guarantees for calibration, which is critical for clinical trust and utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) framework, constrained within an f-divergence ball around the training distribution, can produce survival models that are provably calibrated under a worst-case domain shift, thereby improving the reliability of heterogeneous treatment effect estimation in new clinical settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the DRO framework using f-divergences (e.g., χ², KL) to enforce calibration constraints. The method will integrate with a time-to-event model, such as a Cox-based neural network or recursively imputed survival trees, minimizing a robust loss that penalizes miscalibration under worst-case distributions within the divergence ball. We will develop an efficient adversarial training procedure where one player perturbs the data weights within the uncertainty set to break calibration, and the other updates the model parameters to satisfy calibration guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first method to formally guarantee calibration robustness for survival predictions under bounded domain shifts. It will yield a practical tool for clinical researchers to build models whose risk estimates remain trustworthy when applied to new hospitals or patient subgroups, directly enhancing causal inference from observational data.
proposals.novelty: While DRO for prediction is established, its application to enforce calibration guarantees—especially for censored survival outcomes—is novel. This bridges the gap between distributional robustness literature and the critical need for reliable uncertainty quantification in clinical machine learning.

proposals.title: Conformal Domain-Adaptive Calibration for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Survival Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models for individualized treatment effect (ITE) estimation, particularly in survival settings, often exhibit poor calibration across different clinical domains or patient subgroups. This miscalibration undermines reliable risk communication and clinical decision-making. Existing conformal prediction methods provide marginal guarantees but fail to ensure validity within specific, data-scarce domains where treatment effect heterogeneity is most critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating domain-aware weighting into conformal prediction frameworks, we can achieve rigorous, domain-adaptive calibration guarantees for ITE and survival function predictions, even with censored data and hidden confounding structures.
proposals.method: We will extend conformal risk control and weighted conformal prediction methods to the causal survival setting. The approach will involve: 1) Developing a weighted conformal procedure where weights are derived from domain classifiers or propensity score models to account for covariate shift between source and target domains. 2) Integrating this with a recurrent neural network or survival forest architecture that handles time-to-event data with censoring. 3) Validating the method on benchmarks for heterogeneous treatment effects, ensuring coverage guarantees hold conditionally on domain membership or key latent variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first method for obtaining rigorous, domain-specific calibration guarantees for causal survival predictions. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating calibration under domain shift in treatment effect estimation and deliver a practical tool for clinical risk assessment that is reliable across diverse patient populations.
proposals.novelty: While conformal prediction has been applied to standard regression/classification, its integration with causal survival models for domain-adaptive calibration is novel. The key innovation is providing conditional guarantees for complex, right-censored outcomes in the presence of treatment effect heterogeneity and domain shift, a combination not addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Causal Structure Learning for Diagnosing Multi-Domain Calibration Failures in Survival Prediction Models
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models, particularly for survival prediction, often exhibit poor calibration when deployed across new clinical domains or populations, undermining their reliability for treatment decisions. Current domain adaptation and calibration techniques are largely correlational, failing to distinguish stable causal relationships from spurious, domain-specific associations that lead to miscalibration. A principled, causal understanding of which features drive these calibration failures is critically needed for robust clinical prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying causal discovery methods to multi-domain observational data can identify the subset of predictive features whose relationships with the outcome are non-causal and domain-variant, which are the primary drivers of calibration failure upon domain shift.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that integrates causal structure learning (e.g., the PC or FCI algorithms) with multi-domain calibration assessment. For a trained survival model (e.g., a Cox-based neural net or recursively imputed survival forest), we will perform causal discovery on pooled data from multiple source domains to learn a Markov equivalence class of graphs. We will then simulate interventions on domain indicators to identify features with unstable conditional distributions. Finally, we will test if re-weighting or excluding these spurious features, informed by the causal graph, improves calibration on held-out target domains without sacrificing discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel causal diagnostic tool for model calibration, moving beyond performance metrics to explain *why* models fail across domains. It will contribute a method to identify robust, causally-grounded features for clinical prediction, enhancing the transportability of survival and treatment effect models.
proposals.novelty: While causal discovery has been used for feature selection, its direct application to diagnose and rectify calibration failures under domain shift is novel. This bridges the fields of causal inference, domain adaptation, and predictive model assessment in a unique way.

proposals.title: Minimax Domain Calibration for Robust Survival Prediction Under Hidden Covariate Shift
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction models, performance degrades when deployed across hospitals or trial cohorts due to hidden domain shifts in covariates. Pre-2026 domain adaptation methods often assume known domain labels or focus on marginal feature alignment, failing to guarantee calibration—the reliability of predicted risks—across heterogeneous populations. This is critical for survival analysis, where miscalibrated hazard ratios can lead to erroneous treatment effect estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating multi-domain calibration as a minimax game between a survival predictor and a domain-adversarial player will yield models with provably robust calibration error bounds across unseen clinical domains, outperforming standard adversarial domain adaptation on calibration metrics.
proposals.method: We will extend the Recursively Imputed Survival Trees (RIST) framework with a game-theoretic objective. The predictor network minimizes a weighted loss of Cox partial likelihood and a calibration penalty (e.g., via estimated Expected Calibration Error). An adversarial network, receiving hidden representations, attempts to maximize the variance of calibration errors across known source domains. Training follows an iterative minimax optimization, akin to Generative Adversarial Networks, to find an equilibrium where the predictor's calibration is invariant to domain-identifying features. We will benchmark against existing causal survival and domain adaptation methods using biased offline clinical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for domain-invariant calibration, directly addressing hidden covariate shift in clinical risk prediction. It will yield a new benchmark for robust off-policy evaluation of treatment effect heterogeneity across domains, enhancing the transportability of causal survival estimates derived from observational data.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial methods exist for domain-invariant representation learning, applying a minimax game specifically to optimize multi-domain *calibration*—rather than just discrimination—is novel. Integrating this with survival analysis and hidden domain shifts directly addresses a gap in pre-2026 clinical prediction methodology.

proposals.title: Multi-Domain Calibration for High-Dimensional Heterogeneity: A Causal Meta-Learning Framework for Survival and Treatment Effect Prediction
proposals.motivation: Modern clinical datasets often contain hundreds of fine-grained domains (e.g., specific hospital units, rare patient subgroups, treatment protocols). Standard calibration methods fail at this scale, leading to unreliable risk predictions and heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimates crucial for personalized medicine. This undermines the safe deployment of predictive models in healthcare, where miscalibration can directly impact clinical decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning architecture, which learns to share calibration parameters across related micro-domains while regularizing for domain-specific causal structure, can achieve accurate and well-calibrated predictions across hundreds of domains, even with sparse data per domain.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning framework where the base learner is a deep survival or HTE model (e.g., a DeepHit or Dragonnet variant). The meta-learner will be a hypernetwork that generates calibration-aware parameters (e.g., temperature scaling parameters, bias terms) for each micro-domain, conditioned on domain embeddings derived from observed covariates and estimated latent domain confounders. We will incorporate a causal regularization loss, inspired by Shalit's previous work on balancing representations, to ensure domain-specific predictions are not spuriously correlated with domain membership. Scalability will be addressed via amortized variational inference for the domain embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable method for calibrated prediction across massive numbers of fine-grained clinical domains. It will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating multi-domain calibration in survival analysis and HTE estimation, alongside an open-source software package implementing the proposed framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging causal representation learning for domain heterogeneity with meta-learning for calibration, specifically targeting the high-dimensional domain regime which existing multi-task or federated calibration approaches do not efficiently handle.

proposals.title: Does Single-Domain Calibration Confer Robustness? A Causal Perspective on Predictive Stability Under Minimal Shifts
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction models, perfect calibration on a single training domain is often achieved, yet performance can degrade sharply under minor distribution shifts (e.g., new hospital, slight protocol change). Pre-2026 robustness research focuses on large shifts or multi-domain training, but a theory for minimal, clinically plausible shifts is lacking. Understanding if and when calibration implies stability is crucial for deploying reliable models in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for certain classes of minimal distribution shifts—specifically those affecting only the marginal distribution of covariates—strong calibration on a single source domain implies bounded error degradation on the target domain, provided the causal structure of the data-generating process is preserved.
proposals.method: We will formalize 'minimal shift' using a causal graph framework, distinguishing shifts in covariates from shifts in mechanisms. Leveraging Uri Shalit's expertise, we will extend the theoretical analysis of calibration under covariate shift, connecting it to hidden confounding and treatment effect estimation. We will empirically test the theory using benchmarks from survival analysis and treatment effect estimation (e.g., adapting Recursively Imputed Trees), measuring calibration loss and prediction error under simulated, realistic clinical shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical link between calibration and robustness, offering practical conditions under which a calibrated model can be trusted post-deployment. It will yield a new diagnostic framework for assessing model fragility to specific, minimal distribution shifts in clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in re-interpreting calibration not just as a performance metric but as a potential indicator of causal stability, specifically for the under-studied regime of minimal shifts, bridging causal inference and predictive robustness literature.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Correcting Survival Model Miscalibration via Out-of-Domain Failure Mode Analysis
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models for survival prediction and treatment effect estimation are increasingly used for clinical decision support. However, their calibration—the reliability of predicted risk scores—often degrades under distributional shift, a common scenario in healthcare due to varying patient populations or clinical practices. Current calibration diagnostics are largely in-domain and fail to identify the specific nature of miscalibration, limiting robust deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic patterns of performance degradation on carefully constructed out-of-domain (OOD) datasets can be mapped to specific calibration deficiencies (e.g., overconfidence in high-risk groups, time-varying bias), enabling targeted recalibration.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first uses causal structure or domain knowledge to define meaningful OOD splits (e.g., by hospital, latent subgroup). For a survival model (e.g., a Cox-based deep net or survival forest), we will analyze its error patterns across these splits using metrics like time-dependent Brier score decomposition and calibration plots. We will then train a diagnostic meta-model to predict the type and magnitude of miscalibration from OOD performance signatures. Finally, we will implement targeted recalibration methods, such as temperature scaling with group-specific parameters or Bayesian bias correction layers, informed by the diagnostic output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for auditing the robustness of survival and treatment effect models. It will contribute a practical tool for model diagnostics that moves beyond aggregate measures to pinpoint calibration flaws, and a recalibration procedure that improves reliability under distribution shift without requiring full retraining.
proposals.novelty: While OOD detection exists, using structured OOD performance as a diagnostic signal for *specific* calibration errors is novel. This approach uniquely bridges causal inference concepts (for defining shifts) with deep learning calibration techniques, tailored for censored time-to-event outcomes.

proposals.title: Discovering Latent Clinical Subgroups via Inverse Calibration Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction models, systematic miscalibration—where predicted risks are consistently over- or under-estimated for certain patient groups—is a critical failure mode indicating model misspecification or unmodeled heterogeneity. Pre-2026, calibration is typically assessed and corrected at the population level, but patterns of miscalibration across individuals may reveal latent, clinically meaningful subgroups with distinct risk profiles or treatment responses. This problem is acute in survival analysis and treatment effect estimation, where hidden covariates can bias predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patterns of model miscalibration are not random but structurally linked to latent domains (e.g., unmeasured patient subtypes), and that clustering based on these patterns can recover these domains more effectively than clustering on raw input features alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that first fits a flexible survival or treatment effect model (e.g., a Cox model with neural net hazards or a causal forest) on observational clinical data. For each sample, we will compute a vector of calibration residuals across multiple time points or risk strata. We will then apply a probabilistic clustering algorithm (e.g., a Gaussian Mixture Model or a deep latent variable model) to these residual vectors to identify latent domains. The approach will be validated on semi-synthetic datasets where latent domains are known and on real-world clinical cohorts with suspected heterogeneity, such as the ICSI dataset from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-agnostic framework for domain discovery that leverages model failure patterns as a signal. It will contribute a new tool for exploratory analysis in clinical datasets to identify patient subgroups that may require separate models or have heterogeneous treatment effects, ultimately improving model reliability and personalization.
proposals.novelty: While calibration is used diagnostically, inverting it to discover latent structure is novel. This differs from standard clustering or latent variable models by using the *discrepancy* between model and data as the primary signal, which may be more robust to confounding in the raw features.

proposals.title: Calibrated High-Dimensional Predictions: A Causal Framework for Uncertainty in Image Segmentation and Structured Outputs
proposals.motivation: In clinical prediction tasks like medical image segmentation, deep learning models provide high-dimensional outputs but lack reliable, calibrated uncertainty estimates. Pre-2026, calibration theory is well-developed for scalar outcomes but fails for structured outputs, limiting trust in automated diagnostics and downstream causal analyses of treatment effects on imaging biomarkers. This gap hinders the reliable deployment of AI in personalized medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibration principles for scalar predictions can be generalized to high-dimensional, structured outputs by leveraging latent variable models and causal graphical frameworks, leading to uncertainty estimates that are both statistically valid and clinically interpretable.
proposals.method: We will extend multi-domain calibration theory by treating each pixel/voxel in a segmentation map as a node in a graphical model, linking calibration to the estimation of latent confounders. Building on recent work on hidden covariates and survival analysis, we will develop a novel neural architecture with a calibrated output head. This head will use a conditional generative model (e.g., a conditional Variational Autoencoder) to model the distribution of high-dimensional outputs given low-dimensional, calibrated summary statistics derived from the latent space. We will adapt recursively imputed trees and causal inference techniques to ensure calibration holds across heterogeneous patient subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and practical toolkit for calibration in high-dimensional prediction spaces, directly applicable to medical image analysis. It will enable more rigorous evaluation of heterogeneous treatment effects where the outcome is an imaging phenotype, strengthening causal claims from observational data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing causal inference with high-dimensional calibration via structured latent variable models, moving beyond scalar probability calibration to spatially-structured uncertainty—a largely unexplored frontier pre-2026.

ideas.title: Invert the need for a generative model
ideas.description: Estimate causal concept effects without training a separate generative model for interventions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explaining Classifiers with Causal Concept Effect (CaCE)

ideas.title: Non-linear Confounding Bias with Identifiability-Enforcing Constraints
ideas.description: This approach generalizes the confounding bias model beyond linearity while directly combating non-identifiability. It posits a flexible but constrained non-linear function for the bias, where the constraints (e.g., smoothness, monotonicity relative to propensity) are derived from domain reasoning or theoretical assumptions about the confounding mechanism. Estimation proceeds via a penalized objective that trades off fit against complexity, and model selection uses a dedicated procedure to test the sensitivity of the causal estimate to the allowed flexibility. This yields a more robust and interpretable estimator than an unconstrained non-linear model, as it prevents overfitting and provides a clear domain-justified range for the causal effect.

ideas.title: Diagnose and Engineer Overlap: A Framework for Causal Inference with Deficient Common Support
ideas.description: This research program accepts that reliable causal estimation requires some common support and focuses on systematically addressing its absence. It develops tools to rigorously diagnose the nature and severity of overlap problems, quantifying the 'extrapolation load' for any given causal query and target population. The core innovation is a principled methodology for designing or synthesizing data—through experimental augmentation, sampling strategies, or the integration of auxiliary studies—to strategically create the necessary overlap. The output is a transparent pipeline that either enables robust estimation or definitively signals when the available data is insufficient for the desired inference without heroic assumptions.

ideas.title: Testable Latent State Assumptions: Formal Validation in Partially Observed Dual Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a formal methodology to statistically test the foundational assumptions of latent variable models—such as conditional independence, parametric form, or Markov structure—in settings where the latent states are only partially and occasionally observed. By leveraging the sporadic ground truth observations, the approach constructs targeted hypothesis tests that use the combined experimental and observational data to validate or falsify the model's core premises, rather than just evaluating its predictive performance. This transforms the partial observations from a mere convenience for model training into a critical tool for assumption auditing, enabling stronger causal claims. The result is a dual learning system that is not only adapted to partial observability but is also rigorously self-validating regarding its own latent state construction.

ideas.title: Adapt Geophysical Blind Source Separation for Causal Latent Variable Models
ideas.description: The original idea proposes combining a grounding approach with deep generative models to learn richer representations of confounding. By drawing an analogy from geophysics, specifically the use of blind source separation techniques like ICA to disentangle independent seismic sources from mixed signals, we can adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to incorporate constraints inspired by blind source separation into the training of deep latent variable models for causal inference. This would involve designing model architectures and loss functions that encourage the latent variables to separate the true confounding factors from other independent sources of noise or variation in the observed data. The outcome would be a more robust and identifiable generative model of the data-generating process, where the learned confounder representations are cleaner and more suitable for subsequent causal adjustment, ultimately leading to more reliable effect estimates.

ideas.title: Evidence-Conditional Latent Dynamics with Explicit Observation Models
ideas.description: This framework explicitly models the process by which latent states are occasionally observed, treating it as a separate, potentially noisy or biased sensor channel. The learning objective combines the standard evidence lower bound (ELBO) on primary data with a conditional term that maximizes the likelihood of the partial latent observations given the inferred posterior. This forces the model to reconcile its learned dynamics with the sporadic evidence, robustly integrating it without over-reliance. The approach yields a more coherent and testable latent state model that can quantify its own uncertainty about the partially observed variables.
